topics.title: Determination of Flash Point of Hydrocarbon Liquids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL507 - FIRE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Flash Point of Hydrocarbon Liquids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flash Point (KTU Standard Definition)**
> The **flash point** of a hydrocarbon liquid is defined as the **lowest temperature** at which the liquid produces a sufficient amount of **flammable vapour** in air, such that this vapour–air mixture can be **ignited momentarily (flash)** when an external ignition source is brought near the surface of the liquid, under specified test conditions.

The flash point is **NOT** the temperature at which the liquid itself ignites. Rather, it is the temperature at which the vapour phase above the liquid becomes **just ignitable** by a pilot flame. The corresponding temperature at which the vapour continues to burn for **at least 5 seconds** after ignition is known as the **fire point**, which is typically **5–30 °C higher** than the flash point for most hydrocarbons.

**Related Thermodynamic Metrics (in Bold):**

- **Flash Point ($T_{flash}$)** – Temperature of momentary ignition, in **°C or K**.
- **Fire Point ($T_{fire}$)** – Temperature of sustained combustion, in **°C or K**.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** – Temperature at which spontaneous combustion occurs **without** an external flame, typically **300–600 °C** for hydrocarbons.
- **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** – The **minimum volumetric concentration** of vapour in air required for combustion, expressed in **% by volume** (for most hydrocarbons: **0.6 % to 1.4 %**).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The "Boiling Kettle Effect"**
> Imagine a sealed kettle of water sitting on a **cold stove**. At room temperature, water molecules evaporate too slowly to feel anything. As the stove heats up, steam begins to escape. The "flash point" of water vapour in air is when **just enough steam** is mixing with air that a candle flame brought close will cause a brief *whoomph* — but the water does not keep burning. The kettle has not reached the "fire point" yet (which for water is impossible, but think of petrol). For petrol in a garage, the flash point is the **temperature below which** you can spill it and feel safe; above it, **one spark is enough** to start a disaster.

In fire engineering practice, the flash point is the **single most important physical property** that classifies a liquid as:
- **Class A / Class B Flammable Liquid** (Flash Point $<$ 37.8 °C — highly hazardous),
- **Class C Combustible Liquid** (Flash Point $\geq$ 37.8 °C — moderately hazardous),
as per **NFPA 30** and **IS 1446** standards adopted in Indian/KTU fire engineering practice.

## 1.3 The Fire Triangle Connection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Flash point is governed by the simultaneous satisfaction of **all three** vertices of the **Fire Triangle**:
> 1. **Fuel** (hydrocarbon vapour at concentration $\geq$ LEL),
> 2. **Oxidiser** (atmospheric oxygen, ~21 % by volume),
> 3. **Ignition Source** (test flame / spark) of sufficient energy.
>
> A substance is *not* flammable below its flash point because the **fuel leg of the fire triangle is incomplete** — insufficient vapour is generated to support combustion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Origin of Flash Point

The flash point is fundamentally a **vapour-pressure phenomenon**. As the temperature of a hydrocarbon liquid rises, its **saturated vapour pressure ($P_{vap}$)** increases exponentially (described by the **Clausius–Clapeyron relation**). At the flash point, the vapour pressure becomes just high enough that the vapour concentration in the air–vapour mixture above the liquid equals the **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** of that substance.

### Logic Flow — Why the Flash Point Exists

- **Step 1 — Molecular Evaporation:** Hydrocarbon molecules at the liquid surface possess kinetic energy following a **Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution**. Only the high-energy tail escapes into the vapour phase.
- **Step 2 — Vapour Pressure Build-up:** As temperature rises, the rate of evaporation increases, raising $P_{vap}$. At equilibrium: **Rate of evaporation = Rate of condensation**.
- **Step 3 — Concentration Threshold:** The volumetric concentration $C$ of vapour above the liquid is governed by **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**.
- **Step 4 — Flash Condition:** Combustion becomes possible only when $C \geq LEL$. The temperature at which this condition is first satisfied is defined as the **flash point**.

## 2.2 The Governing Equations

### 2.2.1 Dalton's Law (Concentration of Vapour)

For an ideal vapour–air mixture in equilibrium with the liquid:

$$C_{vol\%} = \frac{P_{vap}}{P_{atm}} \times 100$$

At the **flash point**, the vapour concentration must equal the LEL:

$$P_{vap,\, flash} = \frac{LEL}{100} \times P_{atm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Insight:** For $n$-hexane, $LEL \approx 1.1\,\%$ by volume. At standard atmospheric pressure ($P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa), the flash point occurs when $P_{vap} \approx 1.115$ kPa. Plugging this into the Antoine equation yields a flash point of approximately **$-22$ °C** — hence hexane is **highly flammable** at room temperature.

### 2.2.2 Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

The vapour pressure as a function of temperature is given by:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{vap,\,2}}{P_{vap,\,1}}\right) = -\,\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

where $\Delta H_{vap}$ is the **enthalpy of vaporisation** (J/mol), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) is the **universal gas constant**, and $T$ is in **Kelvin**.

### 2.2.3 Antoine Equation (Empirical, Engineering-Preferred)

For real hydrocarbons, the **Antoine equation** provides superior accuracy:

$$\log_{10}(P_{vap}) = A - \frac{B}{C + T}$$

where $A$, $B$, $C$ are **substance-specific Antoine constants**, $T$ is in **°C**, and $P_{vap}$ is in **mmHg** (or kPa, depending on the dataset).

## 2.3 Apparatus Classification — Closed Cup vs. Open Cup

| Parameter | **Closed Cup (Pensky-Martens / Abel)** | **Open Cup (Cleveland)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Vapour-Air Mixing** | Confined — saturated vapour pocket above liquid | Open to atmosphere — vapour disperses freely |
| **Measured Flash Point** | **Lower** (more conservative) | **Higher** (typically 5–15 °C above closed cup) |
| **Application** | Petroleum storage, IS 1446, IS 1448 P-20 | Lubricating oils, fire hazard classification |
| **Test Standard** | IS 1448 P-21, ASTM D93 | IS 1448 P-4, ASTM D92 |
| **Energy of Ignition** | **Low** — small test flame introduced via shutter | **Higher** — flame swept across the cup surface |
| **Use in Fire Engineering** | Storage tank safety, regulatory classification | Industrial oil handling, high-temperature fluids |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse the **closed cup** and **open cup** flash point values in numerical problems. Always **declare the method** before solving — the open cup value is **always** higher for the same substance.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{P_{vap}}{P_{atm}} \times 100$ | $C$ in % vol, $P$ in kPa | Vapour concentration in air |
| 2 | $P_{vap,\, flash} = \dfrac{LEL}{100} \times P_{atm}$ | $P$ in kPa, LEL in % | Flash point condition |
| 3 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | $\Delta H_{vap}$ in J/mol, $T$ in K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | Vapour pressure vs temperature |
| 4 | $\log_{10}(P) = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T}$ | $T$ in °C, $P$ in mmHg | Antoine empirical fit |
| 5 | $T_{fire} = T_{flash} + \Delta T$ | $\Delta T \approx 5$ to 30 °C | Fire point from flash point |
| 6 | $P_{vap} = x_i \cdot P_i^{\,sat}$ | $x_i$ = mole fraction | Raoult's Law for mixtures |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The flash point dictates:

- **Storage & Transport Classification** (UN Hazard Classes 3.1, 3.2, 3.3).
- **Insurance Premium Calculations** (NFPA 30-based).
- **Process Safety Vent Sizing** in refineries (API 2000).
- **Selection of Electrical Equipment** in hazardous areas (IS/IEC 60079 — Zone 0, 1, 2).
- **Bonding & Earthing Requirements** during tanker loading/unloading.
- **Design of Fire Suppression Systems** — foams, sprinklers, gas flooding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Clausius–Clapeyron to Flash Point

### Goal
Derive an expression that gives the **flash point temperature** $T_{flash}$ of a pure hydrocarbon liquid, given its enthalpy of vaporisation, its Lower Explosive Limit, and known vapour pressure data at a reference temperature.

### Given
- Reference vapour pressure $P_1$ at temperature $T_1$ (e.g., boiling point at 101.325 kPa).
- Enthalpy of vaporisation $\Delta H_{vap}$ (assumed constant over the temperature range — a valid assumption for narrow $T$ intervals).
- Lower Explosive Limit $LEL$ (volumetric %).
- Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa (assume standard).

### Step 1 — State the Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

For two vapour-pressure states $(P_1, T_1)$ and $(P_2, T_2)$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Explanation:** The natural log of the pressure ratio equals the negative enthalpy of vaporisation divided by the gas constant, multiplied by the difference in inverse absolute temperatures. This equation arises from integrating the exact Clapeyron equation under the assumption that the vapour phase behaves as an **ideal gas** and $\Delta H_{vap}$ is **temperature-independent**.

### Step 2 — Define the Flash Point Condition

At the flash point, by Dalton's Law:

$$P_2 = P_{vap,\, flash} = \frac{LEL}{100} \times P_{atm}$$

**Explanation:** Combustion of the vapour–air mixture becomes possible only when the partial pressure of the vapour reaches the threshold corresponding to its LEL. Below this pressure, the vapour is too lean to propagate a flame front.

### Step 3 — Substitute $P_2$ into the Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{(LEL/100)\cdot P_{atm}}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{flash}} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Explanation:** We now replace the unknown $P_2$ with the flash-point vapour pressure expression, and the unknown $T_2$ with $T_{flash}$.

### Step 4 — Isolate the $\dfrac{1}{T_{flash}}$ Term

Move the negative reciprocal to the other side:

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_{flash}} = \frac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{(LEL/100)\cdot P_{atm}}{P_1}\right)$$

**Explanation:** Multiplying both sides of the equation by $-1$ reverses the sign inside the bracket, allowing us to isolate $1/T_{flash}$ on the right-hand side.

### Step 5 — Solve for $T_{flash}$

$$\frac{1}{T_{flash}} = \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{(LEL/100)\cdot P_{atm}}{P_1}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{flash} = \left[\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{(LEL/100)\cdot P_{atm}}{P_1}\right)\right]^{-1}\,}$$

**Explanation:** The final expression gives the absolute flash point in **Kelvin**. Convert to Celsius using $T(^\circ C) = T(K) - 273.15$.

### Step 6 — Consistency Check (Dimensional Analysis)

- $R / \Delta H_{vap}$ has units of $\dfrac{\text{J/(mol·K)}}{\text{J/mol}} = \text{K}^{-1}$. ✓
- The argument of $\ln(\cdot)$ is a **dimensionless pressure ratio**. ✓
- $1/T_1$ is in $\text{K}^{-1}$. ✓
- The entire RHS has units of $\text{K}^{-1}$, so the inversion yields Kelvin. ✓

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

### Problem
For $n$-heptane, given:
- $T_1 = 371.6$ K (boiling point at 101.325 kPa), $P_1 = 101.325$ kPa.
- $\Delta H_{vap} = 31{,}770$ J/mol.
- $LEL = 1.05\,\%$ by volume.
- $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.

Compute the **predicted flash point**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step A — Compute flash point vapour pressure:**

$$P_2 = \frac{1.05}{100} \times 101.325 = 1.0639 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step B — Compute the pressure ratio:**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{1.0639}{101.325} = 0.01050$$

**Step C — Compute the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln(0.01050) = -4.556$$

**Step D — Compute $R/\Delta H_{vap}$:**

$$\frac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} = \frac{8.314}{31{,}770} = 2.617 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step E — Compute the second term of the equation:**

$$\frac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = (2.617 \times 10^{-4}) \times (-4.556) = -1.192 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step F — Compute $1/T_{flash}$:**

$$\frac{1}{T_{flash}} = \frac{1}{371.6} - (-1.192 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.691 \times 10^{-3} + 1.192 \times 10^{-3} = 3.883 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step G — Invert to obtain $T_{flash}$:**

$$T_{flash} = \frac{1}{3.883 \times 10^{-3}} = 257.5 \text{ K}$$

**Step H — Convert to Celsius:**

$$T_{flash}(^\circ C) = 257.5 - 273.15 = -15.6 \,^\circ C$$

**Comparison with literature:** Experimental closed-cup flash point of $n$-heptane is **$-4$ °C**. The deviation (~12 °C) arises from the constant-$\Delta H_{vap}$ assumption and the simplicity of the model — acceptable for **first-order engineering estimates**.

## 3.3 Analytical Procedure Matrix (Lab View)

| Stage | Action | Observation / Reading | Recorded Quantity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clean and dry the cup, fill with sample to the marked level | Meniscus aligns with mark | Sample volume ($\approx$ 50 mL) |
| 2 | Assemble cup, lid, stirrer, and thermometers | Apparatus sealed | Atmospheric pressure noted from barometer |
| 3 | Heat at **5 °C/min** with continuous stirring | Temperature rises steadily | Bath temperature vs. sample temperature |
| 4 | At every **2 °C** increment (below expected flash), introduce test flame for **0.5 s** | "Tick" sound, no flash | Continue heating |
| 5 | At the first appearance of a **distinct blue halo** that propagates across the cup | **Flash observed** | Record $T_{flash}$ to nearest 0.5 °C |
| 6 | Continue heating at **2 °C/min** to find fire point | Vapour burns for **$\geq$ 5 s** | Record $T_{fire}$ |
| 7 | Cool the apparatus, repeat twice | Average of three trials | Mean $T_{flash} \pm$ std. dev. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction for Atmospheric Pressure (KTU Exam Hot Topic):**
> The observed flash point must be corrected to **standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa)** using the formula:
>
> $$T_{flash,\, corrected} = T_{flash,\, observed} + 0.25 \times (101.325 - P_{atm,\, observed})$$
>
> This correction is awarded **2 marks** in KTU exams if the student remembers to apply it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Pensky-Martens Closed-Cup Flash Point Tester

The diagram below represents the **block-level functional architecture** of a closed-cup flash point apparatus, showing the thermal, mechanical, and ignition subsystems and their interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Heating Bath Reservoir] --> B[Heat Transfer Fluid]
    B --> C[Test Cup with Hydrocarbon Sample]
    C --> D[Stirring Motor with Coupling Shaft]
    E[Thermometer T1 Sample] --> C
    F[Thermometer T2 Bath] --> B
    G[Ignition Flame Source Pilot] --> H[Shutter Mechanism]
    H --> C
    I[Barometric Pressure Sensor] --> J[Data Logger]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    J --> K[Operator Display and Recorder]
    D --> C
    L[Cooling Fan Post-Test] --> A
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **heating bath** (A) transfers heat via a fluid medium (B) to the test cup (C).
- A **stirring motor** (D) ensures uniform sample temperature.
- **Two thermometers** — one in the sample (E) and one in the bath (F) — feed the data logger (J).
- The **pilot flame** (G) is dipped into the cup via a **shutter mechanism** (H) at regular temperature intervals.
- A **barometric sensor** (I) provides the atmospheric pressure for the correction formula.
- The **cooling fan** (L) is engaged after the test for safe disassembly.

## 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flow (KTU Lab Record Format)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Clean and Dry Apparatus] --> P2[Fill Test Cup to Mark with Sample]
    P2 --> P3[Assemble Lid Stirrer and Thermometers]
    P3 --> P4[Light Pilot Flame and Adjust]
    P4 --> P5[Begin Heating at 5 C per Minute with Stirring]
    P5 --> P6{Is Temperature within 20 C of Expected Flash?}
    P6 -- No --> P5
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Reduce Heating Rate to 2 C per Minute]
    P7 --> P8[Introduce Flame for 0.5 s every 2 C]
    P8 --> P9{Is Flash Observed?}
    P9 -- No --> P8
    P9 -- Yes --> P10[Record Flash Point Temperature T1]
    P10 --> P11[Continue Heating for Fire Point]
    P11 --> P12[Burning Sustained for 5 s]
    P12 --> P13[Record Fire Point Temperature T2]
    P13 --> P14[Shut Off Heat and Cool Apparatus]
    P14 --> P15[Apply Atmospheric Pressure Correction]
    P15 --> P16[Report Final Flash Point with Units]
```

## 4.3 Fire Triangle — Condition Satisfied at Flash Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FT [Fire Triangle at Flash Point]
        direction TB
        F1[FUEL: Hydrocarbon Vapour] --> CON[Concentration equals LEL]
        O1[OXYGEN: Atmospheric Air 21 percent] --> CON
        I1[IGNITION: Pilot Flame] --> CON
    end
    CON --> OUT[Combustion Flash Event Momentary]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** All three vertices — fuel vapour at the LEL threshold, atmospheric oxygen, and a pilot ignition source — are simultaneously satisfied at the flash point, producing a **momentary flash** that does not sustain itself. Sustained combustion (the **fire point**) requires additional energy input or higher temperature.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "flash point" of a hydrocarbon liquid. How is it different from the "fire point"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Flash Point (2 marks):** The flash point is the lowest temperature at which a hydrocarbon liquid produces enough flammable vapour to form an ignitable mixture with air, such that application of a small pilot flame causes a **momentary flash** across the liquid surface.
- **Difference from Fire Point (1 mark):** The fire point is the temperature at which the vapour continues to burn for **at least 5 seconds** after ignition. It is **always higher** than the flash point (typically by **5 to 30 °C**).

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why does the open-cup flash point of a hydrocarbon oil differ from the closed-cup value? Which one is used for storage classification under NFPA 30?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Reason for difference (2 marks):** In an **open cup**, vapour escapes freely into the atmosphere, requiring a higher temperature to build up the LEL concentration. In a **closed cup**, vapour accumulates in a confined space, so the LEL is reached at a **lower temperature**. Hence, **open-cup values are always higher** than closed-cup values for the same liquid.
- **NFPA 30 preference (1 mark):** NFPA 30 uses the **closed-cup flash point** for the classification of flammable and combustible liquids because it represents the **worst-case (most hazardous)** scenario in enclosed storage tanks.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B.A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Pensky-Martens closed-cup apparatus** used for the determination of the flash point of a hydrocarbon liquid. List the **IS standard** followed. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A sample of lubricating oil is tested in a closed-cup flash point apparatus. The observed flash point is **180 °C** at a barometric pressure of **99.0 kPa**. Compute the **corrected flash point** at standard atmospheric pressure. The Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) of the oil vapour is **0.9 % by volume**. State the **fire hazard class** as per NFPA 30. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — **[7 Marks]**

1. **Apparatus Description (3 marks):**
   The Pensky-Martens apparatus consists of:
   - A **brass test cup** of standard volume (~$70$ mL) with a filling mark.
   - A **lid assembly** with a sliding **shutter** to introduce the pilot flame.
   - A **stirring mechanism** driven by a flexible shaft from a top-mounted motor.
   - A **heating bath** containing heat-transfer fluid (typically silicone oil or water).
   - **Two thermometers** — one immersed in the sample, one in the bath.
   - A **pilot flame** (gas-fed) supplied via a flexible tube.

2. **IS Standard (1 mark):** **IS 1448 (Part 21) : 2019** / equivalent to **ASTM D93**.

3. **Procedure (3 marks):**
   - Fill the cup to the mark with the sample.
   - Assemble the lid, stirrer, and thermometers; record atmospheric pressure.
   - Heat at **5 °C/min** with continuous stirring.
   - At every **2 °C** interval (within 20 °C of expected flash), dip the test flame for **0.5 s** by operating the shutter.
   - The **first appearance of a blue flame halo** that propagates across the cup is the flash point. Record to the nearest **0.5 °C**.
   - Repeat the test **twice more**; report the **mean** value.
   - Apply the **atmospheric pressure correction** to obtain the standard flash point.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify the correction formula (1 mark):**

$$T_{flash,\, corrected} = T_{flash,\, observed} + 0.25 \times (101.325 - P_{atm,\, observed})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (2 marks):**

$$T_{flash,\, corrected} = 180 + 0.25 \times (101.325 - 99.0)$$

$$T_{flash,\, corrected} = 180 + 0.25 \times 2.325$$

$$T_{flash,\, corrected} = 180 + 0.581 = 180.58 \approx 180.6 \,^\circ C$$

**Step 3 — Verification using LEL data (3 marks):**
- Vapour pressure at flash point:
$$P_{vap,\, flash} = \frac{LEL}{100} \times P_{atm} = \frac{0.9}{100} \times 101.325 = 0.912 \text{ kPa}$$
- For a lubricating oil with $\Delta H_{vap} \approx 40{,}000$ J/mol and boiling point $\approx 600$ K, applying the Clausius–Clapeyron equation yields a flash point consistent with the corrected value of **180.6 °C**.

**Step 4 — NFPA 30 Classification (1 mark):**
Since the corrected flash point is **180.6 °C**, which is **greater than 93.3 °C (200 °F)**, the oil is classified as a **Class IIIB Combustible Liquid** (NFPA 30).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the atmospheric pressure correction** — costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using the open-cup formula instead of closed-cup** — costs **1 mark** for method identification.
> 3. **Not converting the final answer to °C** (or leaving it unrounded) — costs **0.5 mark** for unit/sig-fig discipline.
> 4. **Misclassifying the NFPA class** — students often write "Class I" for any flammable liquid; remember, **Class IIIB** is for liquids with flash point **> 93.3 °C**.

---

### **Question B.B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Derive the relation between the **flash point** of a pure hydrocarbon liquid and its **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** using the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation**. Clearly state all assumptions. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The vapour pressure of **acetone** is **13.33 kPa** at **0 °C (273.15 K)** and **53.33 kPa** at **40 °C (313.15 K)**. Given $LEL_{acetone} = 2.5\,\%$ by volume and $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, calculate the **flash point of acetone** in °C. Take $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — **[7 Marks]**

**1. State the assumptions (2 marks):**
- Vapour phase behaves as an **ideal gas**.
- Enthalpy of vaporisation $\Delta H_{vap}$ is **constant** over the temperature range.
- The liquid and vapour are in **thermodynamic equilibrium** at the liquid surface.
- The **flash point** corresponds to the condition where the vapour concentration just reaches the LEL.

**2. Begin with the Clausius–Clapeyron equation (2 marks):**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**3. Apply Dalton's Law at flash point (2 marks):**

$$P_2 = \frac{LEL}{100} \cdot P_{atm}$$

**4. Combine and solve for $T_{flash}$ (1 mark):**

$$T_{flash} = \left[\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} \ln\!\left(\frac{(LEL/100) \cdot P_{atm}}{P_1}\right)\right]^{-1}$$

This gives the flash point in **Kelvin**. See Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete derivation.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta H_{vap}$ from the given vapour pressure data (3 marks):**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{53.33}{13.33}\right) = \ln(4.0) = 1.3863$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{313.15} - \frac{1}{273.15} = 3.1934 \times 10^{-3} - 3.6610 \times 10^{-3} = -4.676 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta H_{vap} = - \frac{R \cdot \ln(P_2/P_1)}{(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)} = - \frac{8.314 \times 1.3863}{-4.676 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\Delta H_{vap} = 24{,}648 \text{ J/mol} \approx 24.65 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P_{vap,\, flash}$ (1 mark):**

$$P_2 = \frac{2.5}{100} \times 101.325 = 2.533 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the pressure ratio and its logarithm (1 mark):**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{2.533}{13.33} = 0.1901 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \ln(0.1901) = -1.660$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_{flash}$ (1 mark):**

$$\frac{1}{T_{flash}} = \frac{1}{273.15} - \frac{8.314}{24{,}648} \times (-1.660)$$

$$\frac{1}{T_{flash}} = 3.6610 \times 10^{-3} + 5.600 \times 10^{-4} = 4.221 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$T_{flash} = 236.9 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Celsius (1 mark):**

$$T_{flash} = 236.9 - 273.15 = -36.2 \,^\circ C$$

**Comparison with literature:** The experimental closed-cup flash point of acetone is **$-20$ °C**. The calculated value of **$-36.2$ °C** underestimates by about 16 °C, which is acceptable for a **first-order Clausius–Clapeyron estimate** with constant-$\Delta H_{vap}$ assumption.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Sign error** in the $\Delta H_{vap}$ formula — students forget the negative sign and obtain a negative $\Delta H_{vap}$. **Cost: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Mixing up $T_1$ and $T_2$** — always place the unknown temperature ($T_{flash}$) as $T_2$ in the standard form, OR use the derived form directly. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Failing to convert the final answer to °C** — leaving it in Kelvin. **Cost: 0.5 mark.**
> 4. **Using the wrong LEL value** — acetone is **2.5 %**, not 1.1 % (which is $n$-hexane). **Cost: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Flash Point Determination**

- **Definition:** Flash point = lowest temperature at which vapour–air mixture **momentarily ignites** with a pilot flame. **NOT** the temperature of sustained burning.
- **Fire Point** is **always higher** than flash point by **5–30 °C**; the vapour burns for **≥ 5 s**.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** is much higher (typically **300–600 °C** for hydrocarbons) and **does not require an external flame**.
- **At flash point, $P_{vap} = (LEL / 100) \times P_{atm}$** — this is the **master condition**.
- **Closed-cup flash point $<$ Open-cup flash point** for the same liquid (always).
- **NFPA 30 Classification Thresholds:**
  - Class IA: $T_{flash} < 22.8$ °C, **BP** $< 37.8$ °C
  - Class IB: $T_{flash} < 22.8$ °C, **BP** $\geq 37.8$ °C
  - Class IC: $22.8 \leq T_{flash} < 37.8$ °C
  - Class II: $37.8 \leq T_{flash} < 60$ °C
  - Class IIIA: $60 \leq T_{flash} \leq 93.3$ °C
  - Class IIIB: $T_{flash} > 93.3$ °C
- **IS Standards:** Closed cup — **IS 1448 P-21** / ASTM D93; Open cup — **IS 1448 P-4** / ASTM D92; Abel — **IS 1448 P-20**.
- **Atmospheric Pressure Correction Formula:** $\Delta T = 0.25 \times (101.325 - P_{observed})$ (in kPa); add to observed flash point.
- **Heating Rate:** **5 °C/min** for bulk heating, reduced to **2 °C/min** near the expected flash point.
- **Test Flame Application:** **0.5 seconds** every **2 °C** increment.
- **Reporting:** Mean of **three trials**, corrected to standard pressure, rounded to nearest **0.5 °C**.
- **Fire Triangle:** All three legs (Fuel at LEL, O$_2 \geq 21$ %, Ignition source) must be satisfied simultaneously.
- **Common Hydrocarbon Flash Points (must memorise for KTU viva):**
  - $n$-Hexane: **$-22$ °C** (CC)
  - $n$-Heptane: **$-4$ °C** (CC)
  - Acetone: **$-20$ °C** (CC)
  - Petrol/Gasoline: **$-43$ °C** (CC)
  - Diesel: **52–96 °C** (CC)
  - Kerosene: **38–72 °C** (CC)
  - Lubricating oil: **150–230 °C** (CC)
- **Clausius–Clapeyron Constant-$\Delta H_{vap}$ Assumption** gives first-order accuracy within **±15 °C** of experimental values — sufficient for KTU numerical problems.
- **Engineering Safety Insight:** In any storage facility, the operating temperature should remain **at least 5–10 °C below** the closed-cup flash point to maintain a **safety margin** against accidental ignition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Fire Point and Pour Point of Hydrocarbon Liquids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL507 - FIRE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Fire Point and Pour Point of Hydrocarbon Liquids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fire Point – Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Point (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> The **Fire Point** of a hydrocarbon liquid is the *lowest temperature* at which the vapors of the liquid, when heated under prescribed test conditions (ASTM D92 – Cleveland Open Cup Method), ignite and continue to burn as a stable flame on the liquid surface for a minimum duration of **5 seconds** after the removal of the test flame (pilot flame).

**Symbol / Notation:** $T_{fp}$ (Temperature of Fire Point) — measured in **°C** or **K**.

**Key Distinction from Flash Point:** Unlike the *flash point* (where a momentary "flash" or "pop" occurs above the liquid surface), the **fire point** is reached when the rate of vaporization is *high enough* to sustain continuous combustion. The fire point is always **higher** than the flash point, typically by:
- **5 °C to 30 °C** for most hydrocarbon liquids
- **1 °C to 5 °C** for highly volatile liquids

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Fire Point matters in Fire Engineering:** The fire point is the *true minimum temperature* at which a liquid becomes a self-sustaining fire hazard. For fire protection engineers, it is the **design parameter** for bund sizing, foam suppression, sprinkler activation, and storage tank classification under **IS 1446** and **OISD 118** standards.

### Intuitive Analogy – The "Candle Wax" Concept

Imagine a half-melted candle. At room temperature, it does not burn because the wax is solid and releases no vapors. As you heat it, the wax melts, then *smokes* — at this stage, if you bring a matchstick, the vapors will produce a brief "flash" (this is the **flash point**). Now heat it further. The wax begins to evaporate *much faster* — at this point, even after you remove the matchstick, the candle continues to burn on its own (this is the **fire point**). The fire point is essentially the **"ignition stability temperature"** of the liquid fuel.

---

## 1.2 Pour Point – Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pour Point (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> The **Pour Point** of a hydrocarbon liquid is the *lowest temperature* (expressed as a multiple of **3 °C** in standard testing) at which the liquid is observed to *flow* when the test jar is tilted horizontally, under conditions specified by **ASTM D97**.

**Symbol / Notation:** $T_{pp}$ (Temperature of Pour Point) — measured in **°C**.

**Significance in Fire Engineering:**
- **Low-temperature operability** of firefighting foam concentrates
- **Storage and handling** of diesel, kerosene, and lube oils in cold regions
- **Pipeline transportation** of crude oils (where wax deposition can choke flow)
- **Selection of fire pump fuels** for installations in high-altitude / cold-climate zones

> [!NOTE]
> **Pour Point vs. Freezing Point:** The pour point is *NOT* a thermodynamic phase-change temperature. It is a *rheological* (flow-related) property. A liquid can lose its ability to flow well above its thermodynamic freezing point because of **wax crystallization** or **viscosity increase**. The pour point is typically **2 °C to 5 °C higher** than the freezing point.

### Intuitive Analogy – The "Honey in the Fridge" Concept

Pour honey at room temperature — it flows easily. Now put the honey in a refrigerator. It thickens dramatically, then stops flowing altogether if you tilt the bottle, even though it is still *liquid* (not frozen solid). The temperature at which the honey just *stops flowing* when you tilt the bottle is analogous to the **pour point** of a hydrocarbon. For crude oils and heavy fuels, the culprit is **paraffin wax** that crystallizes into a gel-like network — locking the oil in place even though it has not turned into a solid.

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Methods at a Glance

| Property | Standard Method | Apparatus | Sample Volume | Heating / Cooling Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fire Point** | ASTM D92 / IS 1446 | Cleveland Open Cup (COC) | ~70 mL | 5 – 6 °C/min, then 2 °C/min near fire point |
| **Pour Point** | ASTM D97 / IS 1448 (P:10) | Pour Point Jar + Cooling Bath | ~50 mL | Cooled progressively, checked every 3 °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Both experiments are conducted as **paired observations** in the Fire Engineering Lab. A single sample (e.g., diesel, kerosene, lube oil) is typically used to determine *both* the fire point and the pour point in consecutive sub-experiments.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background – Fire Point

The combustion of a liquid fuel occurs in three sequential thermodynamic and kinetic stages:

1. **Vaporization** — liquid → vapor (rate governed by Antoine / Clausius-Clapeyron equation)
2. **Mixing with air** — vapor + oxygen → flammable mixture (governed by LFL / UFL — Lower / Upper Flammability Limits)
3. **Ignition & Sustained Combustion** — heat release rate exceeds heat loss rate

At the **flash point**, the vaporization rate produces a *flammable mixture* only in a thin layer just above the liquid. The pilot flame momentarily ignites this layer, but the heat release is *insufficient* to vaporize more liquid — the flame self-extinguishes.

At the **fire point**, the vaporization rate is *higher* (faster molecular escape from the liquid surface). The heat released by the burning vapor is now *sufficient* to maintain the liquid surface temperature above the flash point, creating a self-sustaining feedback loop:

$$
\underbrace{\dot{Q}_{release}}_{\text{from burning vapor}} \;>\; \underbrace{\dot{Q}_{loss}}_{\text{conduction + convection + radiation}}
$$

This thermal balance is the *physical reason* the fire point is always above the flash point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The difference (Fire Point − Flash Point) is a rough indicator of *fire hazard severity*. A *small* difference (< 5 °C) means the liquid transitions to a sustained fire very quickly after flashing — these are the most dangerous liquids in fire protection design (Class IB liquids per NFPA 30).

## 2.2 Theoretical Background – Pour Point

As a hydrocarbon liquid is cooled, **n-paraffin waxes** (long-chain alkanes like C18H38, C20H42) begin to crystallize. The first wax crystals form at the **Cloud Point**. As cooling continues, the crystals grow and interlock, forming a **gel-like 3-D network** that traps the remaining liquid. The temperature at which this network becomes rigid enough to *prevent bulk flow* under gravity is the **pour point**.

The pour point is therefore governed by:
- **Wax content** of the crude (paraffinic crudes have high pour points)
- **Molecular weight distribution** (longer chains → higher pour point)
- **Presence of resins / asphaltenes** (act as natural pour point depressants)
- **Previous thermal history** (heating the sample destroys wax crystal "memory")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test Protocol Logic (ASTM D97):** Temperatures are reported as **multiples of 3 °C** because the sample is checked for flow at *3 °C intervals*. The recorded pour point is **3 °C above the lowest temperature at which no movement is observed** in the test jar.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU 2024 Board Exam** — memorize this table. For fire point calculations, only the **barometric pressure correction** is typically numerical. For pour point, the result is read directly — no algebraic computation is required.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corrected Fire Point | $T_{fp,corrected} = T_{fp,observed} + C_{p}$ | $C_{p}$ = barometric pressure correction | **°C** |
| 2 | Pressure Correction | $C_{p} = 0.25 \times (101.325 - P_{atm}) \times 0.1$ | Simplified correction (ASTMD92) | **°C** |
| 3 | Pressure Correction (Full form) | $C_{p} = \dfrac{101.325 - P_{atm}}{8}$ | Common board-exam formula | **°C** |
| 4 | Flash-Fire Point Spread | $\Delta T = T_{fp} - T_{fl}$ | Indicates fire hazard severity | **°C** |
| 5 | Pour Point Reporting | $T_{pp} = T_{no-flow} + 3 \, °C$ | Final report value (ASTM D97) | **°C** |
| 6 | Vapor Pressure (Antoine) | $\log_{10}(P) = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | Used to predict flash / fire point | **mmHg** |
| 7 | Clausius-Clapeyron | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Theoretical basis of vaporization | **dimensionless** |
| 8 | Fire Hazard Index (qualitative) | $\text{FHI} = \dfrac{T_{fp} - T_{ambient}}{T_{fl}}$ | Custom KTU exam indicator | **dimensionless** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Best Practice for KTU Board Exam:** Use $P_{atm}$ for atmospheric pressure (do not use $P_{atm} \vert$ — the vertical bar breaks markdown tables). Always convert $P_{atm}$ from **mmHg** to **kPa** when required ($1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 760 \text{ mmHg}$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Petroleum Refining** | Classifying crude oils (waxy vs. paraffinic), designing pipeline heating systems |
| **Fire Protection Design** | Determining bund wall height, foam application rate, sprinkler temperature rating |
| **Aviation Fuels** | Jet-A / JP-8 pour point specification (-40 °C) for high-altitude flight |
| **Storage Tank Safety** | IS 1446, OISD 118, NFPA 30 classification uses fire point for tank spacing |
| **Lubricant Blending** | Pour Point Depressants (PPDs) like PMA, PAMA are added to engine oils |
| **Cold-Climate Fire Pumps** | Diesel fuel for fire pumps in mountain stations must have pour point < ambient minimum |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Barometric Pressure Correction for Fire Point

The Cleveland Open Cup (COC) apparatus is **open to atmosphere**, so the observed fire point depends on the local barometric pressure. At *lower pressures* (higher altitudes), the partial pressure of oxygen is reduced — the liquid must be heated to a *higher temperature* to vaporize enough fuel to reach the fire point. Conversely, at *higher pressures*, the observed fire point is *lower*.

The **ASTM D92 empirical correction** is derived from a first-order linearization of the Clausius-Clapeyron equation around standard atmospheric pressure.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Standard Reference Pressure**
The COC test is calibrated at standard atmospheric pressure:
$$
P_{std} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa} = 1 \; \text{atm} = 760 \; \text{mmHg}
$$

**Step 2 — Empirical Pressure-Fire Point Correlation (ASTM D92)**
Empirical observations on hydrocarbon liquids yield a linear relationship:
$$
\frac{dT_{fp}}{dP} \approx 0.25 \; \frac{°C}{\text{kPa}} \quad \text{(for open-cup methods)}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate between Observed and Standard Pressure**
$$
\Delta T_{fp} = 0.25 \times (P_{std} - P_{atm})
$$
This gives the temperature correction to *add to the observed fire point* to bring it to standard pressure.

**Step 4 — Final Corrected Fire Point**
$$
T_{fp, corrected} = T_{fp, observed} + 0.25 \times (101.325 - P_{atm})
$$

**Step 5 — Compact Form (Common KTU Board Form)**
$$
T_{fp, corrected} = T_{fp, observed} + \frac{P_{std} - P_{atm}}{4}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-typical):** If $P_{atm} = 95.325$ kPa and $T_{fp, observed} = 210 \; °C$,
> $$T_{fp, corrected} = 210 + 0.25 \times (101.325 - 95.325) = 210 + 0.25 \times 6 = 210 + 1.5 = 211.5 \; °C$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relation Between Fire Point and Flash Point via Clausius-Clapeyron

The flash point $T_{fl}$ and fire point $T_{fp}$ both correspond to the same *lower flammable limit (LFL)* concentration of vapor, but at *different vaporization rates*. The ratio of vapor pressures at these two temperatures can be expressed through the Clausius-Clapeyron equation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Vapor Pressure at Flash Point**
At $T_{fl}$, the vapor pressure $P_{fl}$ corresponds to the LFL concentration:
$$
\ln(P_{fl}) = A - \frac{B}{T_{fl}}
$$
where $A$, $B$ are Antoine constants for the hydrocarbon.

**Step 2 — Vapor Pressure at Fire Point**
At $T_{fp}$, the vapor pressure $P_{fp}$ is *higher* (sustained combustion needs more vapor):
$$
\ln(P_{fp}) = A - \frac{B}{T_{fp}}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract (eliminate A)**
$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{fp}}{P_{fl}}\right) = B \left(\frac{1}{T_{fl}} - \frac{1}{T_{fp}}\right) = \frac{B \cdot (T_{fp} - T_{fl})}{T_{fl} \cdot T_{fp}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Approximate Ratio of Vapor Pressures (Empirical)**
For most hydrocarbons, the ratio of vapor pressures at fire point to flash point is approximately:
$$
\frac{P_{fp}}{P_{fl}} \approx e^{0.5} \approx 1.65
$$
This corresponds to a typical $T_{fp} - T_{fl} \approx 8$ to $20$ °C for kerosene / diesel class fuels.

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form**
$$
\boxed{\,T_{fp} - T_{fl} \;\approx\; \frac{T_{fl}^2 \cdot \ln(1.65)}{B + T_{fl} \cdot \ln(1.65)}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** KTU does **not** typically ask students to solve the full Clausius-Clapeyron derivation. The expected answer is the *qualitative* result: **fire point is always higher than flash point, by 5–30 °C**, and the *physical reason* (heat balance) should be stated clearly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pour Point Calculation (ASTM D97 Logic)

The pour point is a *discrete observation*, not a continuous calculation. However, the ASTM D97 protocol requires a specific arithmetic step to report the final value.

### Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1 — Record No-Flow Temperature $T_{no-flow}$**
The sample jar is cooled in **3 °C steps** and tilted horizontally for **5 seconds** at each step. The *lowest* temperature at which *no surface movement* is observed is recorded:
$$
T_{no-flow} = \text{Observed temperature} \; (\text{in } \, °C)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply ASTM D97 Reporting Rule**
The reported pour point is **3 °C above** the no-flow temperature (since the next-lower test interval is 3 °C colder):
$$
T_{pp} = T_{no-flow} + 3 \, °C
$$

**Step 3 — Round to Nearest Integer (Standard Practice)**
$$
T_{pp, final} = \text{Round}(T_{pp}) \quad \text{to nearest } 1 \, °C
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU-style)**
A diesel sample shows no flow at $T_{no-flow} = -9 \; °C$. The reported pour point is:
$$
T_{pp} = -9 + 3 = -6 \; °C
$$

This is a typical result for **commercial automotive diesel** sold in Kerala during winter.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary (High-Yield KTU Table)

| Parameter | Fire Point | Pour Point |
|---|---|---|
| **Property Type** | High-temperature combustion property | Low-temperature rheological property |
| **What it measures** | Minimum temperature for sustained combustion | Minimum temperature for liquid flow |
| **Test Standard** | ASTM D92 (Cleveland Open Cup) | ASTM D97 |
| **Apparatus** | Cleveland Open Cup (brass, ~70 mL) | Test jar (50 mL) + cooling bath |
| **Sample State** | Heated (rising temperature) | Cooled (falling temperature) |
| **Indicator of** | Fire hazard severity (higher = safer to handle) | Cold-flow operability (lower = better) |
| **Typical Range (Diesel)** | 200 – 250 °C | −6 to −15 °C |
| **Typical Range (Kerosene)** | 150 – 200 °C | −30 to −50 °C |
| **Typical Range (Lube Oil)** | 250 – 350 °C | −6 to +10 °C |
| **Time of Observation** | When flame persists ≥ 5 s after pilot removal | When surface does *not* move in 5 s of tilting |
| **Pressure Dependence** | Yes (open cup) — needs correction | No (closed jar) — no correction needed |
| **Relation to Other Property** | $T_{fp} > T_{fl}$ (always) | $T_{pp} > T_{cp}$ (Cloud Point), $T_{pp} < T_{f}$ (Freezing Point) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** A common student error is *confusing* the apparatus used for the two tests. Cleveland Open Cup is **only** for the fire point. Pour point uses a *glass test jar with a water/ice/CO₂ bath* — never the COC. Examiners will deduct marks for apparatus mix-up.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cleveland Open Cup (COC) Apparatus – Block-Level Functional Architecture

The Cleveland Open Cup is a *physical apparatus* — a Mermaid schematic of its functional flow is the most accurate visual representation in a text environment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Liquid 70 mL Poured into Brass Cup] --> B[Heating Plate Gas or Electric]
    B --> C[Rise in Sample Temperature at 5 to 6 C per min]
    C --> D{Temperature Reached Near Expected Fire Point}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Reduce Heating Rate to 2 C per min]
    E --> F[Apply Test Flame Every 2 C at 4 mm Above Surface]
    F --> G{Flame Flashes Above Surface}
    G -- Yes Transient --> H[Record Flash Point Tfl]
    H --> E
    G -- Yes Flame Persists 5 s --> I[Record Fire Point Tfp]
    I --> J[Stop Heating and Apply Pressure Correction]
    J --> K[Final Corrected Fire Point Tfp corrected]

    styleA fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333
    styleB fill:#A0D8F1,stroke:#333
    styleC fill:#B7E1B1,stroke:#333
    styleD fill:#F4B5B5,stroke:#333
    styleE fill:#B7E1B1,stroke:#333
    styleF fill:#D5B8E6,stroke:#333
    styleG fill:#F4B5B5,stroke:#333
    styleH fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333
    styleI fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    styleJ fill:#A0D8F1,stroke:#333
    styleK fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each **red** node is a *decision point* (test flame application), each **green** node is a *continuous process* (heating), each **blue** node is an *input/calculation*, and each **orange** node is a *recorded output*. The **green terminal node K** is the final reported fire point value.

---

## 4.2 Pour Point Determination – Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Heated to 45 to 50 C to Erase Wax Memory] --> B[Pour 50 mL into Glass Test Jar]
    B --> C[Fit Thermometer and Cork Jacket Assembly]
    C --> D[Place Jar in Cooling Bath]
    D --> E{Cooling to Expected Pour Point}
    E -- No --> D
    E -- Yes --> F[Tilt Jar Horizontally for 5 s Every 3 C Drop]
    F --> G{Surface Movement Observed}
    G -- Yes Liquid Flows --> H[Continue Cooling by 3 C]
    H --> F
    G -- No Surface Stays Still --> I[Record No Flow Temperature T no flow]
    I --> J[Apply ASTM D97 Reporting Rule Tpp = T no flow + 3 C]
    J --> K[Round to Nearest 1 C]
    K --> L[Final Reported Pour Point Tpp]

    styleA fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333
    styleB fill:#A0D8F1,stroke:#333
    styleC fill:#A0D8F1,stroke:#333
    styleD fill:#B7E1B1,stroke:#333
    styleE fill:#F4B5B5,stroke:#333
    styleF fill:#D5B8E6,stroke:#333
    styleG fill:#F4B5B5,stroke:#333
    styleH fill:#B7E1B1,stroke:#333
    styleI fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    styleJ fill:#A0D8F1,stroke:#333
    styleK fill:#A0D8F1,stroke:#333
    styleL fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading:** The decision diamond **G** is the *only* observation that matters. The rest is routine cooling and tilting. Note that the sample is **initially heated** (not cooled from ambient) — this is to dissolve any pre-existing wax crystals and ensure a *standardized thermal history* before cooling.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Process Flow – Fire Point vs. Pour Point in One Lab Session

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP[Sample Preparation]
        SP1[Collect Hydrocarbon Sample] --> SP2[Measure 70 mL for COC]
        SP1 --> SP3[Measure 50 mL for Pour Point Jar]
    end

    SP2 --> FP[Fire Point Test ASTM D92]
    SP3 --> PP[Pour Point Test ASTM D97]

    FP --> FP_Obs[Observed Fire Point]
    FP_Obs --> FP_Corr[Corrected Fire Point with Barometric Pressure]

    PP --> PP_Val[Final Pour Point]

    FP_Corr --> RE[Record and Tabulate Results]
    PP_Val --> RE

    styleFP fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    stylePP fill:#A0D8F1,stroke:#333
    styleRE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Manual Note:** A *single* hydrocarbon sample (typically 100 mL) is sufficient for both tests when run sequentially. The 70 mL is used first for the fire point (COC), and a *fresh* 50 mL aliquot is used for the pour point (after heating to erase wax memory).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define fire point. How is it different from flash point of a hydrocarbon liquid?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Fire point is the *lowest temperature* at which the vapors of a hydrocarbon liquid, when heated under standard test conditions (ASTM D92, Cleveland Open Cup), produce a flame that **sustains itself for at least 5 seconds** after removal of the test flame.

> **Difference from flash point (2 marks):**
> - *Flash point* is the temperature at which the vapors ignite *momentarily* (a brief "flash") when a pilot flame is brought near. The flame does *not* sustain — it self-extinguishes because the rate of vaporization is too low.
> - *Fire point* is the temperature at which the rate of vaporization is high enough that the heat released by combustion *maintains* the liquid surface temperature above the flash point, leading to *self-sustaining* combustion for ≥ 5 seconds.
> - **Fire point is always higher than flash point**, typically by 5–30 °C, depending on the hydrocarbon class.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is pour point? Why is it important for fire engineering applications?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Pour point is the *lowest temperature* (reported as a multiple of 3 °C per ASTM D97) at which a hydrocarbon liquid *just flows* when the test jar is tilted horizontally for 5 seconds under standard cooling conditions.

> **Importance in fire engineering (2 marks):**
> 1. **Fire pump fuel selection:** Diesel used to drive fire pumps in cold regions (mountains, high-altitude installations) must have a *pour point below the design ambient minimum*, or the fuel will gel and the pump will fail.
> 2. **Foam concentrate storage:** AFFF foam concentrates stored outdoors in cold weather must remain flowable; their pour point dictates the storage heating requirement.
> 3. **Pipeline & bund drainage:** Spilled fuel in a bund must remain pumpable at the *worst-expected ambient temperature* for environmental cleanup — pour point sets this limit.
> 4. **Aviation fuel handling:** Jet fuel for airport fire tenders has strict pour point specs to ensure operability in cold-weather rescue operations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **Cleveland Open Cup apparatus** used for the determination of the **fire point** of a hydrocarbon liquid. List the major components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a fire point determination in the Fire Engineering Lab, the following data were recorded:
- Sample: Diesel
- Observed fire point: $T_{fp,obs} = 218 \; °C$
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 96.5 \; \text{kPa}$

Calculate the **corrected fire point** and discuss the safety implications of this value for a bulk diesel storage tank at a coastal installation in Kerala. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

> **Cleveland Open Cup (COC) Apparatus — Block Diagram Description (4 marks):**

The major components and their functions are:

| Component | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Test cup (Brass)** | Brass | Holds ~70 mL of sample; cylindrical with flat bottom |
| **Heating plate** | Cast iron / electric | Provides uniform heat at controlled rate (5–6 °C/min) |
| **Thermometer holder** | Brass | Holds ASTM thermometer 11C/IP 28C vertical in sample |
| **Test flame applicator** | Gas jet / taper | Applies 4 mm flame across cup at intervals |
| **Wind shield** | Sheet metal | Shields flame from drafts (essential in open cup) |
| **Gas supply / electric heater** | — | Energy source for heating |

> **Test procedure (3 marks):**
> 1. **Clean** the brass cup thoroughly, fill with sample up to the filling mark (~70 mL).
> 2. **Heat** at 5–6 °C/min; place the thermometer so its bulb is immersed without touching the bottom.
> 3. **Apply test flame** (4 mm size) across the cup at every **2 °C rise** when temperature is within 28 °C of expected fire point.
> 4. **Reduce heating rate to 2 °C/min** when within 10 °C of expected fire point.
> 5. **Record flash point** when the first transient flame appears above the surface.
> 6. **Continue heating**; the fire point is reached when the flame **persists for at least 5 seconds** after the test flame is removed.
> 7. **Stop heating**, allow to cool, and record the fire point temperature from the thermometer.

---

**Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

> **Step 1 — Identify the correction formula (2 marks):**
> The barometric pressure correction for the Cleveland Open Cup is:
> $$C_p = 0.25 \times (P_{std} - P_{atm})$$

> **Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks):**
> - $P_{std} = 101.325$ kPa
> - $P_{atm} = 96.5$ kPa
> - $T_{fp,obs} = 218 \; °C$
> $$C_p = 0.25 \times (101.325 - 96.5) = 0.25 \times 4.825 = 1.21 \; °C$$

> **Step 3 — Corrected fire point (1 mark):**
> $$T_{fp,corr} = T_{fp,obs} + C_p = 218 + 1.21 = 219.21 \; °C \approx 219 \; °C$$

> **Step 4 — Safety implications (2 marks):**
> - A corrected fire point of **~219 °C** classifies diesel as a **Class IIIA liquid** (per NFPA 30: $93.3 \; °C \le FP < 140 \; °C$ is Class IIIA, but fire point 219 °C is well above that — re-classify as **Class II** since flash point of diesel is typically 52–96 °C and fire point is the *upper* reference).
> - At a **coastal Kerala installation** (ambient max ~38 °C), the fire point is **~181 °C above ambient** — meaning the diesel itself is *not* a spontaneous ignition hazard, but **pool fires from spills** can reach this temperature rapidly and propagate to adjacent tanks.
> - **Design recommendations:** Use **fixed foam chambers** on the storage tank, maintain **bund wall height ≥ 110% of largest tank volume**, and ensure the **sprinkler / foam pourer temperature rating ≥ 100 °C** (per OISD-118).
> - The pressure correction of **+1.21 °C** is small because Kochi/Trivandrum are at *near-sea-level* pressure — no altitude correction issue.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **ASTM D97 procedure** for the determination of the pour point of a hydrocarbon liquid. Include the role of the cooling bath and the significance of the **+3 °C reporting rule**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A lab assistant performed a pour point test on a lube oil sample and recorded the following:

| Step | Bath Temperature (°C) | Observation (Surface Movement?) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | Yes, flows |
| 2 | −3 | Yes, flows |
| 3 | −6 | Yes, slow flow |
| 4 | −9 | **No movement** |
| 5 | −12 | No movement |

Determine the **final pour point** of the sample. If this lube oil is intended for a fire pump in a hill station where the **minimum ambient temperature is −5 °C**, state whether the oil is *suitable* and justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

> **ASTM D97 Procedure (5 marks):**
> 1. **Sample preparation:** Heat the sample to **45–50 °C** to erase any pre-existing wax crystal memory. (This ensures standardized thermal history.)
> 2. **Pour ~50 mL** into the glass test jar (height ~115 mm, marked at 45 mL level).
> 3. **Fit the thermometer** with its bulb immersed ~3 mm above the jar bottom, using a cork jacket assembly to provide air insulation.
> 4. **Cool the jar** in a **cooling bath**. The bath can be:
>    - *Water/ice bath* for temperatures down to +5 °C
>    - *Ice + salt* mixture for 0 °C to −20 °C
>    - *Dry ice (solid CO₂) + acetone* for temperatures down to −50 °C
> 5. **Cool in 3 °C steps**. At each step, remove the jar, **tilt horizontally for 5 seconds**, and observe whether the surface moves.
> 6. **Record the no-flow temperature** — the lowest temperature at which *no* surface movement is observed.

> **Significance of the +3 °C Reporting Rule (2 marks):**
> - The ASTM D97 standard reports the pour point as **3 °C above the no-flow temperature** because the sample was checked at 3 °C intervals. The *lowest temperature at which it might still flow* is therefore 3 °C *above* the no-flow observation.
> - This rule ensures **reproducibility** between labs — without it, results would depend on the cooling step size chosen by the individual lab technician.

---

**Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

> **Step 1 — Identify the no-flow temperature (2 marks):**
> From the observation table, the *lowest temperature at which no movement was observed* is:
> $$T_{no-flow} = -9 \; °C$$
> (The flow at −6 °C is the *last* positive observation; the first no-flow is at −9 °C.)

> **Step 2 — Apply the +3 °C reporting rule (1 mark):**
> $$T_{pp} = T_{no-flow} + 3 = -9 + 3 = -6 \; °C$$

> **Step 3 — Final reported pour point (1 mark):**
> $$\boxed{T_{pp} = -6 \; °C}$$

> **Step 4 — Suitability assessment for the fire pump (3 marks):**
> - **Minimum ambient temperature at site:** $T_{amb,min} = -5 \; °C$
> - **Lube oil pour point:** $T_{pp} = -6 \; °C$
> - **Decision rule:** The oil is suitable *only if* $T_{pp} \le T_{amb,min}$ (so that the oil remains flowable at the coldest expected condition).
> - **Comparison:** $T_{pp} = -6 \; °C < T_{amb,min} = -5 \; °C$ → **oil is just suitable, but with zero margin.**
> - **Recommendation (1 mark):** The oil is *technically suitable* (pour point is 1 °C below the design minimum), but the **safety margin is zero** — any unusually cold snap (e.g., −7 °C in winter) will cause the oil to gel, leading to **fire pump failure during an emergency**. Therefore, the engineer should either:
>   1. **Specify a lube oil with pour point ≤ −15 °C** (recommended for ≥10 °C safety margin), or
>   2. **Install a tank heating / insulation system** to keep the oil above −6 °C at all times.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Direct from KTU Valuation Key Patterns):**
> 1. **Apparatus Confusion (–2 marks):** Students frequently state *"fire point is determined using Pensky-Martens"* — this is **wrong**. Pensky-Martens is for **flash point of viscous oils** (ASTM D93). Fire point is determined using the **Cleveland Open Cup** (ASTM D92). Mark deducted for apparatus misidentification.
> 2. **Forgetting the +3 °C Rule for Pour Point (–1 mark):** Many students directly report the no-flow temperature as the pour point. This is *incorrect* — the +3 °C correction is **mandatory** per ASTM D97.
> 3. **Skipping the Pressure Correction (–2 marks):** For fire point at non-standard pressure, the barometric correction is **mandatory** in board-exam numerical problems. A bare $T_{fp,obs}$ reported as the final answer is *incomplete*.
> 4. **Wrong Units (–1 mark):** Pour point is reported in **°C**, not °F or K. Fire point is **°C**. Mixing units is a common deductable error.
> 5. **Confusing Cloud Point with Pour Point (–1 mark):** Cloud point = *first* wax crystal appearance. Pour point = *complete loss of flow*. They are **not** the same.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Board Exam**

### Fire Point — Key Takeaways
- ✅ **Definition:** Lowest temperature where vapors sustain a flame for **≥ 5 seconds** after test flame removal.
- ✅ **Apparatus:** Cleveland Open Cup (COC), brass cup, 70 mL sample.
- ✅ **Standard:** ASTM D92 / IS 1446.
- ✅ **Heating rate:** 5–6 °C/min initially, 2 °C/min near fire point.
- ✅ **Test flame:** 4 mm diameter, applied every 2 °C near fire point.
- ✅ **Fire point > Flash point** by 5–30 °C.
- ✅ **Pressure correction formula:** $C_p = 0.25 \times (101.325 - P_{atm})$.
- ✅ **Physical reason for fire point being higher:** Heat release rate from burning vapor must exceed heat loss rate (sustained combustion requires thermal balance).

### Pour Point — Key Takeaways
- ✅ **Definition:** Lowest temperature at which liquid *just flows* when test jar is tilted.
- ✅ **Apparatus:** Glass test jar (50 mL) + cooling bath (ice / dry ice / acetone).
- ✅ **Standard:** ASTM D97 / IS 1448 (P:10).
- ✅ **Pre-treatment:** Heat sample to 45–50 °C to erase wax memory.
- ✅ **Cooling steps:** Every 3 °C, check flow.
- ✅ **Reporting rule:** $T_{pp} = T_{no-flow} + 3 \; °C$.
- ✅ **Pour point is *not* the freezing point** — it is a *rheological* property.
- ✅ **Pour point is *higher* than cloud point** (cloud point is *first* crystal appearance).
- ✅ **Engineering use:** Fire pump fuel, foam concentrate storage, pipeline operation, aviation fuel spec.

### Cross-Comparison
- ✅ Fire point = **high-temperature hazard**; Pour point = **low-temperature operability**.
- ✅ Both are **paired lab tests** in PCSFL507 Module Experiment 2.
- ✅ Both are **direct observational** tests — no complex algebra; only the **+3 °C** (pour) and **barometric** (fire) corrections.
- ✅ Both require careful **thermometer placement** and **standardized sample preparation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Board Exam Strategy:** If a 14-mark question appears, structure your answer as: **(i) definition (1 mark) → (ii) apparatus + diagram (3 marks) → (iii) procedure (4–5 marks) → (iv) calculation / observation table (3–4 marks) → (v) safety implication / engineering use (1–2 marks)**. This is the exact pattern KTU examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study on the Effect of Temperature on the Viscosity of Hydrocarbon Liquids Using Redwood Viscometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL507 - FIRE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study on the Effect of Temperature on the Viscosity of Hydrocarbon Liquids Using Redwood Viscometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Viscosity (η)** is the internal resistance offered by a fluid to flow, arising from intermolecular cohesive forces and momentum transfer between adjacent fluid layers. **Kinematic viscosity (ν)** is the ratio of dynamic viscosity (η) to the fluid's density (ρ), expressed as $\nu = \eta / \rho$ with units of **m²/s** (or centistokes, **cSt**, where $1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$).

The **Redwood Viscometer** is a standard, gravity-flow capillary-type viscometer used to measure the **kinematic viscosity of petroleum products and hydrocarbon liquids** at any controlled temperature. It is governed by **IS 1448 (Part 25)** / **IP 70** standards. Two models exist:

- **Redwood Viscometer No. I** – for low-viscosity liquids (efflux time 30–2000 s).
- **Redwood Viscometer No. II** – for high-viscosity liquids (efflux time 20–200 s).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The experiment establishes the inverse relationship between temperature and kinematic viscosity of hydrocarbon fuels — a **critical fire-engineering parameter** influencing spray atomization, pump performance, and pipeline transport in sprinkler hydraulics and foam concentrate distribution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Honey vs. Water" Intuition

Imagine pouring **honey** and **water** from identical bottles. Honey flows sluggishly because its molecules cling tightly to each other; water flows freely. Now, warm the honey on a stove — it suddenly pours like water. This is exactly the behavior of **hydrocarbon liquids**: as temperature rises, molecular kinetic energy overcomes cohesive (van der Waals) forces, and the fluid "thins out," reducing its viscosity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance in Fire Engineering:**
> - **Fuel oil handling** – Viscosity dictates pumping energy and pre-heating requirements.
> - **Foam concentrate proportioning** – Affects induction accuracy in foam systems.
> - **Spray nozzles** – Determines droplet size, which governs cooling efficiency and suppression effectiveness.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Physics of Flow

The Redwood Viscometer operates on the principle of **efflux time** measurement: a fixed volume (**50 mL**) of liquid, held at a precisely controlled temperature, is allowed to discharge under **hydrostatic head** through a **standard orifice** (jet). The time taken (`t` seconds) is empirically correlated to kinematic viscosity.

**Why "Empirical" rather than pure Hagen-Poiseuille?**
Because the jet length and entrance effects deviate from ideal capillary flow, the relationship is captured by an empirical linear-hyperbolic equation calibrated against standard reference fluids.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Hydrostatic Pressure** drives the fluid from the cup through the jet.
2. The **fixed head (h)** above the jet ensures constant driving pressure (cup is replenished so head is practically constant).
3. The **efflux time (t)** for 50 mL collection is recorded.
4. Empirical constants **A** and **B** are used to convert time to kinematic viscosity.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units | Validity Range |
|---|---------|-------------------------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | $\nu = A \cdot t - \dfrac{B}{t}$ | Redwood empirical equation | **cSt** | t = 30–2000 s (No. I) |
| 2 | $\nu = 0.26 \, t - \dfrac{172}{t}$ | Redwood **No. I** (A = 0.26, B = 172) | cSt | 30 ≤ t ≤ 2000 s |
| 3 | $\nu = 0.247 \, t - \dfrac{65}{t}$ | Redwood **No. II** (A = 0.247, B = 65) | cSt | 20 ≤ t ≤ 200 s |
| 4 | $1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 1 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$ | Unit conversion | — | Universal |
| 5 | $\nu = \dfrac{\eta}{\rho}$ | Definition of kinematic viscosity | m²/s | Definition |
| 6 | $\ln \nu = \ln A' - \dfrac{E_a}{R T}$ | Arrhenius-type temp–viscosity relation | — | All Newtonian liquids |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{\text{bath}} \leq \pm 0.5$ | Bath temperature tolerance (during test) | °C | Standard requirement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants A and B are NOT universal** — they are calibrated for the specific jet geometry. Never use Redwood No. II constants for a No. I instrument. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire pump suction lines:** Highly viscous fuel (e.g., HFO) must be heated to maintain ν < 200 cSt for proper pump priming.
- **Foam concentrate injection:** Foam proportioners (inductors) are calibrated for specific viscosity ranges; deviations alter induction percentage.
- **Sprinkler spray patterns:** Viscosity affects droplet trajectory, impacting water distribution and fire suppression.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

### 3.1 Derivation of the Redwood Empirical Equation

The **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for laminar flow through a capillary under constant head gives:

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi \, r^4 \, \rho \, g \, h}{8 \, \eta \, L}$$

For a fixed volume V flowing in time t, $Q = V/t$:

$$\dfrac{V}{t} = \dfrac{\pi \, r^4 \, \rho \, g \, h}{8 \, \eta \, L}$$

Since $\nu = \eta/\rho$, rearranging:

$$\nu = \dfrac{\pi \, r^4 \, g \, h}{8 \, L} \cdot t = K \cdot t$$

This is the **ideal** form (kinematic viscosity proportional to efflux time). However, due to **kinetic energy correction** at the jet entrance, an additional negative term is required:

$$\nu = K \cdot t - \dfrac{C}{t}$$

By calibration against standard reference oils at the **National Physical Laboratory (UK)**, the empirical constants for the standard 50 mL Redwood No. I viscometer are obtained as:

$$A = 0.26, \quad B = 172$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\nu \, (\text{cSt}) = 0.26 \, t - \dfrac{172}{t}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A sample of furnace oil is tested in a **Redwood Viscometer No. I** at three different temperatures. The efflux times for 50 mL collection are recorded as:

| Temperature (°C) | Efflux Time `t` (s) |
|------------------|---------------------|
| 40 | 412 |
| 60 | 198 |
| 80 | 110 |

**Calculate the kinematic viscosity at each temperature, convert to m²/s, and plot ν vs T.**

#### Step 1 — Calculate ν at 40 °C

$$\nu_{40} = (0.26 \times 412) - \dfrac{172}{412}$$

$$\nu_{40} = 107.12 - 0.4175$$

$$\nu_{40} = 106.70 \, \text{cSt}$$

Converting to SI:

$$\nu_{40} = 106.70 \times 10^{-6} = 1.067 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

#### Step 2 — Calculate ν at 60 °C

$$\nu_{60} = (0.26 \times 198) - \dfrac{172}{198}$$

$$\nu_{60} = 51.48 - 0.8687$$

$$\nu_{60} = 50.61 \, \text{cSt} = 5.061 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate ν at 80 °C

$$\nu_{80} = (0.26 \times 110) - \dfrac{172}{110}$$

$$\nu_{80} = 28.60 - 1.5636$$

$$\nu_{80} = 27.04 \, \text{cSt} = 2.704 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

#### Step 4 — Tabulated Results

| Temp (°C) | Temp (K) | t (s) | ν (cSt) | ν (m²/s) |
|-----------|----------|-------|---------|----------|
| 40 | 313 | 412 | 106.70 | $1.067 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 60 | 333 | 198 | 50.61 | $5.061 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 80 | 353 | 110 | 27.04 | $2.704 \times 10^{-5}$ |

#### Step 5 — Graphical Plot (ν vs T)

A smooth curve is drawn with **T (°C)** on the **x-axis** and **ν (cSt)** on the **y-axis**. The curve is **monotonically decreasing** and asymptotic toward lower temperatures — characteristic of hydrocarbon behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **For Arrhenius verification:** Plot $\ln \nu$ vs $1/T$ (K⁻¹). A straight line confirms Newtonian thermal behavior; the slope yields $E_a / R$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Redwood Viscometer — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional flow architecture** of the Redwood Viscometer apparatus, mapping component interactions and signal/data flow during a single test run.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Hydrocarbon Liquid Sample] --> A2[Sample Cup with Level Mark]
    A2 --> A3[Jet Orifice 1.62 mm Diameter]
    A3 --> A4[Ball Valve Control]
    A4 --> A5[Efflux Stream 50 mL]
    A5 --> A6[Kohlrausch Flask Receiver]
    A6 --> A7[Stopwatch Timing Module]
    A7 --> A8[Time t in seconds]
    A8 --> A9[Computation Block A t minus B over t]
    A9 --> A10[Kinematic Viscosity cSt]
    A10 --> A11[Unit Conversion 10 power minus 6]
    A11 --> A12[Viscosity in m square per second]
    B1[Water Bath with Heater] --> A2
    B2[Thermometer 0 to 110 degree C] --> A2
    B3[Stirrer Motor] --> A2
    C1[Temperature Variation Loop] --> A10
    C1 --> A13[Plot nu vs T Curve]
    A13 --> A14[Arrhenius Verification ln nu vs 1 by T]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Component Legend

| Block | Physical Component | Function |
|-------|-------------------|----------|
| A1 → A2 | Sample cup (brass, ~85 mL capacity) | Holds test liquid up to gauge mark |
| A3 | Jet orifice (No. I = 1.62 mm, No. II = 3.4 mm) | Standardized flow restriction |
| A4 | Brass ball valve | Initiates / stops flow |
| A6 | Kohlrausch flask (50 mL ± 0.05 mL) | Volume collection |
| B1 | Thermostatically controlled water bath | Maintains test temperature ± 0.5 °C |
| B2 | ASTM thermometer | Independent temperature verification |
| B3 | Mechanical stirrer | Ensures thermal uniformity |
| C1 | Iteration loop | Repeats test at multiple temperatures |

### 4.3 Typical ν vs T Curve (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["T = 40 deg C, nu = 106.7 cSt"] --> P2["T = 60 deg C, nu = 50.6 cSt"]
    P2 --> P3["T = 80 deg C, nu = 27.0 cSt"]
    P3 --> P4["T = 100 deg C, nu near 10 cSt"]
    P4 --> P5["Asymptotic Fall toward Lower nu"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The curve is **NOT linear** — it follows an **exponential-decay form** governed by the Arrhenius-type equation. KTU expects students to identify this trend and justify it physically (molecular kinetic energy overcoming cohesive forces).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define kinematic viscosity. State the empirical equation used to calculate the kinematic viscosity of a hydrocarbon liquid from a Redwood Viscometer No. I, with the standard values of the empirical constants.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Kinematic viscosity is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity (η) to the density (ρ) of the fluid:

$$\nu = \dfrac{\eta}{\rho} \quad (\text{units: m}^2/\text{s} \text{ or cSt})$$

**Equation with constants (2 Marks):**
For a Redwood Viscometer No. I, the empirical equation is:

$$\nu = A \cdot t - \dfrac{B}{t}$$

with **A = 0.26** and **B = 172**, valid for efflux times `t` between **30 s and 2000 s**. The result is obtained in **centistokes (cSt)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why does the kinematic viscosity of hydrocarbon liquids decrease with an increase in temperature? Mention the role of molecular cohesive forces.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

As temperature increases, the **kinetic energy of molecules** rises, increasing the average intermolecular spacing and reducing the magnitude of **van der Waals (cohesive) forces** between adjacent fluid layers. (2 Marks)

Consequently, the internal friction — and hence the **dynamic viscosity (η)** — falls. Since density (ρ) decreases only marginally, the **kinematic viscosity ν = η/ρ** also decreases sharply with rising temperature. (1 Mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write only "temperature increases, viscosity decreases" without explaining the **molecular mechanism** (cohesive forces vs. kinetic energy). This is a **1-mark deduction** in KTU valuation. Always state the *cause* at the molecular level.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **construction and working principle** of a Redwood Viscometer No. I with a neat labeled diagram. List the apparatus and standard test conditions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A petroleum oil gives an efflux time of **250 seconds** in a Redwood Viscometer No. I at **50 °C**. Calculate the kinematic viscosity in **cSt** and in **m²/s**. State whether the value is acceptable for a typical **diesel fuel** (acceptable range: 2–8 cSt at 40 °C, qualitatively compared).

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

- **Sample cup (2 marks):** Cylindrical brass cup, capacity ~85 mL, fitted with a leveling gauge. Closed at the bottom by a precisely bored **jet (orifice)** of **1.62 mm** diameter for No. I. A ball valve regulates flow.
- **Water bath (1.5 marks):** A surrounding water bath with heating coil, stirrer, and thermostatic control maintains test temperature within **± 0.5 °C**.
- **Working principle (2.5 marks):** The cup is filled with the test liquid to the gauge mark. After thermal equilibrium, the ball valve is opened and a **50 mL Kohlrausch flask** is placed to collect the efflux. The **time `t`** for 50 mL collection is noted. Kinematic viscosity is computed using the empirical equation. The experiment is repeated at multiple temperatures.
- **Apparatus list (1 mark):** Redwood cup, jet, ball valve, water bath, stirrer, ASTM thermometer, Kohlrausch flask (50 mL), stopwatch.

**(b) Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given: $t = 250$ s, T = 50 °C, Redwood No. I.

$$\nu = (0.26 \times 250) - \dfrac{172}{250}$$

$$\nu = 65.00 - 0.688$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 64.31 \text{ cSt}}$$

Converting to SI units:

$$\nu = 64.31 \times 10^{-6} = 6.431 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Comparison (1 mark for comment):** The value 64.31 cSt at 50 °C is **too high** for diesel fuel. Even at the warmer 50 °C (above standard 40 °C test), it suggests the oil is **heavier than diesel** — likely a **light fuel oil or furnace oil**, indicating it is unsuitable for direct diesel-engine use.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **experimental procedure** to study the effect of temperature on the viscosity of a hydrocarbon liquid using a Redwood Viscometer. State **five precautions** to be observed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The kinematic viscosities of an oil at three temperatures are:

| T (°C) | ν (cSt) |
|--------|---------|
| 30 | 180 |
| 50 | 75 |
| 70 | 38 |

Verify whether the liquid obeys the **Arrhenius-type relation** $\ln \nu = \ln A' - \dfrac{E_a}{R T}$ by computing values and sketching the expected straight-line plot.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure and Precautions (7 Marks):**

**Procedure (4.5 marks):**
1. Clean the Redwood cup thoroughly with a suitable solvent and dry it. (0.5)
2. Close the ball valve and fill the cup with the test oil up to the gauge mark. (0.5)
3. Assemble the apparatus and allow thermal equilibrium in the water bath for ~10 minutes. (0.5)
4. Place a clean dry **Kohlrausch flask** (50 mL) directly below the jet. (0.5)
5. Open the ball valve and **simultaneously start the stopwatch**. (0.5)
6. Record the time when exactly **50 mL** of oil is collected. (0.5)
7. Repeat the test at **4–5 different temperatures** (e.g., 40, 50, 60, 70, 80 °C). (0.5)
8. Calculate ν at each temperature using the empirical equation. (0.5)
9. Plot **ν vs T** and **ln ν vs 1/T** graphs. (0.5)

**Five Precautions (2.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. Maintain bath temperature within **± 0.5 °C** of the test temperature.
