2. The jet must be **clean and free of deposits**; clean with solvent before each run.
3. The **Kohlrausch flask** must be **calibrated and dry**.
4. Avoid **air bubbles** in the sample; stir gently and wait for bubbles to escape.
5. Take **at least two readings** at each temperature and average; reject outliers.

**(b) Arrhenius Verification (7 Marks):**

Compute $1/T$ and $\ln \nu$:

| T (°C) | T (K) | $1/T \times 10^{-3}$ (K⁻¹) | ν (cSt) | $\ln \nu$ |
|--------|-------|----------------------------|---------|-----------|
| 30 | 303 | 3.300 | 180 | 5.193 |
| 50 | 323 | 3.096 | 75 | 4.317 |
| 70 | 343 | 2.915 | 38 | 3.638 |

**Linear fit reasoning (4 marks):**
The three points $(3.300, 5.193)$, $(3.096, 4.317)$, $(2.915, 3.638)$ lie approximately on a straight line with **negative slope**. Compute slope:

$$m = \dfrac{4.317 - 5.193}{3.096 - 3.300} = \dfrac{-0.876}{-0.204} \times 10^3 = 4294 \text{ K}$$

$$m = \dfrac{3.638 - 4.317}{2.915 - 3.096} = \dfrac{-0.679}{-0.181} \times 10^3 = 3751 \text{ K}$$

Slopes are approximately equal, confirming the **Arrhenius-type linear relation** with:

$$\dfrac{E_a}{R} \approx 4000 \text{ K} \implies E_a \approx 33.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Plot description (2 marks):** A graph of $\ln \nu$ (y-axis) vs $1/T$ (x-axis) yields a **straight line with negative slope**, confirming Newtonian thermal behavior.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Common mistakes students make in Part B (b):
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** before taking $1/T$ → instant **2-mark loss**.
> 2. **Using natural log (ln) vs. log₁₀** inconsistently — KTU specifically expects **natural log (ln)** for Arrhenius.
> 3. Failing to **state the slope interpretation** ($E_a/R$) explicitly — loses the **"application" mark**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Redwood No. I** is used for **low-viscosity** hydrocarbon liquids; **No. II** for **high-viscosity** liquids.
- ✅ **Empirical formula:** $\nu = 0.26 t - 172/t$ (No. I) → result in **cSt**.
- ✅ **Unit conversion:** $1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$.
- ✅ **Volume collected:** Always **50 mL** in a calibrated **Kohlrausch flask**.
- ✅ **Temperature tolerance:** Maintain bath within **± 0.5 °C** of test temperature.
- ✅ **Trend:** Viscosity **decreases exponentially** with temperature; plot is a **monotonically falling curve**.
- ✅ **Arrhenius check:** $\ln \nu$ vs $1/T$ (K⁻¹) gives a **straight line** with negative slope $-E_a/R$.
- ✅ **Jet diameter:** No. I = **1.62 mm**, No. II = **3.4 mm** (don't mix them up).
- ✅ **Use ASTM thermometer** with **0.5 °C least count** for accuracy.
- ✅ **Molecular reason for viscosity drop:** Increased kinetic energy overcomes **van der Waals cohesive forces**.
- ✅ **Fire-engineering relevance:** Affects fuel pumpability, foam proportioning, and sprinkler spray atomization.
- ✅ **Constants A and B** are **empirically calibrated** — not derivable from first principles due to kinetic energy corrections.
- ✅ **Take multiple readings**, average, and **discard outliers** for reliable data.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Tests on Dry Chemical Powder as per Relevant Indian Standard Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL507 - FIRE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Physical Tests on Dry Chemical Powder as per Relevant Indian Standard Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Dry Chemical Powder (DCP)** is a fine, free-flowing fire-extinguishing agent composed primarily of monoammonium phosphate, ammonium sulphate, or other base chemicals, which extinguishes fire by inhibiting the chain reaction of combustion. As per **IS 4308:1982** and **IS 14609:1999**, every batch of DCP intended for fire-fighting applications must conform to a defined set of *physical* (and chemical) performance characteristics before being certified fit for service in stored-pressure fire extinguishers.

**Physical Tests on DCP** are a battery of standardized laboratory procedures that quantify the *non-chemical* engineering properties of the powder — its density, granulometry, moisture content, flow behaviour, abrasiveness, and caking resistance. These tests ensure the powder remains *pneumatic-conveyable* (i.e., it can be propelled by nitrogen propellant without clogging valves, hoses, or nozzles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> Module Experiment 2 specifically requires the student to perform, observe, and record the physical test results of DCP in accordance with the *relevant Indian Standard Specifications* — namely **IS 4308** for BC powder, **IS 14609** for ABC powder, and **IS 15493** for general powder characteristics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of Dry Chemical Powder as the **"instant coffee of firefighting."** Just as instant coffee must be fine, dry, and free-flowing so it dissolves instantly in hot water, DCP must be fine, dry, and free-flowing so it discharges instantly from an extinguisher into a flame. If the coffee is clumpy (caked) or damp (moist), it blocks the spoon and ruins the cup — in the same way, lumpy or moist DCP blocks the extinguisher nozzle and fails to suppress fire.

The **physical tests** are essentially a "**quality control passport**" — much like how pharmaceutical companies test a tablet for *hardness, disintegration time, and flow* before shipping, fire engineers test DCP for *apparent density, sieve grading, and flowability* before allowing it into a pressurized vessel.

### 1.3 Indian Standards Governing Physical Tests

| IS Code | Year | Title / Coverage |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **IS 4308** | 1982 | Specification for Dry Chemical Powder (BC type) for fire-fighting |
| **IS 14609** | 1999 | Specification for ABC type Dry Chemical Powder |
| **IS 15493** | 2004 | Methods of test for Dry Chemical Powder |
| **IS 636** | 1988 | Non-Performance requirements for portable extinguishers (uses DCP grades) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in Bold):**
> - **Apparent (Bulk) Density:** 0.85 to 1.05 g/cm³ (typical acceptance band per IS)
> - **Particle Size:** 100% passing through **75 µm IS sieve** for ABC grade (finer than BC)
> - **Moisture Content:** **≤ 0.25 %** by mass (max permissible)
> - **Flow Rate:** ≥ **1200 g/min** through standard funnel
> - **Temperature of Test:** **27 °C ± 2 °C** (laboratory ambient)

### 1.4 Significance in the Fire Engineering Lab
Physical tests are the *first line of acceptance* for any DCP batch. Even if the chemical composition (monoammonium phosphate percentage, silica coating, etc.) is perfect, physical failure — for example, caking due to moisture ingress — can render the entire batch *useless* in a real fire emergency. The lab experiment trains students to use the *exact test apparatus and procedures* prescribed by the BIS, so that they can independently certify incoming powder lots in industry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Physical Tests
The Indian Standards collectively prescribe **seven major physical tests** on DCP. These are not optional — every batch must clear all seven for certification:

1. **Apparent Density Test (Bulk / Tap Density)**
2. **Particle Size Distribution (Sieve Analysis)**
3. **Moisture Content Determination**
4. **Flowability (Free-Flow) Test**
5. **Abrasiveness Test**
6. **Caking Resistance Test**
7. **Water Repellency (Repellent Quality) Test**

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

#### Test 1 — Apparent Density
- **Why?** Determines how much powder (by mass) occupies a given volume. Critical for extinguisher charge calculations.
- **How?** A known mass of powder is gently poured into a graduated cylinder; the unsettled volume gives *bulk density*. After mechanical tapping (e.g., 100 taps on a tap density apparatus), the re-settled volume gives *tap density*.
- **Governing Formula:**
$$
\rho_{\text{bulk}} \;=\; \frac{m}{V_{\text{bulk}}}
\qquad \text{and} \qquad
\rho_{\text{tap}} \;=\; \frac{m}{V_{\text{tap}}}
$$
where $m$ = mass of powder (g), $V$ = volume occupied (cm³).

#### Test 2 — Particle Size Distribution
- **Why?** Controls discharge velocity, throw range, and surface-area-driven chemical action.
- **How?** A stack of IS sieves (typically **75 µm, 45 µm, and pan**) is loaded with ~100 g of powder and vibrated for a defined period.
- **Governing Formula:**
$$
\% \text{ Retained} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{sieve}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100
\qquad ; \qquad
\% \text{ Passing} \;=\; 100 - \%\text{ Retained}
$$

#### Test 3 — Moisture Content
- **Why?** Moisture causes caking, corrodes the extinguisher vessel, and chemically degrades the powder.
- **How?** A known mass of powder is oven-dried at **105 °C ± 2 °C** for **2 hours**, cooled in a desiccator, and re-weighed.
- **Governing Formula:**
$$
M \,=\, \frac{W_{1} - W_{2}}{W_{1}} \times 100 \;\; (\%)
$$
where $W_{1}$ = initial mass (g), $W_{2}$ = mass after drying (g).

#### Test 4 — Flowability
- **Why?** Powder must discharge uniformly under propellant gas pressure.
- **How?** A standard **flow cup funnel** (Hopper with 6 mm orifice per IS 15493) is filled and the time for **120 g of powder** to discharge is recorded.
- **Governing Formula:**
$$
Q_{\text{flow}} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{discharged}}}{t_{\text{flow}}} \quad \big[\text{g/min}\big]
$$

#### Test 5 — Abrasiveness
- **Why?** Hard, angular particles erode valve seats and discharge hoses.
- **How?** Powder is agitated against a polished metal disc; mass loss of the disc = abrasiveness index.
- **Governing Formula:**
$$
A \,=\, \frac{m_{\text{disc,initial}} - m_{\text{disc,final}}}{m_{\text{powder}}} \quad \big[\text{mg/kg}\big]
$$

#### Test 6 — Caking Resistance
- **Why?** Powder stored for years must not solidify into lumps.
- **How?** A measured quantity is subjected to elevated humidity (e.g., 75 % RH) and temperature (40 °C) for a defined period, then sieved; % residue on a coarse sieve = caking index.
- **Governing Formula:**
$$
\text{Caking Index} \,=\, \frac{m_{\text{retained on coarse sieve}}}{m_{\text{initial}}} \times 100
$$

#### Test 7 — Water Repellency
- **Why?** DCP must not absorb atmospheric humidity.
- **How?** A measured mass is exposed to controlled humid air; mass gain = hygroscopic absorption.
- **Governing Formula:**
$$
H \,=\, \frac{m_{\text{final}} - m_{\text{initial}}}{m_{\text{initial}}} \times 100
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Test | Governing Equation | Standard Unit | Typical IS Acceptance |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Apparent Density | $\rho = m / V$ | g/cm³ | **0.85 – 1.05** |
| 2 | Sieve Retention | $R = m_{\text{sieve}} / m_{\text{total}} \times 100$ | % | < 5 % on 75 µm |
| 3 | Moisture Content | $M = (W_{1} - W_{2}) / W_{1} \times 100$ | % | **≤ 0.25** |
| 4 | Flow Rate | $Q = m_{\text{disch}} / t$ | g/min | **≥ 1200** |
| 5 | Abrasiveness | $A = \Delta m_{\text{disc}} / m_{\text{powder}}$ | mg/kg | ≤ 50 |
| 6 | Caking Index | $C = m_{\text{cake}} / m_{\text{initial}} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 5 |
| 7 | Hygroscopicity | $H = \Delta m / m_{\text{initial}} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 1.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Symbols:** All vertical-bar / modulus symbols in the formulas above have been replaced with the LaTeX safe form ($\rho$, $m$, $V$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do *not* use raw `|` in KTU submission tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Extinguisher Design:** Knowing $\rho_{\text{bulk}}$ lets the designer calculate the fill mass for a given vessel volume.
- **Propellant Sizing:** Flow rate data informs the nitrogen charge pressure required to discharge powder over a 5–7 m throw range.
- **Shelf-Life Certification:** Caking + moisture tests together predict whether the powder will remain serviceable for the **12-year statutory re-test cycle** under IS 2190.
- **Hose & Nozzle Material Selection:** Abrasiveness data determines whether brass, aluminium, or polymer nozzles are acceptable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Tap Density from Bulk Density
The relationship between bulk density and tap density yields the **Carr Index (Compressibility Index)**, a critical flowability predictor used widely in powder handling engineering.

**Starting Definitions:**

$$
\rho_{\text{bulk}} = \frac{m}{V_{\text{bulk}}}
\qquad , \qquad
\rho_{\text{tap}} = \frac{m}{V_{\text{tap}}}
$$

**Compressibility (Carr) Index definition:**

$$
C_{\text{index}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{tap}} - \rho_{\text{bulk}}}{\rho_{\text{tap}}} \times 100
$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{index}} &= \frac{\dfrac{m}{V_{\text{bulk}}} - \dfrac{m}{V_{\text{tap}}}}{\dfrac{m}{V_{\text{tap}}}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{m \cdot V_{\text{tap}} - m \cdot V_{\text{bulk}}}{V_{\text{bulk}} \cdot V_{\text{tap}}}}{\dfrac{m}{V_{\text{tap}}}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{m(V_{\text{tap}} - V_{\text{bulk}})}{V_{\text{bulk}} \cdot V_{\text{tap}}} \times \frac{V_{\text{tap}}}{m} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{V_{\text{tap}} - V_{\text{bulk}}}{V_{\text{bulk}}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the Carr Index can equivalently be expressed as a *volume ratio*:

$$
\boxed{\, C_{\text{index}} = \left( \frac{V_{\text{tap}} - V_{\text{bulk}}}{V_{\text{bulk}}} \right) \times 100 \,}
$$

This is the same expression as the **Hausner Ratio** in reciprocal form:

$$
H_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{tap}}}{\rho_{\text{bulk}}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{bulk}}}{V_{\text{tap}}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Apparent Density & Moisture Content

**Given Data (from a KTU lab observation sheet):**
- Mass of empty graduated cylinder, $m_c = 120.0$ g
- Mass of cylinder + powder (after pouring), $m_1 = 220.0$ g
- Reading on cylinder: $V_{\text{bulk}} = 95$ cm³
- After 100 taps: $V_{\text{tap}} = 82$ cm³
- Initial powder mass for moisture test, $W_1 = 50.00$ g
- Mass after 2 h at 105 °C, $W_2 = 49.86$ g

**Step 1 — Mass of powder:**

$$
m = m_1 - m_c = 220.0 - 120.0 = 100.0 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Bulk Density:**

$$
\rho_{\text{bulk}} = \frac{m}{V_{\text{bulk}}} = \frac{100.0}{95} = 1.0526 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Tap Density:**

$$
\rho_{\text{tap}} = \frac{m}{V_{\text{tap}}} = \frac{100.0}{82} = 1.2195 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Carr Index:**

$$
C_{\text{index}} = \left( \frac{1.2195 - 1.0526}{1.2195} \right) \times 100 = 13.68 \,\%
$$

A Carr Index of 13.68 % indicates **good flowability** (5 – 15 % range per pharmacopeial and powder-handling standards).

**Step 5 — Moisture Content:**

$$
M = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{50.00 - 49.86}{50.00} \times 100 = 0.28 \,\%
$$

Since $M = 0.28\,\% > 0.25\,\%$, the **batch FAILS the IS 4308 moisture criterion** and must be rejected or re-dried.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Particle Size Distribution (Sieve Analysis)

**Given Data:**
- Total powder mass loaded: $m_{\text{total}} = 100.0$ g
- Mass retained on 75 µm sieve: $m_{75} = 4.2$ g
- Mass retained on 45 µm sieve: $m_{45} = 12.6$ g
- Mass retained on pan (< 45 µm): $m_{\text{pan}} = 83.2$ g

**Step 1 — % Retained on each sieve:**

$$
R_{75} = \frac{4.2}{100} \times 100 = 4.2 \,\%
$$

$$
R_{45} = \frac{12.6}{100} \times 100 = 12.6 \,\%
$$

**Step 2 — % Passing (cumulative):**

$$
P_{75} = 100 - 4.2 = 95.8 \,\%
$$

$$
P_{45} = 95.8 - 12.6 = 83.2 \,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Verification (mass balance):**

$$
4.2 + 12.6 + 83.2 = 100.0 \text{ g} \;\;\checkmark
$$

For an **ABC-grade DCP** per **IS 14609**, the requirement is typically *at least 95 % passing 75 µm* — here $P_{75} = 95.8\,\%$, so the batch **passes**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pass / Fail Decision Logic

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision} &= \text{AND}\big[\,\rho_{\text{bulk}} \in [0.85, 1.05],\; M \leq 0.25\%, \\
&\quad\;\; P_{75} \geq 95\%,\; Q_{\text{flow}} \geq 1200,\; C \leq 5\% \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

All conditions must be true for the batch to be *IS-conformant*. A single failure (e.g., the moisture = 0.28 % in §3.2) is sufficient to reject the entire lot.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DCP Physical Testing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: DCP Batch Sample Received] --> B0[Sample Conditioning: 27 deg C, 24 h]
    B0 --> C0[Weigh Representative Sub-Sample]

    C0 --> D1[Test 1: Apparent Density]
    C0 --> D2[Test 2: Sieve Analysis]
    C0 --> D3[Test 3: Moisture Content]
    C0 --> D4[Test 4: Flowability]
    C0 --> D5[Test 5: Abrasiveness]
    C0 --> D6[Test 6: Caking Resistance]
    C0 --> D7[Test 7: Water Repellency]

    D1 --> E1[Compute rho bulk and rho tap]
    D2 --> E2[Compute percent retained on each sieve]
    D3 --> E3[Compute moisture percent M]
    D4 --> E4[Compute flow rate Q]
    D5 --> E5[Compute abrasiveness A]
    D6 --> E6[Compute caking index]
    D7 --> E7[Compute hygroscopicity H]

    E1 --> F1{rho in 0.85 to 1.05?}
    E2 --> F2{95 percent passes 75 micron?}
    E3 --> F3{M less than or equal 0.25 percent?}
    E4 --> F4{Q greater than or equal 1200?}
    E5 --> F5{A less than or equal 50?}
    E6 --> F6{C less than or equal 5?}
    E7 --> F7{H less than or equal 1?}

    F1 -->|Yes| G1
    F1 -->|No| H1
    F2 -->|Yes| G2
    F2 -->|No| H2
    F3 -->|Yes| G3
    F3 -->|No| H3
    F4 -->|Yes| G4
    F4 -->|No| H4
    F5 -->|Yes| G5
    F5 -->|No| H5
    F6 -->|Yes| G6
    F6 -->|No| H6
    F7 -->|Yes| G7
    F7 -->|No| H7

    G1 --> Z[All Checks Passed]
    G2 --> Z
    G3 --> Z
    G4 --> Z
    G5 --> Z
    G6 --> Z
    G7 --> Z

    H1 --> R1[Reject: Re-Granulate]
    H2 --> R2[Reject: Re-Grind]
    H3 --> R3[Reject: Re-Dry at 105 deg C]
    H4 --> R4[Reject: Re-Silica Coat]
    H5 --> R5[Reject: Soften Particle]
    H6 --> R6[Reject: Re-Test Storage]
    H7 --> R7[Reject: Re-Coat Hydrophobic]

    Z --> END[Certify Batch as IS 4308 / IS 14609 Compliant]
    R1 --> END
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
    R4 --> END
    R5 --> END
    R6 --> END
    R7 --> END
```

### 4.2 Apparatus Inter-Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[IS Sieve Set: 75, 45, Pan] --> SIEVE[Sieve Shaker]
    O2[Graduated Cylinder 100 mL] --> TAPP[Tap Density Apparatus]
    O3[Oven 105 deg C] --> OVEN[Drying Chamber + Desiccator]
    O4[Hopper Funnel 6 mm Orifice] --> FLOW[Flow Cup Stand]
    O5[Polished Brass Disc] --> ABR[Abrasiveness Rotator]
    O6[Humidity Chamber 75 percent RH] --> CAKE[Caking Test Cabinet]
    O7[Humidity Cabinet] --> REP[Repellent Test Chamber]

    SIEVE --> DATA[Test Data Sheet]
    TAPP --> DATA
    OVEN --> DATA
    FLOW --> DATA
    ABR --> DATA
    CAKE --> DATA
    REP --> DATA

    DATA --> REPORT[Lab Report: Physical Test Results per IS]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Batch Acceptance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Batch Tested] --> CHECK{All 7 Tests within IS limits?}
    CHECK -->|Yes| ACC[Accept and Certify]
    CHECK -->|No| FAIL[Identify Failed Test]
    FAIL --> RET[Re-Process Batch]
    RET --> RETEST[Re-Test Full Panel]
    RETEST --> CHECK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A0`, `D1`, `F1`, `Z`) and labels use only plain uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown formatting inside node labels — to ensure clean rendering in the KTU lab notebook export.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three physical tests prescribed by IS 4308 for Dry Chemical Powder and state the acceptance limit for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Apparent (Bulk) Density Test** — Acceptance limit: 0.85 to 1.05 g/cm³. **(1 mark)**
2. **Moisture Content Test** — Acceptance limit: ≤ 0.25 % by mass when dried at 105 °C for 2 hours. **(1 mark)**
3. **Particle Size Distribution (Sieve Analysis)** — At least 95 % must pass through 75 µm IS sieve. **(1 mark)**

> [!TIP]
> Examiner awards full 3 marks only if the *standard reference* (IS 4308 / IS 14609) is cited alongside the limit. Students commonly lose 1 mark for omitting the IS code.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the significance of the Carr Index in Dry Chemical Powder quality control? How is it computed?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The Carr Index (Compressibility Index) quantifies the **flowability** of DCP — a high value indicates poor flow, caking tendency, and risk of nozzle blockage. **(1 mark)**
- Formula:

$$
C_{\text{index}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{tap}} - \rho_{\text{bulk}}}{\rho_{\text{tap}}} \times 100
$$

- **(1 mark)**
- An index **below 15 %** is considered acceptable for DCP; **above 25 %** indicates poor flowability and the batch is rejected. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat procedure the **Apparent Density and Tap Density Test** on Dry Chemical Powder as per **IS 15493:2004**. Discuss how the result influences extinguisher charge calculations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of ABC-type DCP weighing **120 g** is tested for moisture content. After oven-drying at 105 °C for 2 h, the residue weighs **119.65 g**. Sieve analysis yields 6.0 g retained on 75 µm, 18.5 g on 45 µm, and the rest on the pan. Determine whether the batch is **IS 14609 compliant** with respect to moisture and particle size. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):** *(7 marks)*

**Apparatus Required (1 mark):**
- 100 mL graduated glass cylinder (accuracy ± 0.5 mL)
- Analytical balance (accuracy ± 0.01 g)
- Tap density apparatus (mechanical, 100 taps at 10 mm drop height)
- Drying oven set to 105 °C ± 2 °C
- Desiccator with silica gel

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Weigh the empty cylinder ($m_c$) accurately to 0.01 g.
2. Gently pour approximately 100 g of DCP through a wide-stem funnel into the cylinder. Do *not* tap or compact.
3. Read the unsettled volume $V_{\text{bulk}}$ to the nearest mL.
4. Weigh the cylinder + powder ($m_1$). Compute $m = m_1 - m_c$.
5. Mount the cylinder on the tap density apparatus. Apply **100 taps** (or 200 for ABC grade per IS 14609) at a constant drop height of **10 ± 0.5 mm**.
6. Read the new (settled) volume $V_{\text{tap}}$.
7. Compute $\rho_{\text{bulk}}$ and $\rho_{\text{tap}}$ using the standard formulas.

**Engineering Significance (2 marks):**
- The **bulk density** dictates the *charge mass* for a fixed extinguisher shell volume. For a 4 kg DCP extinguisher with 5 L usable volume, the powder must have $\rho_{\text{bulk}} \geq 0.8$ g/cm³ to ensure full charge within the vessel.
- A high $\rho_{\text{tap}}$ (low Carr Index) ensures the powder packs uniformly, avoiding *voids* that can cause *pressure surges* during discharge.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Moisture Content (3 marks):**

$$
M = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{120.00 - 119.65}{120.00} \times 100 = 0.2917 \,\%
$$

IS 14609 limit: $M \leq 0.25\,\%$.
Observed: $M = 0.2917\,\% > 0.25\,\%$.

**→ Moisture test: FAILS** (Batch must be re-dried or rejected). *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Particle Size Distribution (3 marks):**

$$
\% \text{ Retained on 75 µm} = \frac{6.0}{120.0} \times 100 = 5.0 \,\%
$$

$$
\% \text{ Passing 75 µm} = 100 - 5.0 = 95.0 \,\%
$$

IS 14609 requirement: at least 95 % passing 75 µm sieve.
Observed: exactly 95.0 % — *just meets* the requirement (borderline).

**→ Particle size: PASSES** (marginally). *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Overall Verdict (1 mark):**
The batch **fails overall IS 14609 compliance** because the moisture content (0.2917 %) exceeds the 0.25 % limit. The particle size is *just* within specification. Recommended action: **re-dry the powder at 105 °C for 2 h and re-test**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **Flowability Test** and **Caking Resistance Test** for DCP. State the IS limits and explain the engineering consequences if the powder fails either test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DCP sample of 100 g is tested in a flow cup funnel. The time taken for complete discharge is **3.8 seconds**. Determine the flow rate in g/min. Comment on whether the powder meets the IS 15493 minimum flowability criterion of **1200 g/min**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):** *(7 marks)*

**Flowability Test (3.5 marks):**
- **Apparatus:** Standard IS flow cup with hopper and 6 mm orifice, mounted on a rigid stand, with a receiver below.
- **Procedure:** A pre-conditioned (27 °C, 24 h) sample of **120 g of DCP** is filled into the hopper, the orifice is opened, and the time for *complete* discharge is recorded using a stopwatch.
- **Acceptance Criterion (IS 15493):** The flow rate must be ≥ 1200 g/min (i.e., 120 g discharged in ≤ 6 seconds).
- **Engineering Consequence of Failure:** A low flow rate causes *nozzle clogging*, *erratic discharge pressure*, *reduced throw range*, and *incomplete fire suppression* in real emergencies. In a stored-pressure extinguisher, partial blockage can cause *propellant gas leakage* through the siphon tube.

**Caking Resistance Test (3.5 marks):**
- **Apparatus:** Humidity chamber set to 40 °C and 75 % RH; sieve set (75 µm and coarse).
- **Procedure:** A known mass of DCP is exposed to the humid environment for a defined period (typically 24 h or accelerated 7 days). After exposure, the sample is sieved; the mass retained on a coarse sieve is the *caked fraction*.
- **Acceptance Criterion:** Caking index ≤ 5 % by mass.
- **Engineering Consequence of Failure:** Caked powder forms *lumps* that block the siphon tube and discharge hose, leading to *extinguisher failure on demand* — a critical life-safety risk.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Convert time to consistent units (2 marks):**

$$
t = 3.8 \text{ s} = \frac{3.8}{60} \text{ min} = 0.0633 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute flow rate (3 marks):**

$$
Q = \frac{m}{t} = \frac{100 \text{ g}}{0.0633 \text{ min}} = 1579 \text{ g/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with IS 15493 criterion (2 marks):**

$$
Q_{\text{observed}} = 1579 \text{ g/min} \;>\; Q_{\text{IS limit}} = 1200 \text{ g/min}
$$

**→ Flowability test: PASSES** the IS 15493 acceptance criterion by a margin of 379 g/min (≈ 31.6 % above the minimum). The powder is rated *good flowability* and is fit for use in stored-pressure extinguishers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in flow rate:** Students often leave $t$ in *seconds* but report $Q$ in *g/min* — this is the single most common error, losing 2 to 3 marks silently. Always convert time first.
> 2. **Confusing bulk density with tap density:** The cylinder is *not* tapped before reading $V_{\text{bulk}}$. Tapping gives $V_{\text{tap}}$. Mixing these up reverses the Carr Index formula sign.
> 3. **Forgetting the mass balance check in sieve analysis:** Always sum the retained masses and verify they equal the loaded mass (within 0.5 % experimental error). Examiners award 1 mark for this verification step.
> 4. **Using bare `|` symbols in formula tables** — the markdown will break, and the formula will not render. Use LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Seven physical tests** are mandatory under IS 4308 / IS 14609 / IS 15493: **Apparent Density, Sieve Analysis, Moisture, Flow, Abrasiveness, Caking, Water Repellency**.
- **Apparent (bulk) density = 0.85 – 1.05 g/cm³** is the universal acceptance window.
- **Moisture ≤ 0.25 %** — the *most frequently failing* test in industry; oven-dry at 105 °C for 2 h.
- **At least 95 % must pass through 75 µm** sieve for ABC-grade DCP.
- **Flow rate ≥ 1200 g/min** through the standard 6 mm orifice funnel.
- **Carr Index (5 – 15 %)** indicates good flowability; > 25 % = reject.
- **Abrasiveness ≤ 50 mg/kg** to protect brass valves and aluminium nozzles.
- **Caking index ≤ 5 %** after accelerated humidity exposure.
- **Test temperature is 27 °C ± 2 °C** with sample pre-conditioning of 24 h.
- All results must be recorded in a **standardized lab observation sheet** with apparatus calibration IDs — a KTU lab-report marking requirement.
- **Verdict logic** is conjunctive (AND): a single failed test rejects the *entire batch*.
- Always quote the **IS code** (4308 / 14609 / 15493) when stating limits — it carries marks in the KTU valuation key.
- Use **LaTeX-safe modulus** (`\vert`, `\mid`, `\rho`) in markdown tables; never use raw `|`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Tests on AFFF as per Relevant Indian Standard Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL507 - FIRE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Physical Tests on AFFF as per Relevant Indian Standard Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of AFFF (Aqueous Film-Forming Foam)

**AFFF (Aqueous Film-Forming Foam)** is a synthetic, fluorosurfactant-based firefighting foam concentrate designed to suppress **Class B fires** involving flammable and combustible liquids (hydrocarbons). When mixed with water in the correct proportion (typically **1%, 3%, or 6%** by volume) and aerated, it produces a foam that drains an aqueous film across the surface of the fuel, simultaneously cooling and sealing the liquid from oxygen and flame.

The relevant Indian Standard governing AFFF concentrate quality is:
- **IS 15528:2004** – *Specification for Aqueous Film-Forming Foam (AFFF) Concentrate for Fire Fighting* (formerly IS 4989 Part IV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AFFF is the **only foam** among all firefighting foams that forms a **true aqueous film** (not just a foam blanket) on the fuel surface. This film is typically **less than 0.01 mm thick** and spreads spontaneously due to positive spreading coefficient.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine dropping a small amount of **dish-washing liquid** onto a thin layer of vegetable oil floating on water. The soap spreads rapidly, pushing the oil aside and forming a thin, glistening film on the water surface. AFFF works on the **exact same principle** — but engineered to industrial firefighting scale.

The fluorosurfactants in AFFF have two ends:
- A **water-loving (hydrophilic)** tail
- An **oil-repelling (oleophobic) and water-attracting** head that refuses to mix with fuel

The result: a self-spreading, ultra-thin water film that **chokes off the oxygen supply** to the fuel and **cools the surface**, extinguishing the fire in seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams**: AFFF's film formation is governed by a **positive spreading coefficient** — this is the *single most important* theoretical concept examiners love to test.

## 1.3 Why "Physical Tests" Matter

Before AFFF is accepted for service, it must satisfy a series of **physical property tests** that confirm its identity, concentration integrity, viscosity, pH stability, density, film-forming ability, and storage life. These tests ensure that a foam stored in a fire station for 5–10 years will still knock down a hydrocarbon pool fire effectively.

The main physical tests covered under **IS 15528:2004** for AFFF concentrate are:

| Sl. No. | Physical Test | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Appearance & Colour | Visual identity check |
| 2 | Specific Gravity / Density | Concentration verification |
| 3 | Viscosity (Kinematic / Absolute) | Flow & proportioning behaviour |
| 4 | pH Value | Chemical stability & corrosion indicator |
| 5 | Freezing Point | Cold-climate operability |
| 6 | Sediment Content | Storage stability |
| 7 | Surface Tension | Film formation capability |
| 8 | Spreading Coefficient | Fuel surface film-forming ability |
| 9 | Refractive Index | Concentration check (field method) |
| 10 | Foaming Properties (Expansion & Drainage) | Foam quality verification |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry Behind AFFF – Why the Film Forms

AFFF contains three functional ingredient groups:

1. **Fluorosurfactants** (e.g., perfluoroalkyl sulfonates) — reduce surface tension of water to **~15–18 mN/m**, well below hydrocarbon fuel's surface tension (~20–25 mN/m for heptane/cyclohexane).
2. **Hydrocarbon surfactants** (e.g., SLS) — stabilise the foam bubbles and the aqueous film.
3. **Solvents & Thickeners** (Glycols, Butyl Carbitol, Xanthan gum) — lower freezing point, control viscosity, prevent stratification.

## 2.2 Operational Theory of Each Physical Test

### 2.2.1 Specific Gravity / Density Test
- **Principle**: Ratio of mass of AFFF concentrate to mass of equal volume of water at a standard temperature (usually **27 °C** or **20 °C**).
- **Instrument**: **Density bottle** (pycnometer) or **digital density meter (oscillating U-tube)**.
- **Significance**: AFFF concentrates have SG > 1.0 (typically **1.02 – 1.20**). Deviation indicates contamination or dilution.

### 2.2.2 Viscosity Test
- **Principle**: Measure resistance to flow using a **capillary viscometer** (e.g., Redwood, Saybolt, or Ubbelohde) or **rotational viscometer** (Brookfield).
- **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu$ is measured in **cSt (centistokes)** or **mm²/s**.
- **Dynamic viscosity** $\mu$ is measured in **cP (centipoise)** or **mPa·s**.
- **Significance**: Viscosity dictates **proportioning accuracy** through venturi-type inductors. Too high → poor mixing; too low → over-proportioning.

### 2.2.3 pH Test
- **Principle**: Electrometric measurement using a **calibrated pH meter** with glass electrode.
- **Acceptance Range (IS 15528)**: **pH = 6.5 to 9.5** at 27 °C.
- **Significance**: pH outside range indicates degradation, hydrolysis of fluorosurfactants, or bacterial growth.

### 2.2.4 Freezing Point Test
- **Principle**: Sample is cooled gradually in a **freezing point apparatus** until first crystal formation is observed.
- **Significance**: AFFF must remain pourable at the lowest expected storage temperature. Freezing permanently destroys foam structure (ice crystals rupture micelles).

### 2.2.5 Sediment Content Test
- **Principle**: Concentrate is centrifuged, filtered, or dissolved and the insoluble residue is weighed.
- **Acceptance Limit (IS 15528)**: **≤ 0.25 % by mass**.
- **Significance**: Excess sediment blocks proportioners, nozzles, and pipelines.

### 2.2.6 Surface Tension Test
- **Principle**: Measured by **Du Noüy ring tensiometer** or **Wilhelmy plate method**.
- **Acceptance Limit (IS 15528)**: AFFF solution (6 % in water) must have surface tension **≤ 18 mN/m at 27 °C**.
- **Significance**: A surface tension lower than the fuel's surface tension is the fundamental prerequisite for film formation.

### 2.2.7 Spreading Coefficient Test
- **Principle**: A drop of AFFF solution is placed on a hydrocarbon surface (e.g., cyclohexane). If it **spreads spontaneously** (positive coefficient), the test is passed. It can also be calculated from three interfacial tensions.
- **Significance**: A positive spreading coefficient confirms the aqueous film will self-spread and seal the fuel surface — the *defining* property of AFFF.

### 2.2.8 Refractive Index Test
- **Principle**: Measured by **Abbe refractometer** at 27 °C.
- **Significance**: Used as a **field concentration check** — refractive index is proportional to AFFF concentration in water.

### 2.2.9 Foaming Properties (Expansion & 25 % Drainage Time)
- **Principle**: Foam is generated through a **standard nozzle** at fixed pressure and the expansion ratio + drainage time are measured.
- **Acceptance Limits (IS 15528)**:
  - **Expansion ratio ≥ 5** (low expansion)
  - **25 % drainage time ≥ 2 minutes**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Unit | Acceptance (IS 15528) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Gravity | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{AFFF}}{\rho_{water}}$ | — | $\geq 1.00$ |
| 2 | Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ or $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Report actual |
| 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\text{cSt}$ or $\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$ | $\leq 200 \text{ cSt}$ @ 20 °C (typical) |
| 4 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$ | $\text{cP}$ or $\text{mPa·s}$ | Report actual |
| 5 | Surface Tension (AFFF solution) | $\sigma_{sol}$ | $\text{mN/m}$ | $\leq 18$ |
| 6 | Spreading Coefficient | $C_s = \sigma_{f/f'} - \sigma_{f/w} - \sigma_{w/f'}$ | $\text{mN/m}$ | $> 0$ (positive) |
| 7 | Expansion Ratio | $E = \dfrac{V_{foam}}{V_{solution}}$ | — | $\geq 5$ |
| 8 | 25 % Drainage Time | $t_{25}$ (time for 25 % liquid to drain) | $\text{minutes}$ | $\geq 2 \text{ min}$ |
| 9 | Refractive Index | $n = \dfrac{\sin i}{\sin r}$ | — | As reported |
| 10 | Sediment | $S = \dfrac{m_{residue}}{m_{sample}} \times 100$ | % | $\leq 0.25$ % |
| 11 | Freezing Point | $T_f$ (°C observed) | °C | $\leq -5$ °C (typical) |
| 12 | pH | $pH = -\log[H^+]$ | — | $6.5 - 9.5$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **spreading coefficient equation** is the single most tested formula in KTU for this topic. Memorize: $C_s = \sigma_{f/f'} - \sigma_{f/w} - \sigma_{w/f'}$
> Where $f$ = fuel, $f'$ = foam solution, $w$ = water.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire station inventory management**: Annual physical tests detect aged/stratified AFFF before it fails in an emergency.
- **Proportioner calibration**: Viscosity & density data feed the venturi throat calibration.
- **Forensic fire investigation**: Refractive index test confirms whether the correct concentration of foam was applied at a fire scene.
- **Environmental compliance**: Modern Indian specifications push towards **Fluorine-Free Foams (F3)** — but AFFF physical tests still form the benchmark.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Spreading Coefficient (C_s)

The **spreading coefficient** is derived from a force balance at the three-phase contact line where the foam solution, fuel, and water film meet.

### Step 1 — Define the Three Interfacial Tensions

At the contact line, three interfaces meet:

- $\sigma_{f/f'}$ = interfacial tension between **fuel and foam solution**
- $\sigma_{f/w}$ = interfacial tension between **fuel and water**
- $\sigma_{w/f'}$ = interfacial tension between **water and foam solution**

### Step 2 — Apply Young's Equation (Force Balance)

For a small virtual displacement of the contact line (spreading by an infinitesimal area $dA$):

The work done by the **fuel–water interface disappearing** = $(+\sigma_{f/w}) \, dA$

The work done to **create the new fuel–foam interface** = $(-\sigma_{f/f'}) \, dA$

The work done to **create the new foam–water interface** = $(-\sigma_{w/f'}) \, dA$

### Step 3 — Net Energy Change

$$
\Delta G = \left(\sigma_{f/f'} + \sigma_{w/f'} - \sigma_{f/w}\right) dA
$$

### Step 4 — Spreading Coefficient Definition

The **spreading coefficient** is defined as the negative of the net free energy change (a thermodynamic convention):

$$
C_s = -\frac{\Delta G}{dA} = \sigma_{f/w} - \sigma_{f/f'} - \sigma_{w/f'}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_s = \sigma_{f/w} - \sigma_{f/f'} - \sigma_{w/f'}\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Condition for Spontaneous Spreading

For the foam solution to spread spontaneously on the fuel:

$$
\Delta G < 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_s > 0
$$

### Step 6 — Verification with Typical Numerical Values

For AFFF solution on cyclohexane (typical values):

$$
\sigma_{f/w} = 50.0 \text{ mN/m}, \quad \sigma_{f/f'} = 2.5 \text{ mN/m}, \quad \sigma_{w/f'} = 1.5 \text{ mN/m}
$$

$$
C_s = 50.0 - 2.5 - 1.5 = +46.0 \text{ mN/m}
$$

Since $C_s > 0$, **the aqueous film spreads spontaneously**. ✓ (Test passes.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternate notation used in textbooks**: Some authors write $C_s = \sigma_{f/f'} - \sigma_{f/w} - \sigma_{w/f'}$ and the condition for *non-spreading* is $C_s < 0$. **Always check sign convention** in the KTU question paper. The "Bernardin-type" convention above is most widely accepted in IS 15528 derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation of Kinematic Viscosity from Capillary Flow

### Step 1 — Hagen-Poiseuille Law

For a liquid flowing under gravity through a capillary viscometer (e.g., Redwood No. 1):

$$
\mu = \dfrac{\pi \cdot R^4 \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot h \cdot t}{8 \cdot L \cdot V}
$$

Where:
- $R$ = capillary radius
- $L$ = capillary length
- $h$ = effective liquid head
- $V$ = volume of liquid collected
- $t$ = time of flow
- $\rho$ = density of liquid
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity

### Step 2 — Eliminating Instrument Constants

For a given viscometer at constant $V$, instrument constants $A$ and $B$ are defined:

$$
\nu = A \cdot t - \dfrac{B}{t}
$$

### Step 3 — Practical Form for Redwood Viscometer (in cSt)

$$
\nu = 0.0026 \, t - \dfrac{1.72}{t} \quad \text{(for Redwood No. 1, t in seconds)}
$$

### Step 4 — Dynamic Viscosity

$$
\mu = \nu \cdot \rho
$$

> Both $\nu$ and $\mu$ are temperature-dependent; hence IS 15528 mandates reporting viscosity at a **standard reference temperature of 20 °C or 27 °C**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Decision Logic for "Test Pass / Fail"

| Test | Measured Value | Spec Limit | Pass? | Probable Cause if Failed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Gravity | 1.08 | ≥ 1.00 | ✓ | — |
| Viscosity @ 20 °C | 80 cSt | ≤ 200 cSt | ✓ | — |
| pH | 7.2 | 6.5 – 9.5 | ✓ | — |
| Freezing Point | −10 °C | ≤ −5 °C | ✓ | — |
| Sediment | 0.10 % | ≤ 0.25 % | ✓ | — |
| Surface Tension (6 % sol.) | 16.0 mN/m | ≤ 18 mN/m | ✓ | — |
| Spreading Coefficient | +5.0 mN/m | > 0 | ✓ | — |
| Expansion Ratio | 6.5 | ≥ 5 | ✓ | — |
| 25 % Drainage Time | 3.5 min | ≥ 2 min | ✓ | — |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Q: An AFFF concentrate sample has mass 52.40 g filling a 50 mL density bottle at 27 °C. Tap water in the same bottle at 27 °C weighs 49.85 g. Calculate specific gravity and comment on its acceptability as per IS 15528.**

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Density of AFFF**

$$
\rho_{AFFF} = \dfrac{m_{AFFF}}{V} = \dfrac{52.40 \text{ g}}{50 \text{ mL}} = 1.048 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Density of Water (at 27 °C)**

$$
\rho_{water} = \dfrac{49.85}{50} = 0.997 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Specific Gravity**

$$
SG = \dfrac{\rho_{AFFF}}{\rho_{water}} = \dfrac{1.048}{0.997} = 1.051
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with IS 15528**

Since **SG = 1.051 ≥ 1.00**, the sample **PASSES** the specific gravity requirement. ✓

---

**Q: An AFFF 6 % solution has dynamic viscosity 2.4 cP and density 1.01 g/cm³. Calculate the kinematic viscosity in cSt. Comment on its flow behaviour for proportioning.**

### Solution:

$$
\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho} = \dfrac{2.4 \text{ cP}}{1.01 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 2.376 \text{ cSt}
$$

Since kinematic viscosity is **very low (≈ 2.4 cSt)**, the solution flows easily through a venturi proportioner with negligible pressure loss. **Suitable for proportioning.** ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram – Physical Test Workflow (AFFF Quality Control)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive AFFF Concentrate Sample] --> B[Visual Inspection: Appearance and Colour]
    B --> C[Measure Specific Gravity using Pycnometer]
    C --> D[Measure pH using Calibrated pH Meter]
    D --> E[Measure Viscosity using Capillary Viscometer]
    E --> F[Measure Surface Tension using Du Noy Ring]
    F --> G[Measure Refractive Index using Abbe Refractometer]
    G --> H[Spreading Coefficient Test on Cyclohexane]
    H --> I[Sediment Content Test via Centrifugation]
    I --> J[Freezing Point Test using Cooling Bath]
    J --> K[Generate Foam via Standard Nozzle]
    K --> L[Measure Expansion Ratio and Drainage Time]
    L --> M{All Values within IS 15528 Limits}
    M -- Yes --> N[ACCEPT Lot for Service]
    M -- No --> O[REJECT Lot and Quarantine]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inter-Relationship of AFFF Physical Tests

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Input: AFFF Concentrate]
        IN1[Concentrate Sample in Sealed Container]
    end

    subgraph ID[Identity Tests]
        T1[Appearance and Colour]
        T2[Specific Gravity]
        T3[Refractive Index]
    end

    subgraph CHEM[Chemical Stability Tests]
        T4[pH Value]
        T5[Sediment Content]
    end

    subgraph FLOW[Flow and Proportioning Tests]
        T6[Viscosity at 20 C]
        T7[Freezing Point]
    end

    subgraph FIRE[Fire Performance Indicator Tests]
        T8[Surface Tension of Solution]
        T9[Spreading Coefficient]
        T10[Expansion Ratio]
        T11[25 Percent Drainage Time]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Final Decision]
        D1{All Pass as per IS 15528}
        D2[Accept for Fire Service Use]
        D3[Reject and Send Back to Supplier]
    end

    IN1 --> ID
    IN1 --> CHEM
    IN1 --> FLOW
    IN1 --> FIRE
    ID --> D1
    CHEM --> D1
    FLOW --> D1
    FIRE --> D1
    D1 -- Yes --> D2
    D1 -- No --> D3
```

## 4.3 Spreading Film Schematic (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AIR[Air above fuel]
        A1[Atmosphere]
    end

    subgraph FOAM[Foam blanket layer]
        F1[Bubble 1]
        F2[Bubble 2]
        F3[Bubble 3]
    end

    subgraph FILM[Thin aqueous film - less than 0.01 mm]
        FL1[Film spreads on fuel surface]
    end

    subgraph FUEL[Hydrocarbon fuel layer]
        FU1[Flammable liquid e.g. cyclohexane or heptane]
    end

    A1 --> F1
    A1 --> F2
    A1 --> F3
    F1 --> FL1
    F2 --> FL1
    F3 --> FL1
    FL1 --> FU1
```

## 4.4 Test Apparatus Layout (Tabular Schematic)

| Test | Apparatus Used | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Gravity | 50 mL Pycnometer, Analytical Balance | IS 15528 Cl. 5.2 |
| Viscosity | Redwood Viscometer No. 1 / Brookfield | IS 15528 Cl. 5.3 |
| pH | Digital pH Meter (Glass Electrode) | IS 15528 Cl. 5.4 |
| Freezing Point | Freezing Point Apparatus with Cooling Bath | IS 15528 Cl. 5.5 |
| Sediment | Centrifuge, Gooch Crucible, Oven | IS 15528 Cl. 5.6 |
| Surface Tension | Du Noüy Ring Tensiometer | IS 15528 Cl. 5.7 |
| Spreading Coefficient | Petri Dish with Cyclohexane, Dropper | IS 15528 Cl. 5.8 |
| Refractive Index | Abbe Refractometer | IS 15528 Cl. 5.9 |
| Foam Expansion & Drainage | Standard Foam Nozzle, Collecting Cylinder | IS 15528 Cl. 5.10 |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define AFFF. Why is it considered the most effective foam for hydrocarbon fires?**

**Model Answer:**

AFFF (Aqueous Film-Forming Foam) is a synthetic fluorosurfactant-based foam concentrate that, when proportioned with water (typically at 1 %, 3 %, or 6 %) and aerated, forms both a foam blanket and a **self-spreading aqueous film** over the surface of a hydrocarbon fuel.

It is the most effective foam for hydrocarbon fires because:

1. The fluorosurfactants **reduce the surface tension of water** below that of the fuel, allowing the drained solution to form a thin film.
2. The film **spreads spontaneously** with a **positive spreading coefficient**, rapidly sealing the entire fuel surface.
3. The film **cools the fuel** (latent heat of vaporisation) and **excludes oxygen**, achieving faster knockdown than protein or AFFF-free foams.

**(3 Marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six physical tests conducted on AFFF concentrate as per IS 15528.**

**Model Answer:**

As per IS 15528, the six physical tests conducted on AFFF concentrate are:

1. Appearance and Colour
2. Specific Gravity (Density)
3. Viscosity (Kinematic / Dynamic)
4. pH Value
5. Freezing Point
6. Sediment Content

(Other acceptable tests: Surface Tension, Spreading Coefficient, Refractive Index, Foam Expansion Ratio, 25 % Drainage Time.)

**(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Define the term *spreading coefficient* of an AFFF solution on a hydrocarbon fuel. Derive the expression for the spreading coefficient using interfacial tensions and state the condition for spontaneous film formation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An AFFF concentrate sample has a mass of 41.30 g in a 40 mL density bottle at 27 °C. Distilled water in the same bottle weighs 39.90 g. Calculate the specific gravity and determine whether the sample conforms to IS 15528. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (a)**

**Definition:** The spreading coefficient ($C_s$) is a thermodynamic measure that indicates whether an AFFF solution will spread spontaneously over a hydrocarbon fuel surface. It is the net decrease in surface free energy when the foam solution displaces the fuel–water interface.

**Derivation:**

Consider the three interfaces meeting at the three-phase contact line:

- Fuel–Water interface: tension $\sigma_{f/w}$
- Fuel–Foam interface: tension $\sigma_{f/f'}$
- Water–Foam interface: tension $\sigma_{w/f'}$

When the foam solution spreads over the fuel by an infinitesimal area $dA$:

- The fuel–water interface of area $dA$ is **destroyed** → energy gain = $+\sigma_{f/w} \, dA$
- A fuel–foam interface of area $dA$ is **created** → energy cost = $-\sigma_{f/f'} \, dA$
- A water–foam interface of area $dA$ is **created** → energy cost = $-\sigma_{w/f'} \, dA$

Net change in surface free energy:

$$
\Delta G = \left(\sigma_{f/f'} + \sigma_{w/f'} - \sigma_{f/w}\right) dA
$$

The **spreading coefficient** is defined as the negative of this free-energy change per unit area:

$$
C_s = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{dA} = \sigma_{f/w} - \sigma_{f/f'} - \sigma_{w/f'}
$$

**Condition for spontaneous film formation:**

$$
C_s > 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \sigma_{f/w} > \sigma_{f/f'} + \sigma_{w/f'}
$$

This means the fuel–water interfacial tension must exceed the sum of the two newly created interfacial tensions. AFFF's fluorosurfactants ensure this by **drastically reducing** $\sigma_{f/f'}$ and $\sigma_{w/f'}$.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (b)**

**Given:**
- Mass of AFFF sample in 40 mL bottle: $m_{AFFF} = 41.30$ g
- Mass of distilled water in same bottle: $m_{water} = 39.90$ g

**Step 1 — Density of AFFF concentrate**

$$
\rho_{AFFF} = \dfrac{m_{AFFF}}{V} = \dfrac{41.30 \text{ g}}{40 \text{ mL}} = 1.0325 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Density of water at 27 °C**

$$
\rho_{water} = \dfrac{39.90 \text{ g}}{40 \text{ mL}} = 0.9975 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Specific Gravity**

$$
SG = \dfrac{\rho_{AFFF}}{\rho_{water}} = \dfrac{1.0325}{0.9975} = 1.0351
$$

**Step 4 — Acceptance per IS 15528**

The minimum specified SG is **≥ 1.00**.

Since $SG = 1.0351 \geq 1.00$, the sample **CONFORMS** to IS 15528 and is acceptable for service. ✓

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the procedure for the **surface tension test** of an AFFF solution using the **Du Noüy ring tensiometer**. State the acceptance limit as per IS 15528. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 6 % AFFF solution has dynamic viscosity 2.4 cP and density 1.01 g/cm³. Calculate its kinematic viscosity. State the significance of viscosity in foam proportioning. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (a)**

**Apparatus:** Du Noüy Ring Tensiometer (Platinum-Iridium ring), sample beaker, AFFF 6 % solution (prepared by mixing 6 mL concentrate in 94 mL distilled water), microbalance / force transducer.

**Procedure:**

1. Clean the platinum-iridium ring by rinsing in acetone, then flame it to red-hot in a Bunsen burner to remove organic contaminants. Allow to cool.
2. Place the AFFF 6 % solution in the sample beaker on the tensiometer platform at **27 °C ± 1 °C**.
3. Raise the platform slowly until the ring is just submerged in the liquid.
4. Slowly lower the liquid level (or raise the beam) so that the ring pulls a thin film of liquid upward.
5. The torsion wire applies a counter-force. Record the **maximum force** $F_{max}$ (in dynes or mN) just before the film ruptures.
6. Calculate surface tension:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{F_{max}}{2 L} \times C
$$

   where:
   - $L$ = circumference of the ring (typically 6.0 cm)
   - $C$ = correction factor (Zuidema-Waters or Harkins-Jordan correction)

7. Repeat 3 times and take the average.

**Acceptance limit as per IS 15528:** AFFF 6 % solution must have surface tension **≤ 18 mN/m at 27 °C**.

**Block Schematic of Test Setup:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Platinum Ring submerged in AFFF solution] --> B[Apply Vertical Pull via Torsion Wire]
    B --> C[Thin liquid film forms and stretches]
    C --> D[Measure Maximum Force before film rupture]
    D --> E[Apply Correction Factor]
    E --> F[Compute Surface Tension sigma]
    F --> G{sigma less than or equal to 18 mN per m}
    G -- Yes --> H[Test PASSES]
    G -- No --> I[Test FAILS: Reject concentrate]
```

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (b)**

**Given:**
- Dynamic viscosity $\mu = 2.4$ cP
- Density $\rho = 1.01$ g/cm³

**Step 1 — Kinematic Viscosity**

$$
\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho} = \dfrac{2.4 \text{ cP}}{1.01 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 2.376 \text{ cSt}
$$

**Step 2 — Significance in Foam Proportioning**

1. **Venturi Proportioning:** AFFF concentrate is inducted into the water stream by a **pressure differential** across a venturi throat. The viscosity must be low enough to flow freely through the induction port, otherwise the venturi under-proportions → foam is too weak → fire suppression fails.
2. **Pressure Loss in Pipelines:** High-viscosity concentrate causes excessive friction loss in long pipe runs from storage tank to the fire engine.
3. **Cold-Weather Operation:** Viscosity rises sharply as temperature falls. IS 15528 specifies a **maximum viscosity at 20 °C** to ensure winter operability in Indian climatic conditions (especially North Indian stations).
4. **Nozzle Atomisation:** Low viscosity enables fine droplet formation in the foam nozzle, producing uniform bubbles and stable foam.

**(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Sign convention error in spreading coefficient** — Writing $C_s = \sigma_{f/f'} - \sigma_{f/w} - \sigma_{w/f'}$ and then stating it must be **positive** (this combination is the *non-spreading* form). Always verify with your textbook's sign convention.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (cSt vs. cP, mN/m vs. N/m, g/cm³ vs. kg/m³). KTU examiners deduct **½ mark per unit error**.
> 3. **Confusing dynamic and kinematic viscosity** — KTU frequently asks: "If dynamic viscosity = 5 cP and density = 1.05 g/cm³, find kinematic viscosity." Many students divide the wrong way.
> 4. **Skipping the "comment on acceptance" step** in numericals — IS 15528 acceptance limit must be **explicitly stated and compared**.
> 5. **Writing "AFFF" without expansion** in the first occurrence of the answer — KTU deducts ½ mark for unexpanded acronyms in formal answers.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **AFFF** = Aqueous Film-Forming Foam — synthetic, fluorosurfactant-based, for **Class B hydrocarbon fires**.
- ✅ Relevant Indian Standard = **IS 15528:2004**.
- ✅ Standard proportioning rates: **1 %, 3 %, 6 %** by volume of concentrate in water.
- ✅ Film thickness on fuel: **< 0.01 mm**, spreads due to **positive spreading coefficient**.
- ✅ Spreading coefficient formula: $C_s = \sigma_{f/w} - \sigma_{f/f'} - \sigma_{w/f'}$ → must be **> 0**.
- ✅ Specific gravity of AFFF concentrate: **typically 1.02 – 1.20** (IS 15528 minimum ≥ 1.00).
- ✅ pH range: **6.5 – 9.5** at 27 °C.
- ✅ Surface tension of 6 % AFFF solution: **≤ 18 mN/m** at 27 °C.
- ✅ Expansion ratio: **≥ 5**; 25 % drainage time: **≥ 2 minutes**.
- ✅ Sediment: **≤ 0.25 %** by mass.
- ✅ Kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu / \rho$; Dynamic viscosity $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$.
- ✅ Key instruments: **Pycnometer, Redwood Viscometer, pH Meter, Du Noüy Ring Tensiometer, Abbe Refractometer, Centrifuge**.
- ✅ Cyclohexane is the standard test fuel for spreading coefficient / film formation test.
- ✅ **Failing any one physical test = batch rejection** under IS 15528 QC protocol.
- ✅ For KTU Part B (14 marks), always present: **Principle → Procedure → Formula → Sample Calculation → Acceptance Comment**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU tip**: If the question is "Discuss the physical tests on AFFF", structure the answer as: *Introduction → List of tests → Pick 4 most important (SG, Viscosity, pH, Spreading Coefficient) → Derivation of $C_s$ → Significance*. This single structure has fetched 13–14 marks in the past three KTU cycles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Tests on Portable Fire Extinguishers - AFFF, Water and DCP Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL507 - FIRE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Tests on Portable Fire Extinguishers - AFFF, Water and DCP Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Portable Fire Extinguisher** is a self-contained, hand-held, pressure-charged, fire-suppression device designed to combat incipient-stage fires (Class A, B, or C) by discharging a specific extinguishing medium — Water, Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF), or Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) — through a controlled nozzle under the action of stored propellant pressure (typically **Nitrogen (N₂) at 10–15 bar** or CO₂ cartridge).

**Performance testing** of portable fire extinguishers is a mandatory quality-assurance procedure governed by **BIS standards IS 15683:2018** (general requirements), **IS 2546:1994** (Powder Type), **IS 6234:1986** (Foam Type) and **IS 903:1993** (Water Type). The tests quantitatively validate the extinguisher's ability to:

1. Discharge its entire contents within a stipulated **discharge time (T_d)**
2. Achieve a minimum **discharge rate (R_d)**
3. Project the agent to a minimum **throw range (L_t)**
4. Successfully extinguish a standard test fire of pre-defined **fire rating (e.g., 2A, 21B)**
5. Resist **re-ignition** over a defined post-discharge observation period

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping (CO):** This experiment directly satisfies **CO2 – "Conduct performance tests on portable extinguishers and interpret results as per BIS standards"** at **Bloom's Level 3 (Apply)** and **Level 4 (Analyse)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Firefighter's Water Gun"**
>
> Imagine a pressurized **super-soaker water gun**. Before you buy it, the manufacturer must prove three things: (1) it can squirt for at least a certain *time* (battery life = discharge time), (2) it can squirt *fast enough* to knock down a target (flow rate), and (3) it can squirt *far enough* to hit from a safe distance (throw range). Now, instead of water, the gun shoots foam, powder, or water to put out real fires. The "performance test" is exactly this — the manufacturer lights a standard fire, points the extinguisher, and checks if it can (a) put the fire out completely, (b) stay out, and (c) project its contents over the right distance. **If any check fails, the extinguisher fails the test.**

## 1.3 Classification of Test Fires (Foundation Concept)

| Fire Class | Fuel Type | Extinguishing Medium Tested Today |
|------------|----------|-----------------------------------|
| **Class A** | Wood, paper, cloth (solid combustibles) | **Water-type extinguisher** |
| **Class B** | Petrol, diesel, kerosene (flammable liquids) | **AFFF (Foam) & DCP** |
| **Class C** | Flammable gases (LPG, methane) | **DCP only** |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Test Temperature:** **27 °C ± 5 °C** (ambient)
- **Standard Propellant Pressure:** **10 bar to 15 bar** (N₂)
- **Standard Fire Pan:** Mild steel, water-tight, depth ≈ **200 mm**
- **Discharge Time Tolerance:** Must be **≥ 85%** of rated value
- **Re-ignition Observation Period:** **5 minutes** post-extinguishment for Class B
- **Units used:** Discharge rate in **kg/s** or **L/min**; throw range in **metres (m)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Principle Behind the Test

The extinguishing action of each medium follows a different physical-chemical mechanism, which is why performance tests must be conducted separately for each type.

### 2.1.1 Water-Type Extinguisher (Class A Fires)

Water extinguishes fire via the **cooling mechanism** — it absorbs the latent heat of vaporisation.

$$Q_{abs} = m_w \cdot L_v$$

where:
- $Q_{abs}$ = heat absorbed from fire (kJ)
- $m_w$ = mass of water discharged (kg)
- $L_v$ = **latent heat of vaporisation of water = 2260 kJ/kg**

> [!NOTE]
> **Cooling principle:** 1 litre of water absorbs ~2260 kJ on vaporisation. This is why water is the most effective agent for Class A (solid) fires.

### 2.1.2 AFFF Extinguisher (Class B Fires)

AFFF works on **three simultaneous mechanisms**:

1. **Foam blanket (smothering)** – cuts off O₂ supply
2. **Aqueous film formation** – a thin (~0.01 mm) film spreads on the fuel surface, suppressing vapour release
3. **Cooling** – water content of foam provides minor cooling

The foam expansion ratio is critical:

$$\text{Expansion Ratio} = \frac{V_{foam}}{V_{solution}}$$

Typical AFFF expansion: **6:1 to 8:1** (low-expansion foam).

### 2.1.3 DCP Extinguisher (Class B / C Fires)

DCP (typically **Monoammonium Phosphate – NH₄H₂PO₄ – 75% MAP**) extinguishes by:

1. **Chain-breaking reaction** – MAP interferes with the free-radical chain reaction in the flame
2. **Smothering** – powder cloud displaces O₂ locally
3. **Thermal cooling** – minor heat sink from powder particles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> DCP is the **only multi-class extinguisher** (A, B, C, and even Electrical up to 1000 V at 1 m distance) covered in this experiment.

## 2.2 Performance Test Parameters (Step-by-Step Logic)

### Step 1 — Pre-Test Inspection
- Check hydrostatic pressure (test pressure = **1.5 × working pressure**)
- Verify safety pin, nozzle, pressure gauge
- Confirm extinguisher is **fully charged** to rated capacity

### Step 2 — Discharge Time Measurement
Operate the extinguisher on a calibrated test fire and record time from **first discharge to last discharge**.

### Step 3 — Discharge Rate Calculation
Mass discharged divided by discharge time.

### Step 4 — Throw Range Measurement
Distance from nozzle tip to the **furthest continuous discharge landing point** on the floor.

### Step 5 — Fire Rating Test (Extinguishing Capacity)
The extinguisher is tested on a **standard fire tray** loaded with a pre-defined quantity of fuel. Fire rating is expressed as:
- **Class A:** 1A = 13.5 kg wood crib; 2A = 27 kg; 3A = 40.5 kg
- **Class B:** 8B, 13B, 21B, 34B, 55B, 70B, 89B (square feet of heptane pool fire)
  - 1B = **0.465 m²** of fuel surface

### Step 6 — Re-ignition Test
After successful extinguishment, the fire must **not re-ignite within 5 minutes**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Numerical Value / Unit | Applicable To |
|---|---------------------|---------------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Discharge Rate | $R_d = \dfrac{m}{T_d}$ | kg/s or L/min | All three types |
| 2 | Heat Absorbed (Cooling) | $Q = m \cdot L_v$ | $L_v = 2260$ **kJ/kg** | Water |
| 3 | Foam Expansion Ratio | $E_r = \dfrac{V_{foam}}{V_{sol}}$ | **6:1 to 8:1** | AFFF |
| 4 | Throw Range | $L_t = v_{exit} \cdot t_{flight}$ | metres (m) | All three types |
| 5 | Pressure Drop in Hose | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ | Pascal (Pa) | All three |
| 6 | Fire Rating Area | $A_f = N_B \times 0.465$ | m² | Class B (DCP/AFFF) |
| 7 | Re-ignition Time (Criteria) | $T_{re} \geq 5$ **min** | seconds | AFFF, DCP |
| 8 | Propellant Pressure (Standard) | $P_{prop}$ | **10–15 bar** | All three |
| 9 | Discharge Efficiency | $\eta_d = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{rated}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | All three |
| 10 | Hydrostatic Test Pressure | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{work}$ | bar | All three |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Insurance Compliance:** Fire extinguishers must be performance-certified before installation in commercial buildings, per **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee)** guidelines and **IS 15683**.
- **IS Code Compliance:** Used by **BIS testing labs (ETDC)** and BIS-approved private labs for product certification.
- **Industrial QA/QC:** Every batch-manufactured extinguisher is statistically sampled and tested (sampling per IS 2546 Annex C).
- **Fire Investigation:** Performance test data forms the baseline for forensic determination of whether an extinguisher was *fit for service* during a real fire incident.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Discharge Rate of a 9 L Water Extinguisher

> **Problem Statement:** A 9-litre water-type portable fire extinguisher (stored pressure type) is performance-tested in the lab. The extinguisher is fully charged to its rated capacity. When discharged through its standard nozzle, the **total discharge time is measured as 42 seconds**. The water collected in a calibrated tank weighs **8.91 kg** (assume density of water $\rho_w = 1000$ **kg/m³**). Calculate the **discharge rate (R_d)** in **L/min** and verify it meets the BIS minimum requirement.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given data:**

Rated capacity: $V_{rated} = 9$ L
Actual mass discharged: $m_{actual} = 8.91$ kg
Actual volume discharged: $V_{actual} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{8.91}{1000} = 0.00891$ m³ $= 8.91$ L
Discharge time: $T_d = 42$ s

**Step 2 — Apply the discharge-rate formula:**

$$R_d = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{T_d}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$R_d = \dfrac{8.91 \text{ kg}}{42 \text{ s}}$$

$$R_d = 0.2121 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to L/min for KTU compliance (1 L = 1 kg for water, 1 min = 60 s):**

$$R_d = 0.2121 \times 60 = 12.73 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 5 — Verification against IS 903:1993 minimum discharge rate:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BIS Minimum Discharge Rate for 9 L water extinguisher = 11.4 L/min**
> 
> Computed: 12.73 L/min **> 11.4 L/min → TEST PASSES ✓**

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fire Rating Verification of a 4 kg DCP Extinguisher

> **Problem Statement:** A 4 kg DCP (ABC type) extinguisher is tested on a heptane pool fire. The test pan area is **0.93 m²** (i.e., 10 sq.ft. heptane fuel surface). The extinguisher extinguishes the fire in **8 seconds** with a **discharge time of 12 seconds** and shows **no re-ignition for 6 minutes**. Determine: (a) Fire rating, (b) Discharge rate, (c) Pass/Fail status as per **IS 2546**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Determine Fire Rating:**

$$N_B = \dfrac{A_f}{0.465 \text{ m}^2}$$

$$N_B = \dfrac{0.93}{0.465} = 2.0$$

Since the fire rating is in integer multiples of 1B, the rating rounds to the **next lower standard size**.

$$N_{B,\text{tested}} = 2B$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Discharge Rate:**

$$R_d = \dfrac{m_{DCP}}{T_d} = \dfrac{4 \text{ kg}}{12 \text{ s}} = 0.333 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$R_d = 0.333 \times 60 = 20 \text{ kg/min}$$

**Step 3 — Check against IS 2546 minimums for 4 kg DCP:**

> [!NOTE]
> **BIS Minimums:** Discharge rate ≥ **0.25 kg/s** (15 kg/min) | Re-ignition time ≥ **5 min** | Throw range ≥ **4 m**

| Parameter | Computed | IS 2546 Min | Status |
|-----------|----------|-------------|--------|
| Discharge Rate | 20 kg/min | 15 kg/min | **PASS** ✓ |
| Re-ignition Time | 6 min | 5 min | **PASS** ✓ |
| Fire Rating | 2B | 2B (rated) | **PASS** ✓ |

**Result:** The extinguisher **conforms to IS 2546 specifications**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — AFFF Foam Expansion & 25% Drainage Time

> **Problem Statement:** In an AFFF performance test, **200 mL of AFFF concentrate (3% concentration)** is mixed with **6 L of water** to form the foam solution. The foam is generated and collected. The collected foam volume is **45 L** after 1 minute. After **5 minutes**, the foam has drained **25% of its liquid content**. Calculate: (a) Foam Expansion Ratio, (b) Verify 25% drainage time meets **IS 6234** minimum.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Foam Expansion Ratio:**

$$E_r = \dfrac{V_{foam}}{V_{solution}}$$

$$E_r = \dfrac{45 \text{ L}}{6.2 \text{ L}} = 7.26$$

**Step 2 — Compare with IS 6234 standard:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 6234 specifies AFFF expansion ratio between 6:1 and 8:1.**
> 
> Computed 7.26 → **WITHIN SPECIFICATION** ✓

**Step 3 — Drainage Time Verification:**

25% drainage time = **5 min** ≥ IS 6234 minimum of **2.5 min** → **PASS** ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Extinguisher

| Hazard Scenario | Recommended Extinguisher | Why? |
|-----------------|--------------------------|------|
| Office (paper, wood) | **Water (9 L, Class A)** | Highest cooling capacity |
| Petrol pump forecourt | **AFFF (9 L, Class B)** | Foam floats on petrol, prevents vapour release |
| LPG cylinder storage | **DCP (4 kg, ABC)** | Only agent effective on Class C gas fires |
| Electrical panel room | **DCP (ABC)** or **CO₂** | Non-conductive, non-damaging to electronics |
| Kitchen (oil fires) | **AFFF** or **Wet Chemical** | Prevents re-ignition of oil |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Flow of the Performance Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Charged Extinguisher] --> B[Pre-Test Visual Inspection]
    B --> B1{Hydrostatic Test Passed?}
    B1 -->|No| B2[Reject - Return to Manufacturer]
    B1 -->|Yes| C[Weigh Extinguisher m1]
    C --> D[Light Standard Test Fire]
    D --> D1{Class A Wood Crib}
    D --> D2{Class B Heptane Pool}
    D --> D3{Class C Gas Burner}
    D1 --> E[Discharge Extinguisher]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Record Discharge Time Td]
    F --> G[Record Throw Range Lt]
    G --> H[Weigh Empty Extinguisher m2]
    H --> I[Calculate Discharge Rate Rd]
    I --> J[Observe Re-Ignition Period Tre]
    J --> K{All Parameters Meet IS Standard?}
    K -->|Yes| L[PASS - Issue Certificate]
    K -->|No| M[FAIL - Reject Batch]
    L --> N[End]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Extinguishing Mechanism Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WaterType[Water Type Extinguisher]
        W1[Water Stored at 10 to 15 bar] --> W2[Discharge via Nozzle]
        W2 --> W3[Cools Fire Below Ignition Temp]
        W3 --> W4[Class A Fires Only]
    end
    subgraph AFFFType[AFFF Foam Type Extinguisher]
        F1[Foam Concentrate and Water Mix] --> F2[Generate Foam via Propellant]
        F2 --> F3[Foam Blanket Smothers Fire]
        F2 --> F4[Aqueous Film Suppresses Vapour]
        F3 --> F5[Class B Fires]
        F4 --> F5
    end
    subgraph DCPType[DCP Type Extinguisher]
        D1[ABC Powder in Container] --> D2[Propellant N2 Expels Powder]
        D2 --> D3[Chain Breaking Reaction in Flame]
        D2 --> D4[Smothers Local Oxygen]
        D3 --> D5[Class A B C and Electrical]
        D4 --> D5
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Stored-Pressure Extinguisher

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Propellant Gas N2 10 to 15 bar] --> P2[Pressurised Vessel Body]
    P2 --> P3[Extinguishing Agent Water AFFF or DCP]
    P3 --> P4[Dip Tube]
    P4 --> P5[Discharge Valve and Lever]
    P5 --> P6[Hose and Nozzle Assembly]
    P6 --> P7[Fire Zone]
    P2 --> P8[Pressure Gauge Indicator]
    P5 --> P9[Safety Pin and Tamper Seal]
```

## 4.4 Standard Test Fire Pan Dimensions (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Heptane Fuel 25 mm Depth] --> F2[Mild Steel Pan 200 mm Wall]
    F2 --> F3[Pan Area Determined by Fire Rating N_B]
    F3 --> F4[Ignition Source at Pan Centre]
    F4 --> F5[Pre-Burn Period 60 seconds]
    F5 --> F6[Operator Stands 1.5 m from Pan Edge]
    F6 --> F7[Discharge Begins]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above schematics represent the **functional flow of the experiment** as performed in the PCSFL507 Fire Engineering Laboratory. The exact pan geometry is defined by **IS 2546 Part 2 and IS 15683 Annex E**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**List any three performance parameters that are evaluated during the testing of a portable fire extinguisher as per IS 15683.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three mandatory performance parameters are:

1. **Discharge Time (T_d):** The time taken to release the entire extinguishing agent, must be within ±10% of rated value.
2. **Discharge Rate (R_d):** The mass of agent released per unit time, computed as $R_d = m/T_d$; must meet IS-minimums (e.g., 11.4 L/min for 9 L water type).
3. **Fire Rating / Extinguishing Capacity:** The size of standard test fire that the extinguisher can successfully put out (e.g., 2A, 21B).

*(Each parameter carries 1 mark — full 3 marks if all three are correctly stated with units.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**Why is Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) called a "multi-class" extinguishing agent? Name the chemical used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

DCP is called a multi-class agent because a single ABC-type DCP extinguisher can extinguish **Class A, Class B, Class C fires, and electrical fires up to 1000 V (at 1 m distance)** — this is the broadest fire-class coverage of any portable extinguisher.

The active chemical used is **Monoammonium Phosphate (NH₄H₂PO₄)** at 75% concentration, with the balance 25% being additives (silicones for water repellence, anti-caking agents, and colorants). **(2 marks for explanation + 1 mark for chemical name.)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with **sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** out of an internal choice of two.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a stored-pressure type portable fire extinguisher**. Enumerate the BIS standards applicable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step procedure for conducting the performance test of a 9 L AFFF foam type fire extinguisher** on a Class B fire, including all observations and BIS compliance criteria. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Construction (Block Diagram):**

A stored-pressure extinguisher consists of:

- **Cylindrical pressure vessel** made of mild steel / stainless steel, hydrostatically tested to **1.5 × working pressure**.
- **Propellant gas (N₂)** pre-charged at **10–15 bar** occupying the ullage space above the extinguishing agent.
- **Dip tube** running from the head valve down to the bottom of the vessel — carries the agent up during discharge.
- **Head valve assembly** with a spring-loaded lever, safety pin, and pressure gauge.
- **Discharge hose** (length 400–600 mm for hand-held units) terminating in a **nozzle / foam-making branch pipe**.
- **Pressure gauge** indicating readiness (green zone = operational, red zone = requires recharging).
- **Safety pin and tamper seal** to prevent accidental discharge.

**Working Principle (3-step sequence):**

1. Operator pulls the safety pin and squeezes the discharge lever.
2. The propellant N₂ pushes the agent up through the dip tube, into the hose, and out through the nozzle.
3. For AFFF: the agent passes through a foam-mixing chamber where air is aspirated, generating expanded foam.

**Applicable BIS Standards (1 mark):**
- **IS 15683:2018** — General requirements for all portable extinguishers
- **IS 6234:1986** — Foam type (AFFF)
- **IS 903:1993** — Water type
- **IS 2546:1994** — Dry powder type

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Performance Test Procedure — 9 L AFFF Extinguisher on Class B Fire:**

**Step 1 — Pre-Test Preparations (1 mark):**
- Verify extinguisher is fully charged (pressure gauge in green zone).
- Measure initial mass $m_1$ on a calibrated weighing scale (capacity ≥ 50 kg, least count 0.01 kg).
- Prepare a **heptane pool fire** in a mild steel pan of area corresponding to the rated fire size.
  - For a 9 L AFFF extinguisher rated **21B**, pan area = $21 \times 0.465 = 9.765$ m² (use a 3.2 m × 3.2 m pan if at full scale; in lab, a 1 m² reduced-scale pan is acceptable with proportional fuel load).

**Step 2 — Pre-Burn (1 mark):**
- Ignite the heptane and allow a **60-second pre-burn period** to establish steady-state combustion.

**Step 3 — Discharge Operation (1.5 marks):**
- The trained operator stands **1.5 m from the pan edge** and discharges the extinguisher using sweeping motion.
- Record the **discharge start time** $t_1$ and **discharge end time** $t_2$ using a digital stopwatch.
- Calculate discharge time: $T_d = t_2 - t_1$
- Record the **throw range** $L_t$ as the horizontal distance from nozzle tip to the furthest continuous landing point of foam on the floor.

**Step 4 — Observations Recorded (1.5 marks):**

| Observation | Symbol | Unit |
|-------------|--------|------|
| Initial mass | $m_1$ | kg |
| Final mass (after discharge) | $m_2$ | kg |
| Discharge time | $T_d$ | s |
| Throw range | $L_t$ | m |
| Re-ignition time | $T_{re}$ | min |
| Extinguishing time | $t_{ext}$ | s |

**Step 5 — Calculations (1 mark):**

$$R_d = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{T_d} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$E_r = \dfrac{V_{foam}}{V_{solution}}$$

**Step 6 — Pass/Fail Criteria (1 mark):**

| Parameter | IS 6234 Requirement | Test Result |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------|
| Discharge Time | 30–60 s for 9 L | ___ s |
| Discharge Rate | ≥ 1.5 L/min | ___ L/min |
| Throw Range | ≥ 4 m | ___ m |
| Re-ignition | ≥ 5 min | ___ min |

If all four parameters are met, the extinguisher **passes the performance test** and is issued a **BIS certification mark**.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Compare the **extinguishing mechanism** of Water, AFFF, and DCP type portable extinguishers in a **tabular format with examples of fires each one is suitable for.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **4 kg DCP extinguisher** is tested on a heptane fire of pan area **0.93 m²**. The initial mass is **6.20 kg**, final mass after discharge is **2.15 kg**, discharge time is **11 seconds**, and re-ignition does not occur for **6 minutes**. Determine the **fire rating, discharge rate**, and state whether the extinguisher **passes IS 2546** or not. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Feature | Water Type | AFFF Type | DCP (ABC) Type |
|---------|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| **Active Agent** | Water (H₂O) | 3% AFFF concentrate + 97% water | Monoammonium Phosphate (75%) |
