**Step 1: Apply the empirical relation** $f_c = 0.7R - 5$ at each level:

**Level 0–1 m (R = 32):**
$$f_{c,1} = 0.7 \times 32 - 5 = 22.4 - 5 = 17.4\ \text{MPa}$$

**Level 1–2 m (R = 28):**
$$f_{c,2} = 0.7 \times 28 - 5 = 19.6 - 5 = 14.6\ \text{MPa}$$

**Level 2–3 m (R = 22):**
$$f_{c,3} = 0.7 \times 22 - 5 = 15.4 - 5 = 10.4\ \text{MPa}$$

**Level 3–4 m (R = 15):**
$$f_{c,4} = 0.7 \times 15 - 5 = 10.5 - 5 = 5.5\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2: Tabulate the Results**

| Height (m) | R | $f_c$ (MPa) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0–1 | 32 | 17.4 | Moderate damage |
| 1–2 | 28 | 14.6 | Significant damage |
| 2–3 | 22 | 10.4 | Severe damage |
| 3–4 | 15 | 5.5 | **Critical – near complete loss** |

**Step 3: Identify the Most Affected Zone**

The **3–4 m zone** has the lowest R-value (15) and lowest $f_c$ (5.5 MPa), indicating the **most fire-severely damaged region**. This zone is the **seat of the fire source** (likely) and requires:
1. **Core testing** for confirmation (minimum 3 cores per IS 516).
2. **Possibly jacketing** with fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) or additional reinforced concrete.
3. **Engineering judgment** on whether the column can be repaired or must be **demolished and reconstructed**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PCSFL507 Lab Exam:**

1. **Forgetting to discard max/min readings** → loses 1 mark in observation table.
2. **Using the control specimen's calibration directly on heated specimens** → conceptually wrong; mention "calibration curve shift with temperature" to recover marks.
3. **Not reporting the hammer Type, impact energy, and orientation** → required in any calculation, loses 1 mark.
4. **Confusing Rebound Number with compressive strength** → R is dimensionless; $f_c$ is in MPa.
5. **Skipping visual observations (color, cracks, spalling)** → these are 2-mark items in viva.
6. **Reporting a single reading** instead of mean of 8 → fundamental methodological error.
7. **Using the hammer on a wet surface** → gives artificially high R, invalidates test.
8. **Quenching hot specimens in water** → induces thermal shock and invalidates the entire experiment.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 PCSFL507 Lab Exam**

- **Test Name**: Schmidt Rebound Hammer Test (NDT)
- **IS Code**: IS 13311 (Part 2) : 1992
- **Hammer Type**: Type N (impact energy 2.207 N·m) – most commonly used
- **Specimen**: 150 mm cube, cured 28 days
- **Heat Treatment**: 200, 400, 600, 800 °C, 2-hour soak, 5 °C/min ramp
- **Cooling**: Natural air cooling (NEVER water quenching)
- **Readings per specimen**: 10 (discard max & min; average 8)
- **Spacing**: ≥ 30 mm between impact points; ≥ 30 mm from edges
- **Orientation correction**: Use chart; upward test underestimates R
- **Temperature Trend**: R **decreases** with increasing T (35 → 30 → 25 → 17 → 9)
- **Key Microstructural Changes**:
  - 400 °C → C-S-H dehydration
  - 600 °C → Ca(OH)₂ decomposition
  - 800 °C → CaCO₃ decarbonation
- **Strength Loss Trend**: 0% → 15% → 35% → 60% → 85% (approx.)
- **Calibration Equation**: $f_c = aR^{2} + bR + c$ (specific to hammer)
- **Output Deliverables**: Rebound Number table, $f_c$ vs T plot, % strength loss bar chart
- **Viva Essentials**: Principle, IS code, microstructural changes, error sources
- **Limitation**: Only tests surface layer (~30 mm); not representative of core
- **Post-Fire Use**: Preliminary screening tool before destructive core testing

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test of Non-Combustibility of Building Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL507 - FIRE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Test of Non-Combustibility of Building Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU / IS 3808 Standard)

A **building material** is classified as **non-combustible** when it fails to ignite, sustain flaming combustion, or release significant flammable vapours when subjected to a standardized thermal exposure of **750 °C** in a calibrated electric furnace, as per the test method prescribed in **IS 3808:1979** (Reaffirmed) / **ISO 1182:2010** *"Reaction to Fire Tests — Non-Combustibility Test"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCSFL507 – Module 2):**
> The test determines whether a material contributes fuel to a fire. It is a **"Yes / No" classification test**, NOT a measure of fire resistance duration (which is a separate furnace test on assemblies, IS 3809 / ISO 834).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stove Test"

Imagine placing a sample of butter and a piece of granite side by side on a hot tava (griddle) at **200 °C**:
- The **butter** melts, smokes, and catches fire → *Combustible*.
- The **granite** gets hot but does not burn → *Non-combustible*.

The non-combustibility test simply does this rigorously inside a controlled laboratory furnace at **750 °C** for a fixed duration and measures **three quantifiable parameters** to decide the result objectively, rather than relying on visual judgement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants / Standard Metrics (must be memorized for KTU viva & exam):**
> - Furnace Temperature: **750 °C ± 5 °C**
> - Sample Geometry: Cylinder, **45 mm diameter × 50 mm height**
> - Sample Mass: **~50 g**
> - Thermocouples: **Three Type-K (Chromel-Alumel)** — Furnace, Specimen Surface, Specimen Centre
> - Test Duration: **Until temperature equilibrium is reached, max 60 minutes**

## 1.3 Why Non-Combustibility Matters in Fire Engineering

In a real building fire, the **walls, floors, ceilings, and insulation** form the major fuel load. A non-certified "non-combustible" cladding (e.g., ACM polyethylene-core panels) can cause **catastrophic flashovers** as seen in the **Grenfell Tower (London, 2017)** disaster. Hence, IS 3808 acts as the **first filter** for material acceptance in high-rise construction under the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4, Clause 3.4.10)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of the Test

The principle is to subject a small specimen to a single high-temperature thermal attack and observe:
1. Whether **visible flaming** occurs on the surface.
2. The **temperature rise** of the specimen (mass-weighted energy release proxy).
3. The **mass loss** of the specimen (volatile fuel released).

A material passes (is classified *non-combustible*) only if **all three** of the following are satisfied simultaneously:

| S.No. | Parameter | Pass Criterion (per IS 3808) |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Visible Flaming | **No sustained flaming** for > 5 seconds |
| 2 | Average Specimen Temp. Rise (ΔT) | **≤ 50 °C** above initial equilibrium |
| 3 | Average Furnace Temp. Rise (ΔT_f) | **≤ 50 °C** above 750 °C set point |
| 4 | Mass Loss (Δm) | **≤ 50 %** of original specimen mass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Three × 50" Rule** — A material passes IS 3808 only if Temperature rise of specimen ≤ 50 °C, Furnace temp rise ≤ 50 °C, and Mass loss ≤ 50 %. This is the single most-tested viva point.

## 2.2 Apparatus Description

The apparatus is a **vertical cylindrical furnace** with the following sub-systems:

- **Furnace Tube:** Refractory tube, **75 mm inner diameter, 150 mm long**, wound with nickel-chromium (80/20) resistance wire.
- **Heating Coil:** Non-inductively wound to eliminate electromagnetic interference with thermocouples.
- **Insulation:** Ceramic fibre blanket outside the tube.
- **Specimen Holder:** Stainless steel basket suspended from a low-thermal-inertia wire.
- **Thermocouples (TC):** 
  - TC-1: Furnace wall temperature.
  - TC-2: Specimen surface temperature.
  - TC-3: Specimen centre temperature.
- **Stabilization Shield:** A cylindrical heat shield is lowered over the specimen once inserted to prevent draught disturbance.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Flow (Why & How)

1. **Why** preheat the furnace to 750 °C? — *To ensure a stable thermal boundary so that any temperature rise in the specimen is solely due to its combustion (or lack thereof), not due to the furnace ramping up.*
2. **Why** insert the specimen via a rapid-shutter mechanism? — *To minimize heat loss from the furnace during insertion and to give a sharp t = 0 reference for temperature recording.*
3. **Why** use a low-thermal-mass specimen holder? — *To prevent the holder itself from absorbing heat meant for the specimen, which would mask the true specimen temperature rise.*
4. **How** is "temperature rise" interpreted? — *It is the average of the maximum temperatures reached at the surface (TC-2) and centre (TC-3) of the specimen, minus the initial equilibrium temperature recorded just before insertion.*

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the following three equations verbatim — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B answer for this experiment.**

$$
\Delta T_{specimen} = \frac{(T_{s,max} - T_{s,0}) + (T_{c,max} - T_{c,0})}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{furnace} = T_{f,max} - T_{f,0}
$$

$$
\Delta m \; (\%) = \frac{m_0 - m_f}{m_0} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $T_{s,max}$ = Maximum surface temperature (°C)  
- $T_{c,max}$ = Maximum centre temperature (°C)  
- $T_{s,0}, T_{c,0}$ = Initial equilibrium temperatures (°C)  
- $T_{f,max}$ = Maximum furnace temperature (°C)  
- $T_{f,0}$ = Initial furnace set-point temperature (°C)  
- $m_0$ = Initial specimen mass (g)  
- $m_f$ = Final specimen mass (g) after cooling

**Engineering Utility:** These three values are used by structural fire designers and code officials (NBC, NFPA 220, BS 476 Part 4) to whitelist materials for use in **fire compartments, lift shafts, escape staircases, and server rooms**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the "Average Specimen Temperature Rise" Equation

The specimen is not isothermal during the test — its **surface** heats faster (due to direct radiation) than its **centre**. The standard therefore specifies the arithmetic mean of the two, normalized against the initial equilibrium value.

**Step 1 — Record Initial Equilibrium Readings**
At t = 0 (just before insertion), the operator notes:
$T_{s,0}$ and $T_{c,0}$ from TC-2 and TC-3. These are typically the ambient specimen temperature ~ 25 °C to 40 °C after pre-conditioning.

**Step 2 — Record Maximum Temperatures During Test**
The data logger continuously records TC-2 and TC-3 traces for the full test duration. The peak values are identified:
$T_{s,max} = \max\{T_{s}(t)\}$ and $T_{c,max} = \max\{T_{c}(t)\}$.

**Step 3 — Compute Individual Rises**

$$
\Delta T_s = T_{s,max} - T_{s,0}
$$

$$
\Delta T_c = T_{c,max} - T_{c,0}
$$

**Step 4 — Take Arithmetic Mean (per IS 3808 Clause 6.3)**

$$
\Delta T_{specimen} = \frac{\Delta T_s + \Delta T_c}{2} = \frac{(T_{s,max} - T_{s,0}) + (T_{c,max} - T_{c,0})}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision Rule**

$$
\text{Material is NON-COMBUSTIBLE} \iff \Delta T_{specimen} \le 50 \;^\circ\text{C} \;\; \textbf{AND} \;\; \Delta m \le 50\% \;\; \textbf{AND} \;\; \text{No sustained flaming}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Given Data from a Lab Test:**
A specimen of calcium silicate board (m₀ = 50.2 g) is tested in the IS 3808 furnace.

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Initial surface temperature | $T_{s,0}$ | 32 °C |
| Initial centre temperature | $T_{c,0}$ | 31 °C |
| Max. surface temperature | $T_{s,max}$ | 79 °C |
| Max. centre temperature | $T_{c,max}$ | 76 °C |
| Max. furnace temperature | $T_{f,max}$ | 758 °C |
| Final mass | $m_f$ | 49.4 g |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Furnace Temperature Rise**

$$
\Delta T_{furnace} = 758 - 750 = 8 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

Since 8 °C ≤ 50 °C → ✅ **Furnace criterion PASSES**

**Step 2 — Specimen Surface Rise**

$$
\Delta T_s = 79 - 32 = 47 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Specimen Centre Rise**

$$
\Delta T_c = 76 - 31 = 45 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Specimen Temperature Rise**

$$
\Delta T_{specimen} = \frac{47 + 45}{2} = \frac{92}{2} = 46 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

Since 46 °C ≤ 50 °C → ✅ **Specimen criterion PASSES**

**Step 5 — Percentage Mass Loss**

$$
\Delta m = \frac{50.2 - 49.4}{50.2} \times 100 = \frac{0.8}{50.2} \times 100 \approx 1.59\%
$$

Since 1.59 % ≤ 50 % → ✅ **Mass criterion PASSES**

**Step 6 — Flaming Observation**
No flaming observed for > 5 s → ✅ **Flaming criterion PASSES**

**Final Verdict:** The calcium silicate board is classified as **NON-COMBUSTIBLE** under IS 3808.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Combustible vs Non-Combustible Behaviour

| Parameter | Combustible Material (e.g., Plywood) | Non-Combustible Material (e.g., Gypsum) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Surface temperature rise ΔT_s | 200 °C – 500 °C | ≤ 50 °C |
| Centre temperature rise ΔT_c | 150 °C – 400 °C | ≤ 50 °C |
| Mass loss Δm | 60 % – 90 % | ≤ 5 % (mostly free water) |
| Visible flaming | Sustained > 5 s | None |
| Smoke emission | Heavy, dark | Negligible |
| Energy contribution to fire | Significant fuel load | Negligible |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — IS 3808 Test Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Power Supply and Temperature Controller 750C] --> B[Refractory Furnace Tube 75 mm ID]
    B --> C[Nickel Chromium Heating Coil 80 20]
    C --> D[Ceramic Fibre Insulation]
    E[Specimen Basket with Sample 45x50 mm] --> F[Low Thermal Mass Suspension Wire]
    F --> B
    G[TC1 Furnace Wall] --> H[Data Logger]
    I[TC2 Specimen Surface] --> H
    J[TC3 Specimen Centre] --> H
    H --> K[Decision Module Compute DeltaT DeltaM Flaming]
    K --> L{All Three Criteria Met}
    L -->|Yes| M[Classify as NON COMBUSTIBLE]
    L -->|No| N[Classify as COMBUSTIBLE]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style H fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style M fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style N fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Prepare cylindrical specimen 45 mm dia x 50 mm height] --> S2[Step 2 Condition at 60 percent RH and 27C for 24 hours]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Weigh specimen to 0.01 g precision record m0]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Preheat furnace to 750C and stabilize for 15 min]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Record initial TC1 TC2 TC3 readings]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Rapidly insert specimen via shutter mechanism]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Lower stabilization shield over specimen]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Log temperatures for 60 min or until equilibrium]
    S8 --> S9{Any sustained flaming greater than 5 s}
    S9 -->|Yes| R1[Mark as COMBUSTIBLE Stop]
    S9 -->|No| S10[Step 9 Remove cool and reweigh specimen record mf]
    S10 --> S11[Step 10 Compute DeltaTspecimen DeltaTfurnace and Deltam]
    S11 --> S12{All three 50 percent criteria satisfied}
    S12 -->|Yes| R2[Classify as NON COMBUSTIBLE]
    S12 -->|No| R3[Classify as COMBUSTIBLE]
    style S1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000
    style S6 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style S11 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
    style R2 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R1 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R3 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Test System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"]
        I1[Specimen Geometry and Mass]
        I2[Environmental Conditioning Data]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS["PROCESSING STAGE"]
        P1[Furnace Controller 750C]
        P2[Three Thermocouple Array]
        P3[Data Acquisition at 1 Hz]
    end
    subgraph ANALYSIS["ANALYSIS STAGE"]
        A1[Peak Temperature Extractor]
        A2[Mass Loss Calculator]
        A3[Flaming Observer Manual]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        O1[Pass or Fail Report]
        O2[IS 3808 Compliance Certificate]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> A1
    P3 --> A2
    P3 --> A3
    A1 --> O1
    A2 --> O1
    A3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style P3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style O2 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (Verbal Diagram)

| Zone | Component | Dimension / Spec |
|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Outer steel shell | Cylindrical, ~300 mm dia |
| 2 | Ceramic fibre insulation | 50 mm thick |
| 3 | Refractory tube | 75 mm ID × 150 mm height |
| 4 | Heating coil (NiCr 80/20) | Non-inductively wound |
| 5 | Specimen basket | Stainless steel mesh |
| 6 | Specimen | 45 mm × 50 mm cylinder |
| 7 | TC-1 (Furnace) | Located at tube wall mid-height |
| 8 | TC-2 (Surface) | Touches specimen lateral surface |
| 9 | TC-3 (Centre) | Inserted into specimen core |
| 10 | Stabilization shield | Sliding ceramic cylinder |
| 11 | Suspension wire | Nichrome, low mass |
| 12 | Data logger | 3-channel, 0.1 °C resolution |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "non-combustible material" as per IS 3808. List the three pass criteria.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **non-combustible material** is one that, when tested in a furnace preheated to **750 °C ± 5 °C** in accordance with **IS 3808:1979**, does not ignite, does not release significant combustible vapours, and does not cause a sustained temperature rise in the furnace.

The three pass criteria are:
1. **No sustained flaming** on the specimen surface for more than 5 seconds. **(1 mark)**
2. **Average temperature rise** of the specimen (surface + centre) **≤ 50 °C**. **(1 mark)**
3. **Mass loss** of the specimen **≤ 50 %** of its original mass. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is a low-thermal-mass specimen holder used in the IS 3808 furnace test?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A low-thermal-mass (e.g., thin stainless-steel mesh) specimen holder is used to **minimize the parasitic heat sink effect** during testing. **(1 mark)** If a heavy metallic holder were used, it would absorb a significant portion of the radiant and convective heat from the furnace walls, causing the **measured specimen temperature to be artificially lower** than its true value. **(1 mark)** This would lead to a false "non-combustible" classification even for materials that are actually combustible. The low-mass holder ensures that the heat input from the furnace is delivered predominantly to the specimen, allowing **accurate measurement of true temperature rise** and hence a faithful classification. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, describe the **apparatus and procedure** for determining the non-combustibility of a building material as per **IS 3808**. State the pass/fail criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A specimen of aerated concrete block (initial mass = 48.6 g) was tested in an IS 3808 furnace. The following observations were recorded: Initial surface temperature = 30 °C, initial centre temperature = 29 °C, maximum surface temperature = 84 °C, maximum centre temperature = 79 °C, maximum furnace temperature = 754 °C, final mass = 47.9 g. **Classify the material as combustible or non-combustible** with justification. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Apparatus, Procedure, and Criteria **(7 marks)**

**Apparatus (3 marks):**
The IS 3808 apparatus consists of a **vertical electric furnace** with a refractory tube of **75 mm inner diameter and 150 mm length**, heated by a **non-inductively wound nickel-chromium (80/20) resistance wire** to a uniform temperature of **750 °C ± 5 °C**. The tube is externally insulated with **ceramic fibre blanket**. The specimen is suspended in a **stainless-steel wire-mesh basket** held by a **low-thermal-mass nichrome wire**. **Three Type-K (Chromel-Alumel) thermocouples** are used — TC-1 measures the furnace wall temperature at the tube mid-height, TC-2 is in contact with the specimen lateral surface, and TC-3 is inserted into the geometric centre of the specimen. Temperatures are logged on a **3-channel data acquisition system** with at least **0.1 °C resolution** and a sampling rate of **1 Hz**. A **sliding ceramic stabilization shield** is lowered over the specimen after insertion to prevent air draughts.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
The specimen is cut into a **cylinder of 45 mm diameter and 50 mm height** and **conditioned for 24 hours at 60 % RH and 27 °C**. It is weighed to an accuracy of **0.01 g** (m₀). The furnace is preheated to 750 °C and stabilized for 15 min. Initial thermocouple readings (T_s,0, T_c,0, T_f,0) are recorded. The specimen is **rapidly inserted** into the furnace via a shutter mechanism, the stabilization shield is lowered, and temperatures are logged for **60 minutes or until equilibrium**. The specimen is then removed, cooled in a desiccator, and reweighed (m_f). The operator also notes any **visible flaming** during the test.

**Pass/Fail Criteria (2 marks):**
A material is classified as **non-combustible** if **all three** of the following are satisfied:
1. No flaming for > 5 seconds.
2. Average specimen temperature rise (ΔT) ≤ 50 °C.
3. Mass loss Δm ≤ 50 %.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Classification **(7 marks)**

**Given:** m₀ = 48.6 g, m_f = 47.9 g, T_s,0 = 30 °C, T_c,0 = 29 °C, T_s,max = 84 °C, T_c,max = 79 °C, T_f,max = 754 °C.

**Step 1 — Mass Loss (2 marks)**

$$
\Delta m = \frac{48.6 - 47.9}{48.6} \times 100 = \frac{0.7}{48.6} \times 100 \approx 1.44\%
$$

Since 1.44 % ≤ 50 % → ✅ **PASSES**

**Step 2 — Surface Temperature Rise (1 mark)**

$$
\Delta T_s = 84 - 30 = 54 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Centre Temperature Rise (1 mark)**

$$
\Delta T_c = 79 - 29 = 50 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Specimen Temperature Rise (1 mark)**

$$
\Delta T_{specimen} = \frac{54 + 50}{2} = \frac{104}{2} = 52 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision (2 marks):**
Since **52 °C > 50 °C**, the specimen temperature criterion **FAILS**. The furnace temperature rise of 4 °C and mass loss of 1.44 % are well within limits, but the **specimen temperature rise exceeds the 50 °C ceiling**. Assuming no sustained flaming was observed, the material fails on **one out of three criteria**.

**Verdict:** The aerated concrete block must be classified as **COMBUSTIBLE** under IS 3808, even though it is often marketed as "non-combustible" in commercial literature. The result demonstrates the importance of lab testing over manufacturer claims.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of the non-combustibility test** and differentiate it from the **surface flammability test (IS 3550)** and the **ignitability test (IS 8754)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During an IS 3808 test on a cementitious fire-protection board, the following data was obtained. Compute the specimen temperature rise, furnace temperature rise, and mass loss, and **state the classification** with reasons. **(7 marks)**

Initial surface temperature = 35 °C; Initial centre temperature = 34 °C; Max surface temperature = 76 °C; Max centre temperature = 74 °C; Max furnace temperature = 759 °C; Initial mass = 50.5 g; Final mass = 50.0 g.

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Principle and Differentiation **(7 marks)**

**Principle (3 marks):**
The non-combustibility test (IS 3808) determines whether a **homogeneous building material** contributes combustible fuel to a fully developed fire. A small specimen is exposed to a **uniform, high-temperature (750 °C) furnace environment** simulating the post-flashover compartment fire condition. The test measures the material's **thermal response** (temperature rise), **mass loss** (volatile release), and **flaming behaviour** under this severe thermal attack. The principle is **binary classification**: a material either passes (non-combustible) or fails (combustible) based on three quantitative criteria.

**Differentiation (4 marks):**

| Test | Standard | What it Measures | Pass Criterion | Specimen Size |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Non-Combustibility | **IS 3808** | Bulk fuel contribution | ΔT ≤ 50 °C, Δm ≤ 50 % | 45 × 50 mm cylinder |
| Surface Flammability | **IS 3550** | Flame spread on surface | Critical radiant flux at 25 kW/m² | 150 × 150 mm panel |
| Ignitability | **IS 8754** | Ease of ignition by radiant heat | Time to sustained flaming | 230 × 230 mm panel |

The non-combustibility test is the **most severe** of the three (highest temperature, smallest mass), making it the **first filter** in the regulatory approval chain. The other two tests apply to materials that have already passed IS 3808 to further classify their **surface fire performance**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Mass Loss (2 marks)**

$$
\Delta m = \frac{50.5 - 50.0}{50.5} \times 100 = \frac{0.5}{50.5} \times 100 \approx 0.99\%
$$

0.99 % ≤ 50 % → ✅ **PASS**

**Step 2 — Specimen Temperature Rises (2 marks)**

$$
\Delta T_s = 76 - 35 = 41 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_c = 74 - 34 = 40 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Average Specimen Temperature Rise (1 mark)**

$$
\Delta T_{specimen} = \frac{41 + 40}{2} = 40.5 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

40.5 °C ≤ 50 °C → ✅ **PASS**

**Step 4 — Furnace Temperature Rise (1 mark)**

$$
\Delta T_{furnace} = 759 - 750 = 9 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

9 °C ≤ 50 °C → ✅ **PASS**

**Step 5 — Classification (1 mark):**
All three quantitative criteria pass. Assuming no sustained flaming (standard for cementitious boards) → **Material is classified as NON-COMBUSTIBLE under IS 3808**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing units:** Students often report specimen temperature rise in °F or write the equation without showing the substitution of TC-2 and TC-3 values. *Always show ΔT_s and ΔT_c separately before averaging.*
> 2. **Forgetting the mass-loss check:** Many answers compute ΔT correctly but **omit the mass-loss calculation** (1–2 marks lost). Always include all three criteria in the verdict.
> 3. **Furnace temperature set-point:** The initial furnace temperature is **750 °C**, not 25 °C. Many students mistakenly compute ΔT_furnace = T_f,max − 25 °C. This is a 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Sample dimensions:** Writing 50 mm × 50 mm or 45 mm × 45 mm for the specimen — the correct size is **45 mm dia × 50 mm height**. Examiners are strict on this for apparatus description questions.
> 5. **Standard name:** Writing "IS 3808" without the year (1979) or the title loses half a mark in viva. Always write the **full citation**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Standard:** IS 3808:1979 / ISO 1182:2010 — *Non-Combustibility Test*.
- **Furnace Set Point:** **750 °C ± 5 °C** (memorize this number).
- **Specimen:** Cylindrical, **45 mm diameter × 50 mm height**, mass ~50 g.
- **Three Pass Criteria ("3 × 50 Rule"):** ΔT_specimen ≤ 50 °C, ΔT_furnace ≤ 50 °C, Δm ≤ 50 %, plus **no sustained flaming > 5 s**.
- **Three Thermocouples:** Furnace wall, specimen surface, specimen centre — all Type-K.
- **Stabilization Shield:** Lowers over specimen post-insertion to **prevent air draughts** that would distort temperature readings.
- **Low-Mass Holder:** Used to **avoid parasitic heat absorption** that would mask true temperature rise.
- **Test Duration:** Maximum **60 minutes** or until thermal equilibrium is reached.
- **Output:** Binary classification — **COMBUSTIBLE** or **NON-COMBUSTIBLE**. No intermediate grades.
- **Real-world relevance:** Grenfell Tower disaster (2017) underscores the legal weight of IS 3808 certification in high-rise construction.
- **Related but distinct tests:** IS 3550 (surface flammability), IS 8754 (ignitability), IS 3809 (fire resistance of elements — *not the same as non-combustibility*).
- **Examiner's favourite viva questions:** "Why 750 °C?", "Why three thermocouples?", "What is the role of the stabilization shield?", "Differentiate non-combustibility from fire resistance."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study on Class D Fire Extinguisher
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFL507 - FIRE ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study on Class D Fire Extinguisher ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition A **Class D Fire** (also termed a "Combustible Metal Fire") is defined under **IS 15683 / NFPA 10** as a fire involving **combustible metals and metal alloys** such as **magnesium (Mg), sodium (Na), potassium (K), aluminum (Al), titanium (Ti), zirconium (Zr), lithium (Li), and uranium (U)**. These fires are characterized by extremely high burning temperatures (often exceeding **2000 °C**), the ability to burn in the absence of atmospheric oxygen, violent reaction with water/CO₂, and the production of flammable hydrogen gas upon contact with moisture. A **Class D Fire Extinguisher** is therefore a highly specialized pressurized extinguisher charged with **dry powder extinguishing media** (e.g., **sodium chloride (NaCl), copper powder, graphite-based compounds, or specialized sodium carbonate-based dry powders**) specifically engineered to smother, cool, and isolate the burning metal from ambient oxygen. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the **BIS / IS 15683 classification**, Class D fires form a unique category because **NO water, foam, CO₂, wet chemical, or ABC powder** can be used — they will either explode, violently react, or be utterly ineffective. Only **specialized Class D dry powders** are permitted. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a **barbecue grill embered with magnesium shavings** glowing at over **2500 °C**. If you splash water on it, the water instantly splits into **hydrogen and oxygen gases** (a process called *electrolysis at extreme temperature*), and the hydrogen ignites with a deafening explosion — potentially **catastrophic**. Even worse, burning magnesium strips the oxygen right out of the water molecule (Mg + H₂O → MgO + H₂), producing flammable hydrogen. Think of a Class D extinguisher as a **"smothering blanket of inert salt."** The dry powder (like **sodium chloride — ordinary table salt, but in purified industrial grade**) is thrown onto the burning metal. The salt melts into a hard crust, **physically sealing the metal from oxygen**, while simultaneously **absorbing the intense heat** and **interrupting the chain reaction** of metal vapor combustion. It is essentially the equivalent of putting a **molten-glass lid** on a furnace. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Auto-ignition temperatures** of common Class D fuels: - **Magnesium**: ~**473 °C** (but burns at > 2500 °C) - **Sodium**: ~**115 °C** ignition, burns at ~**883 °C** - **Aluminum (powder)**: ~**760 °C** - **Titanium**: ~**250 °C** - **Zirconium**: ~**20 °C** (pyrophoric — ignites at room temperature!) - **Minimum extinguishing concentration** for NaCl powder on Mg fire: ~**0.5 – 1.5 kg/m²** - **Service pressure** of Class D extinguishers: typically **1.0 – 1.4 MPa (10 – 14 bar)** at 21 °C - **Standard discharge time**: **15 – 30 seconds** for handheld units - **Effective throw distance**: **3 – 6 meters** > [!NOTE] > **Why water fails dramatically:** Water contact with burning Na, K, or Mg liberates **H₂ gas** which instantly auto-ignites, often producing **hydrogen explosions** with blast overpressures exceeding **0.5 bar**. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Ordinary Extinguishing Agents Fail on Class D Fires The extinguishing failure can be explained through three core chemistry failures: | **Ordinary Agent** | **Reaction on Class D Fuel** | **Consequence** | |---|---|---| | **Water (H₂O)** | $2Na + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2NaOH + H_2 \uparrow$ | Hydrogen explosion | | **Water on Mg** | $Mg + 2H_2O \rightarrow MgO + H_2 \uparrow$ | Violent flare-up | | **CO₂** | $2Mg + CO_2 \rightarrow 2MgO + C$ (exothermic) | Fire intensifies | | **ABC Dry Powder (Monoammonium Phosphate)** | Reacts with burning Mg/Al to release **NH₃** and **PO₄** | Ineffective + toxic fumes | | **Foam** | Water content reacts; foam blanket melts | Re-ignition | ### 2.2 Mechanism of Extinguishing by Class D Dry Powder Class D extinguishers operate on a **three-pronged suppression mechanism**: 1. **Smothering / Oxygen Exclusion (Primary Mechanism)** The powder melts at temperatures typically **800 – 1200 °C** to form a **vitreous (glassy) crust** over the burning metal, blocking diffusion of atmospheric O₂. 2. **Heat Absorption / Heat Sink (Secondary Mechanism)** The melting and dissociation of the powder absorbs massive latent heat. For NaCl powder, the heat of fusion is approximately **$\Delta H_{fus}$ = 28.16 kJ/mol** and heat of vaporization is **$\Delta H_{vap}$ ≈ 171 kJ/mol**, effectively cooling the fuel below its ignition threshold. 3. **Chain Reaction Inhibition (Tertiary Mechanism)** Metal vapor combustion proceeds through free-radical chain carriers (e.g., MgO*, AlO* species). The chloride ions (Cl⁻) from NaCl **scavenge these radicals**, interrupting the chain-propagation step. ### 2.3 Classification of Class D Powders by Target Metal | **Extinguishing Powder Type** | **Chemical Formula** | **Target Metals** | **Mechanism** | |---|---|---|---| | **Sodium Chloride Powder** | NaCl (graphite-coated) | Na, K, Mg, Al | Smothering crust | | **Copper Powder** | Cu (graphite-coated) | Li, Li-alloys | Heat conduction away | | **Graphite-Based Powder** | C (with additives) | Mg, Na, K, Zr | Smothering + heat sink | | **Sodium Carbonate-Based** | Na₂CO₃ | Na, K, U | Carbonate decomposition releases CO₂ | | **Specialized Lithium Powder** | Mixed chloride/phosphate | Li, Li-ion battery | Multi-path suppression | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **#** | **Formula / Parameter** | **Expression** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Mass of extinguishing agent required | $m = A \times q$ | kg | | 2 | Where $A$ = area of fire | $A = \pi r^2$ | m² | | 3 | Application rate $q$ (typical) | $q = 0.5 - 1.5$ | kg/m² | | 4 | Discharge time $t_d$ | $t_d = \frac{m_{charge}}{\dot{m}_{discharge}}$ | s | | 5 | Mass flow rate of gas-propellant | $\dot{m}_{prop} = \frac{C_d A_t P_0}{\sqrt{RT_0}}$ | kg/s | | 6 | Throw distance (approx.) | $d \approx \frac{v_0^2 \sin(2\theta)}{g}$ | m | | 7 | Heat absorbed by powder crust | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_f$ | kJ | | 8 | Heat flux from burning metal | $\dot{q} = k \cdot (T_{flame} - T_{surface})$ | kW/m² | | 9 | Effectiveness ratio | $\eta = \frac{Q_{absorbed}}{\dot{q}_{fuel} \cdot t_{extinguish}}$ | dimensionless | | 10 | Service pressure (N₂ at 21 °C) | $P_{service} = 1.0 - 1.4$ | MPa | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Pitfall for KTU Exam:** Never use $P = \rho g h$ style hydrostatic equations for extinguisher pressure. Use the **ideal gas / choked nozzle equation** (formula #5) since the propellant is compressed **N₂ gas**, not a liquid column. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace Industry**: Machining of titanium and magnesium components generates fine metallic chips that spontaneously ignite — Class D extinguishers are mandatory. - **Foundries & Metallurgical Plants**: Molten aluminum and magnesium handling. - **Nuclear Reactors**: Uranium and plutonium handling. - **Pharmaceutical**: Lithium-based reagents and Li-ion battery manufacturing. - **Defense**: Pyrotechnic and propellant manufacturing. --- ## SECTION_3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of Dry Powder Required for a Class D Fire **Problem Statement:** A magnesium fire is burning in a circular spill of radius **$r = 1.5$ m**. If the recommended Class D application rate for magnesium is **$q = 1.2$ kg/m²**, calculate the total mass of dry powder required. **Step-by-Step Solution:** Step 1 — Compute the surface area of the spill (assuming a circular footprint, top-burning metal): $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \pi r^2 \\ A &= \pi \times (1.5)^2 \\ A &= \pi \times 2.25 \\ A &= 7.069 \;\; m^2 \end{aligned} $$ Step 2 — Apply the empirical application rate formula $m = A \times q$: $$ \begin{aligned} m &= A \times q \\ m &= 7.069 \;\; m^2 \times 1.2 \;\; kg/m^2 \\ m &= 8.483 \;\; kg \end{aligned} $$ Step 3 — Apply a **safety factor of 1.5** (KTU best-practice for Class D — never fight with the bare minimum): $$ \begin{aligned} m_{design} &= 1.5 \times m_{calculated} \\ m_{design} &= 1.5 \times 8.483 \\ m_{design} &= 12.72 \;\; kg \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** A **12.72 kg** Class D extinguisher (or two 6 kg units) should be deployed. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Discharge Time of a Class D Extinguisher **Problem Statement:** A Class D extinguisher contains **$m_{charge}$ = 12 kg** of NaCl powder. The discharge nozzle has been characterized to deliver a mass flow rate of **$\dot{m}_{discharge}$ = 0.6 kg/s**. Calculate the discharge time. **Step-by-Step Solution:** Step 1 — Apply the basic discharge relationship: $$ \begin{aligned} t_d &= \frac{m_{charge}}{\dot{m}_{discharge}} \\ t_d &= \frac{12 \;\; kg}{0.6 \;\; kg/s} \\ t_d &= 20 \;\; s \end{aligned} $$ Step 2 — Compare against **NFPA 10 minimum discharge time** for Class D (must be ≥ 15 s): $$ t_d = 20 \;\; s \;\; \geq \;\; 15 \;\; s \;\; \checkmark $$ **Conclusion:** The extinguisher complies with NFPA 10 / IS 15683 minimum discharge duration. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Throw Distance of Powder Jet **Problem Statement:** The propellant gas (N₂) ejects the powder with an initial velocity **$v_0 = 15$ m/s** at an optimum angle **$\theta = 35°$**. Calculate the horizontal throw distance (assume $g = 9.81$ m/s²). **Step-by-Step Solution:** Step 1 — Apply the projectile motion range formula: $$ \begin{aligned} d &= \frac{v_0^2 \cdot \sin(2\theta)}{g} \\ d &= \frac{(15)^2 \cdot \sin(2 \times 35°)}{9.81} \\ d &= \frac{225 \times \sin(70°)}{9.81} \\ d &= \frac{225 \times 0.9397}{9.81} \\ d &= \frac{211.43}{9.81} \\ d &= 21.55 \;\; m \end{aligned} $$ Step 2 — Apply the **drag-correction factor** $\beta = 0.30$ (typical for granular powder cloud): $$ \begin{aligned} d_{actual} &= \beta \times d_{ideal} \\ d_{actual} &= 0.30 \times 21.55 \\ d_{actual} &= 6.47 \;\; m \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** The operator can stand **~6.5 m away** — a critical safety margin when fighting magnesium fires. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Heat Absorption Capacity of Powder Crust **Problem Statement:** A **$m = 5$ kg** sodium chloride powder crust absorbs heat while cooling from the flame temperature of **$T_f = 1000$ °C** down to the metal ignition temperature **$T_i = 473$ °C** (Magnesium). Specific heat of molten NaCl $c_p \approx 1.0$ kJ/(kg·K); latent heat of fusion $L_f = 28.16$ kJ/mol = **481 kJ/kg**. **Step-by-Step Solution:** Step 1 — Sensible heat absorbed by the crust: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{sensible} &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\ \Delta T &= T_f - T_i = 1000 - 473 = 527 \;\; K \\ Q_{sensible} &= 5 \times 1.0 \times 527 \\ Q_{sensible} &= 2635 \;\; kJ \end{aligned} $$ Step 2 — Latent heat absorbed during crust solidification: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{latent} &= m \cdot L_f \\ Q_{latent} &= 5 \times 481 \\ Q_{latent} &= 2405 \;\; kJ \end{aligned} $$ Step 3 — Total heat absorbed: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{total} &= Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent} \\ Q_{total} &= 2635 + 2405 \\ Q_{total} &= 5040 \;\; kJ \end{aligned} $$ **Insight:** 5 kg of NaCl powder absorbs **5040 kJ** — equivalent to the energy released by **~120 g of TNT combustion**, demonstrating the massive heat-sink capacity of Class D powders. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Class D Powders | **Powder** | **m.p. (°C)** | **Crust Quality** | **Best For** | **Re-ignition Risk** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **NaCl** | **801** | Hard, vitreous | Mg, Na, K | **Low** | | **Cu Powder** | **1085** | Conductive metallic | Li, Li-alloys | **Medium** | | **Graphite** | **~3650 (subl.)** | Porous, insulating | Mg, Zr, U | **Low–Medium** | | **Na₂CO₃** | **851** | Brittle, friable | Na, K | **High** | | **Special Li Powder** | **~600–800** | Cl-P mixed | Li-metal | **Low** | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Component-Level Architecture of a Class D Fire Extinguisher ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["CYLINDER BODY
Seamless Steel ASME Certified
Service Pressure 1.4 MPa"] nodeB["DRY POWDER CHARGE
NaCl Graphite-Coated
12 kg Typical"] nodeC["N2 PROPELLANT GAS
Charged at 1.2 MPa
at 21 degree C"] nodeD["DIP TUBE AND SYPHON PIPE
Reaches Bottom of Charge"] nodeE["DISCHARGE VALVE AND LEVER
Squeeze Grip Type"] nodeF["PRESSURE GAUGE
Indicates Charge Status"] nodeG["DISCHARGE HOSE AND NOZZLE
Long Lance Nozzle 60 cm"] nodeH["APPLICATOR SPOON
For Precise Application"] nodeI["SAFETY PIN AND TAMPER SEAL"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeA nodeI --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF ``` ### 4.2 Operational Flow — From Detection to Extinguishment ```mermaid flowchart TD s1["STEP 1 IDENTIFY CLASS D FIRE
Bright White Flames
Intense Radiant Heat
Spattering Metal Sparks"] s2["STEP 2 EVACUATE NON ESSENTIAL PERSONNEL
Minimum 10 m Radius"] s3["STEP 3 RETRIEVE EXTINGUISHER
Carry by Handle
Lance Pointed Downward"] s4["STEP 4 REMOVE SAFETY PIN AND SEAL"] s5["STEP 5 CHECK PRESSURE GAUGE
Needle in GREEN zone"] s6["STEP 6 APPROACH FROM UPWIND
Maintain 3 to 6 m Distance"] s7["STEP 7 SQUEEZE LEVER AND APPLY POWDER
Sweep Gently Over Burning Metal"] s8["STEP 8 BUILD CRUST
Cover Entire Surface of Burning Metal"] s9["STEP 9 HOLD FOR 5 MINUTES
Allow Crust to Harden"] s10["STEP 10 WATCH FOR RE IGNITION
Be Ready for Re Application"] s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9 --> s10 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Mechanism of Extinguishing by NaCl Powder ```mermaid flowchart LR a["BURNING CLASS D METAL
Mg 2500 degree C"] b["NACL POWDER APPLIED
Through Lance"] c["POWDER MELTS AT 801 degree C
Forms Vitreous Crust"] d["CRUST BLOCKS OXYGEN DIFFUSION
Smothering Effect"] e["HEAT ABSORBED
Latent Heat 481 kJ per kg"] f["CL ION SCAVENGES METAL VAPOR RADICALS
Chain Reaction Breaks"] g["FIRE EXTINGUISHED
No Re Ignition if Crust Intact"] a --> b --> c --> d --> g c --> e --> g c --> f --> g ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — 3-Mark Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define a Class D fire. Give two examples of fuels and state why water cannot be used on such fires.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Class D fire is one involving **combustible metals** such as magnesium, sodium, potassium, titanium, zirconium, and lithium (1½ marks). Water cannot be used because it reacts violently with these metals, producing **flammable hydrogen gas** which auto-ignites, leading to explosions. The reaction of water with sodium, for example, is $2Na + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2NaOH + H_2 \uparrow$, where the liberated hydrogen ignites spontaneously (1½ marks). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List three types of dry powders used in Class D extinguishers and the metals they are most suited for.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Sodium Chloride (NaCl) Powder** — suited for magnesium, sodium, potassium fires (1 mark). 2. **Copper Powder (graphite-coated)** — suited for lithium and lithium-alloy fires (1 mark). 3. **Graphite-based dry powder** — suited for magnesium, zirconium, and uranium fires (1 mark). --- ### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]* **Q.A (a)** *Describe in detail the constructional features and working principle of a stored-pressure Class D dry powder extinguisher. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** **(i) Constructional Features (3½ marks):** A stored-pressure Class D extinguisher consists of: - **Cylinder body** — seamless steel, hydrostatically tested at 3 MPa, rated for 1.0–1.4 MPa service. - **Dry powder charge** — typically 8–14 kg of graphite-coated NaCl. - **Propellant** — dry nitrogen gas charged at 1.2 MPa to expel the powder. - **Dip tube / syphon pipe** — reaches the bottom to draw powder up. - **Discharge valve** — squeeze-grip nickel-plated brass lever. - **Pressure gauge** — Bourdon-type, with red/green zones. - **Long discharge lance** — 50–60 cm length to allow operator to maintain distance. - **Applicator spoon/nozzle** — for precise localized application. - **Safety pin and tamper seal**. **(ii) Working Principle (3½ marks):** When the lever is squeezed, the valve opens and pressurized **N₂ gas** flows down the dip tube, fluidizing the NaCl powder. The powder is carried as an aerosol cloud through the hose and discharged through the lance nozzle. The powder impacts the burning metal, melts at 801 °C, and forms a **vitreous crust** that excludes oxygen. Simultaneously, the latent and sensible heat absorption cools the metal below its ignition temperature, and Cl⁻ ions scavenge metal-vapor radicals, breaking the combustion chain reaction. --- **Q.A (b)** *A 12 kg NaCl Class D extinguisher is used on a magnesium fire of circular spill radius 1.2 m. The recommended application rate is 1.5 kg/m². The discharge mass flow rate is 0.5 kg/s. Determine: (i) the mass of powder required with a safety factor of 1.5, and (ii) whether the extinguisher's discharge time meets NFPA 10 minimum of 15 s. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** **(i) Mass of powder required (4 marks):** Step 1 — Calculate spill area: $$ A = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (1.2)^2 = 4.524 \;\; m^2 $$ Step 2 — Calculate base mass: $$ m = A \times q = 4.524 \times 1.5 = 6.786 \;\; kg $$ Step 3 — Apply safety factor: $$ m_{design} = 1.5 \times 6.786 = 10.18 \;\; kg $$ Since **$m_{design}$ = 10.18 kg < 12 kg (extinguisher charge)**, the extinguisher is adequate. **(ii) Discharge time (3 marks):** $$ t_d = \frac{m_{charge}}{\dot{m}_{discharge}} = \frac{12}{0.5} = 24 \;\; s $$ Since **24 s ≥ 15 s**, the extinguisher complies with NFPA 10 / IS 15683 minimum discharge duration. --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]* **Q.B (a)** *Explain with chemical equations why water, CO₂, foam, and ABC powder are unsuitable for Class D fires. Show at least two equations. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** **(i) Water – violently reactive (2 marks):** $$ 2Na + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2NaOH + H_2 \uparrow \quad \text{(hydrogen explosion)} $$ $$ Mg + 2H_2O \rightarrow MgO + H_2 \uparrow \quad \text{(violent flare-up)} $$ **(ii) CO₂ – reacts exothermically (2 marks):** $$ 2Mg + CO_2 \rightarrow 2MgO + C \quad \Delta H \approx -810 \;\; kJ/mol \;\; \text{(fire intensifies)} $$ **(iii) Foam – ineffective due to water content (1½ marks):** AFFF foam has ~94% water content. On magnesium, water splits to release flammable H₂ gas, and the foam blanket is destroyed by the 2500 °C flame. **(iv) ABC Powder – thermal decomposition releases NH₃ and toxic fumes (1½ marks):** $$ NH_4H_2PO_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} H_3PO_4 + NH_3 \uparrow $$ The NH₃ and phosphate do not effectively suppress the metal fire; instead they add toxic byproducts and may even react with hot aluminum: $$ 2Al + 2NH_3 \rightarrow 2AlN + 3H_2 \uparrow $$ --- **Q.B (b)** *Discuss in detail the three-pronged extinguishing mechanism of Class D dry powders. Also compute the total heat absorbed by 6 kg of NaCl powder crust cooling from 1100 °C to 470 °C, given $c_p$ = 1.0 kJ/(kg·K) and latent heat of fusion $L_f$ = 481 kJ/kg. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** **(i) Three-Pronged Mechanism (4 marks):** 1. **Smothering (Primary):** Powder melts to form vitreous crust, blocking O₂ diffusion. 2. **Heat Absorption (Secondary):** Latent + sensible heat removal cools fuel below ignition T. 3. **Chain Reaction Inhibition (Tertiary):** Cl⁻ scavenges metal-vapor free radicals. **(ii) Heat Calculation (3 marks):** Step 1 — Sensible heat: $$ Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 6 \times 1.0 \times (1100 - 470) = 6 \times 630 = 3780 \;\; kJ $$ Step 2 — Latent heat: $$ Q_{latent} = m \cdot L_f = 6 \times 481 = 2886 \;\; kJ $$ Step 3 — Total: $$ Q_{total} = 3780 + 2886 = 6666 \;\; kJ $$ The 6 kg NaCl crust absorbs **6666 kJ** of heat, demonstrating its massive heat-sink capacity. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–4 Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Writing "CO₂ is suitable for Class D"** — NEVER. CO₂ reacts exothermically with magnesium (releases carbon and intensifies fire). This single error costs **all 14 marks** in many valuation scripts. > 2. **Forgetting the safety factor of 1.5** in mass-of-powder calculations — examiners expect it for industrial realism. > 3. **Using horizontal projectile formula $d = v_0 t$** instead of $d = v_0^2 \sin(2\theta)/g$ — full 2-mark deduction. > 4. **Confusing Class D with Class B** — Class B is flammable liquids, not metals. > 5. **Forgetting to mention re-ignition watch** — Class D fires are notorious for reigniting if the crust is disturbed. > 6. **Not mentioning lance/spoon nozzle** — short nozzles are dangerous on Class D because of intense radiant heat; examiners award 1 mark specifically for the long lance. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Class D = Combustible Metal Fires** (Mg, Na, K, Al, Ti, Zr, Li, U). - **Extinguishing media = Specialized Dry Powders only** — never water, foam, CO₂, or ABC powder. - **Common agents:** NaCl (most popular), Cu powder, graphite-based, Na₂CO₃-based. - **Mechanism:** Smothering + Heat absorption + Chain inhibition (three-pronged). - **NaCl melting point:** **801 °C**; **Latent heat:** **481 kJ/kg**. - **Application rate $q$:** **0.5 – 1.5 kg/m²** (use safety factor **1.5×**). - **Discharge time:** ≥ **15 s** (NFPA 10 minimum). - **Service pressure:** **1.0 – 1.4 MPa** of dry N₂. - **Distance:** Operate from **3 – 6 m** away using long lance (50–60 cm). - **Re-ignition:** Class D fires can reignite even after 24 h — never leave site unattended. - **Standard:** **IS 15683 / NFPA 10**. - **Application areas:** Aerospace, foundries, nuclear, defense, lithium-battery manufacturing. - **Key equations to memorize:** $m = A \times q$, $t_d = m/\dot{m}$, $d = v_0^2 \sin(2\theta)/g$, $Q = mc_p \Delta T + mL_f$. - **Colors:** No international color code for Class D; extinguisher body is usually **red** with a **yellow band** and "CLASS D" marking.

topics.title: Study of Extinguishers in Electrical Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL507 - FIRE ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Extinguishers in Electrical Fire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **electrical fire** is classified under the **Indian Standard (IS 2190:1992) classification** as a **Class C fire** — a fire involving **energized electrical equipment**, appliances, wiring, switchgear, transformers, and other live (current-carrying) components. Because the extinguishing medium itself can become a conductor, these fires demand **non-conductive, dielectrically robust extinguishing agents** that can suppress the fire *without* creating a secondary hazard of electric shock to the operator or a short-circuit path that may reignite or escalate the incident.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> *"A Class C fire (per IS 2190) is a fire in or near energized electrical equipment where the extinguishing medium must be non-conductive to prevent electric shock hazard to personnel and to avoid creating short-circuit paths that could intensify the fire."*

The equipment used to combat such fires is called a **Class C rated fire extinguisher**. The most common variants authorised under **IS 15683:2018** for Class C use are:
1. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) Extinguisher** — clean, leaves no residue, electrically non-conductive.
2. **Dry Chemical Powder (DCP / ABC) Extinguisher** — interrupts the chemical chain reaction of combustion.
3. **Clean Agent (Halon-replacement / HCFC / FK-5-1-12) Extinguisher** — used in server rooms, data centres, and avionics.
4. **Dry Powder (for specific metal-clad switchgear)** — Class D, but occasionally encountered in lab visits.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: "Water and Lightning"**
> Imagine you are standing in a flooded room and someone hands you a live 230 V wire. Would you touch it? Of course not — the water around your feet would conduct the current straight through your body. The same logic applies to electrical fires. If you spray **water** (or any foam with a water base) onto a **live electrical panel**, the water becomes a **bridge** for current. The current can travel back through the jet, up the cylinder, and into the operator's hand — turning the rescuer into a circuit. Worse, water reacts violently with molten copper/aluminium in switchgear, producing **hydrogen gas and explosive steam**, which can cause a **secondary explosion**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook for the exam:** *"If it sparks, do not spark it with water."* Electrical fires are fought by **smothering** (cutting off oxygen) or by **chemical chain interruption** (killing the flame's free radicals), **never** by water-based cooling unless the equipment is **confirmed and locked out de-energised**.

### 1.3 Why Standard Water Extinguishers Are Forbidden on Live Equipment

- Ordinary tap water has a **resistivity of roughly 20 Ω·m** and contains dissolved ions (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Cl⁻), making it a **fair conductor**.
- The jet stream maintains a **continuous liquid column** from the nozzle to the fire — this column is a live conductor.
- **Dielectric breakdown voltage** of water at typical nozzle pressures is approximately **30 kV/cm** only when ultra-pure (deionised). In reality, fire-fighting water breaks down far below mains voltage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Students who write "use water to cool the fire" for an electrical fire **will lose full marks**. The textbook answer is *always* **non-conductive media only — CO₂ or DCP**.

### 1.4 Classification of Fires (Quick Reference for Exam)

| Fire Class | Fuel Type | Standard Extinguishing Principle |
|------------|-----------|----------------------------------|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth) | Water cooling / quenching |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids (petrol, oil, solvents) | Smothering / foam blanketing |
| **Class C** | **Energised electrical equipment** | **Non-conductive agent (CO₂ / DCP)** |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals (Mg, Na, K, Ti) | Special dry powder |
| **Class K** | Cooking oils and fats (kitchen) | Wet chemical saponification |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fire Tetrahedron & Mechanism of Extinguishment

A fire sustains itself via four elements: **Fuel + Oxygen + Heat + Sustained Chain Reaction (free radicals ·OH, ·H, ·O)**. Remove any one "face" of this tetrahedron and the fire collapses.

For an **electrical fire**, the mechanisms of extinguishment differ by agent:

**A. Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) — Mechanism: Smothering + Cooling**
1. CO₂ is stored as a **liquid under high pressure (~58 bar at 21 °C)** inside a seamless steel cylinder.
2. On discharge through a siphon tube and horn, the liquid **expands rapidly** and forms **dry-ice snow (solid CO₂ at −78.5 °C)**.
3. The cold snow **displaces oxygen** around the fire (the air is pushed out of the horn's gas cloud).
4. The **latent heat of sublimation (~571 kJ/kg)** is absorbed from the fire and the surrounding equipment, producing a modest cooling effect.
5. Result: oxygen concentration falls below the **flame-propagation threshold of ~15 % (v/v)**.

**B. Dry Chemical Powder (DCP / ABC) — Mechanism: Chemical Chain Inhibition (Negative Catalyst)**
1. Monoammonium phosphate (NH₄H₂PO₄) powder is propelled by **N₂ or CO₂ propellant gas** through a discharge hose.
2. The micron-sized powder particles (typically **10–75 µm diameter**) decompose in the flame to yield **HPO₃ and H₃PO₄ radicals**.
3. These radicals **recombine with the combustion free radicals (·OH, ·H)** that sustain the flame, terminating the chain reaction.
4. Additionally, a fine **powder cloud** smothers the fire and **radiation shields** the unburnt fuel.
5. This is a **chemical intervention** — it works even at low concentrations (~5–10 % by volume in the flame zone).

**C. Clean Agents (FK-5-1-12 / Novec 1230, HFC-227ea) — Mechanism: Heat-Absorption + Mild Chemical Action**
1. Stored as a **liquefied compressed gas** at low pressure (~25 bar).
2. Discharges as a **colourless, odourless, electrically non-conductive vapour**.
3. Absorbs heat through its high **heat of vaporisation (~88 kJ/kg for Novec 1230)**.
4. Leaves **zero residue** — critical for **server rooms and CNC control panels**.

### 2.2 Physical & Dielectric Properties (Critical for Lab Viva)

| Property | CO₂ | DCP (ABC Powder) | Clean Agent (Novec 1230) |
|----------|-----|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Dielectric strength (kV at 1 cm gap)** | **30 kV** | **5 kV** (powder is a *poor* conductor; safe for low/medium voltage) | **>40 kV** |
| **Boiling point** | −78.5 °C (sublimes) | N/A (solid particles) | 49 °C |
| **Storage pressure** | **58–70 bar at 21 °C** | 12–15 bar (N₂ propellant) | 25 bar |
| **Residue left on equipment** | **None** | Heavy white/yellow powder — must be cleaned | **None** |
| **Suitable for voltage class** | Up to **36 kV** (with safe distance) | Up to **1000 V** (low-voltage) | Up to **any** (with safe distance) |
| **Discharge time (typical 4.5 kg cylinder)** | 9–15 s | 12–18 s | 9–10 s |
| **Effective throw range** | 1.5–2.5 m | 3–5 m | 2.5–4 m |
| **Applicable IS Standard** | IS 15683 / IS 2878 | IS 15683 / IS 13849 | IS 15683 (Part 9) |
| **Class of fire rated for** | B, C | A, B, C | B, C (and A in some) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Relation | Units | Engineering Use |
|---------------|-------------------|-------|-----------------|
| **Effective discharge time** | $t_{eff} = \dfrac{V_{cyl} \cdot \rho_{liq}}{Q_{nozzle}}$ | seconds | Predicts how long the cylinder will last |
| **Coverage area** | $A_{cov} = L \cdot W$ where $L = v_{jet} \cdot t_{eff}$ | m² | Floor area the jet can sweep |
| **Oxygen displacement by CO₂** | $\Delta O_2 \approx \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{m_{air}+m_{CO_2}} \times 21\%$ | % | Verifies that flame is starved |
| **Heat absorbed by CO₂ snow** | $Q_{abs} = m_{CO_2} \cdot L_{sub}$ where $L_{sub} = 571 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | kJ | Cooling contribution to extinguishment |
| **Concentration needed for ABC powder** | $C_{ABC} \approx 0.05$ to $0.10$ (mass fraction in flame) | dimensionless | Minimum powder density for chain-break |
| **Dielectric breakdown check** | $V_{supply} < V_{bd} \cdot d_{gap}$ | V | Ensures no arcing through agent |
| **Discharge rate** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\text{Cylinder mass}}{\text{Discharge time}}$ | kg/s | Used to size nozzle |
| **Specific enthalpy of vaporisation (Novec)** | $h_{fg} \approx 88 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | kJ/kg | Heat sink per kg of agent |
| **Critical oxygen concentration (COC)** | $O_{2,c} \approx 15\%$ (v/v) for most fires | % | Threshold below which flame dies |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU shortcut for quick numerical problems:**
> The **effective throw range** in metres ≈ (discharge pressure in bar) × 0.04 — a handy rule of thumb often quoted in IS training manuals.

### 2.4 Why CO₂ Is Preferred for Electrical Fires in the KTU Lab

1. **Zero residue** — does not corrode printed circuit boards, relay contacts, or insulation.
2. **High dielectric strength** — the vapour is essentially a gas insulator, much like SF₆ used in switchgear.
3. **Rapid oxygen displacement** — extinguishment in 5–10 s for a typical 4.5 kg cylinder on a small panel fire.
4. **Self-pressurising cylinder** — no separate propellant cartridge required; the cylinder's own vapour pressure drives the discharge.
5. **Refillable on site** — the cylinder can be recharged from bulk CO₂ without disassembly.

### 2.5 IS Standards Governing the Lab Experiment

- **IS 2190:1992** — Classification of fires and selection of extinguishers.
- **IS 15683:2018** — Portable fire extinguishers — performance and construction (supersedes IS 13849/IS 2878 in many respects).
- **IS 14612–14614** — Maintenance and recharging of portable extinguishers.
- **NFPA 10 (for cross-reference)** — Standard for portable fire extinguishers (used in industrial safety audits).
- **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee) guidelines** — Mandatory ratings for commercial installations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Discharge Time of a CO₂ Cylinder

A standard KTU-lab **CO₂ extinguisher of nominal water capacity 4.5 kg** is taken. The problem is to estimate how long the cylinder will deliver a usable jet.

**Given:**
- Mass of liquid CO₂ charged into cylinder: $m_{CO_2} = 4.5 \text{ kg}$
- Density of liquid CO₂ at 21 °C: $\rho_{liq} = 770 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Nozzle discharge coefficient: $C_d = 0.85$
- Nozzle throat diameter: $d = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.010 \text{ m}$
- Cylinder gauge pressure at 21 °C: $P_{cyl} = 58 \text{ bar} = 5.8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1 — Nozzle exit area:**

$$
A_{nozzle} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.010)^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_{nozzle} = \frac{\pi \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical jet velocity from Bernoulli (assuming isentropic expansion of liquefied gas, simplified):**

$$
v_{jet} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot P_{cyl}}{\rho_{gas}}}
$$

where $\rho_{gas} \approx 1.98 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for CO₂ vapour at 1 atm and 20 °C.

$$
v_{jet} = 0.85 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5.8 \times 10^6}{1.98}}
$$

$$
v_{jet} = 0.85 \cdot \sqrt{5.86 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
v_{jet} = 0.85 \cdot 2420 \text{ m/s (theoretical)}
$$

A practical CO₂ jet reaches a **terminal throw velocity of ~12 m/s** at the horn exit because much of the pressure energy converts into **solid CO₂ snow formation** (phase change), not kinetic energy. We use the **practical** value:

$$
v_{jet,\,practical} \approx 12 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_{nozzle} \cdot v_{jet,\,practical} \cdot \rho_{snow}
$$

where $\rho_{snow}$ (the CO₂ snow + gas mixture at horn) ≈ **1.6 kg/m³**.

$$
\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \times 12 \times 1.6
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \times 19.2
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 1.508 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 1.282 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective discharge time:**

$$
t_{eff} = \frac{m_{CO_2}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{4.5}{1.282 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
t_{eff} \approx 3511 \text{ s}
$$

This is **theoretically** high; in practice, the cylinder pressure drops as liquid is consumed, and only **~50–60 %** of the liquid is deliverable as a usable jet (the rest remains as residual gas at the end of discharge). Applying a **discharge utilisation factor** $\eta_d = 0.55$:

$$
t_{eff,\,actual} = 3511 \times 0.55 \approx 1931 \text{ s}
$$

This is still long. The **empirical manufacturer rating of ~9–15 s for a 4.5 kg CO₂ extinguisher** is based on a much higher practical mass flow rate (~0.30–0.50 kg/s) achieved through **larger horns and high-velocity horn shrouds**. The simplified derivation above is a **first-principles** estimate; the real value comes from the **discharge test certificate** stamped on the cylinder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam takeaway:** For KTU derivations, present the *idealised* Bernoulli form, then quote the **manufacturer-rated discharge time** as the *practical* value, and explain the gap using the **utilisation factor**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Powder Concentration for Chain Inhibition

The **flame-propagation limit** in terms of powder mass fraction in the flame zone is empirically:

$$
C_{min} = \frac{m_{powder}}{m_{powder} + m_{air,\,in\,flame}}
$$

For ABC powder, the empirical minimum is **$C_{min} \approx 0.05$ to $0.10$**. Working out for a 1 m³ flame volume:

$$
m_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot V = 1.2 \times 1 = 1.2 \text{ kg}
$$

At $C_{min} = 0.075$ (mid-range):

$$
m_{powder} = \frac{C_{min} \cdot m_{air}}{1 - C_{min}} = \frac{0.075 \times 1.2}{0.925}
$$

$$
m_{powder} = 0.0973 \text{ kg} \approx 97.3 \text{ g}
$$

A standard 4 kg DCP extinguisher therefore carries **40× the minimum theoretical requirement** for a 1 m³ fire — confirming the **design safety margin** of 30–50× typically used in IS 15683.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Extinguisher for Voltage Class

| Voltage of Equipment | Recommended Extinguisher | Minimum Safe Approach Distance | Special Precaution |
|----------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------------|--------------------|
| **< 1000 V** (domestic, office, lab) | CO₂ (2–4.5 kg) or DCP (2–4 kg) | 1 m | Ensure earthing is **not** disturbed |
| **1 kV – 11 kV** (LT industrial panels) | CO₂ 4.5–9 kg | 2 m | Wear **class 0 insulating gloves** (IEC 60903) |
| **11 kV – 33 kV** (HT switchgear) | CO₂ 22.5–45 kg trolley | 3 m | Approach only after **locking out** if possible |
| **> 33 kV** (EHV substations) | **DO NOT approach** — call professional fire crew | 5 m minimum | Maintain safe distance; never discharge near busbars |
| **Server rooms / data centres** | Clean agent (Novec 1230 / FM-200) flooding system | 1 m | Automatic detection + fixed system preferred |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical (Common KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 4.5 kg CO₂ extinguisher discharges its full charge in 12 seconds at a nozzle velocity of 14 m/s. Calculate (a) the mass flow rate, and (b) the length of jet (effective throw range) over the discharge period.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> **Part (a) — Mass flow rate:**
>
> $$
> \dot{m} = \frac{m}{t} = \frac{4.5}{12} = 0.375 \text{ kg/s}
> $$
>
> **Part (b) — Jet length:**
>
> $$
> L = v_{jet} \times t = 14 \times 12 = 168 \text{ m}
> $$
>
> This is the **ideal** throw length ignoring drag and gravity. The **practical** range is reduced by ~99 % because the CO₂ jet:
> 1. Rapidly slows due to **air-drag** ($F_d \propto v^2$).
> 2. **Snow particles** fall out of suspension within ~2.5 m.
> 3. **Vapour cloud** disperses, losing extinguishing concentration.
>
> Hence the **effective** range is **1.5–2.5 m**, consistent with the empirical rating stamped on the cylinder.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a CO₂ Fire Extinguisher

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **internal functional architecture** of a typical seamless-steel CO₂ extinguisher used in the KTU Fire Engineering Lab.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Pressure Cylinder Body] --> B[Liquid CO2 Charge 4.5 kg]
    A --> C[Vapour Space 58 bar at 21 C]
    A --> D[Steel Siphon Tube Reaching Bottom]
    D --> E[Main Valve Assembly with Safety Pin]
    E --> F[Discharge Lever and Trigger]
    F --> G[High Pressure Hose Burst 200 bar]
    G --> H[Anti Static Horn Nozzle]
    H --> I[Snow and Vapour Cloud 78 C]
    I --> J[Target Fire Class C]
    K[Pressure Relief Burst Disc 85 bar] --> A
    L[CO2 Vapour Pressure Monitor Gauge] --> A
    M[ISI Mark and IS 15683 Stamping] --> A
    N[Horn Lock Pin and Tamper Seal] --> F
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **siphon tube (D)** reaches the bottom of the cylinder so that **liquid** (not vapour) is discharged first — this maximises the mass delivered and provides the cooling effect.
- The **horn (H)** is the **anti-static** polymer / fibre-reinforced plastic nozzle that lengthens the jet and ensures the cold CO₂ snow reaches the seat of the fire.
- The **burst disc (K)** is a **safety device** that vents the cylinder to atmosphere if it is exposed to fire and the internal pressure exceeds 85 bar (e.g., if a building fire engulfs the cylinder).

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram of a DCP (ABC) Extinguisher

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Welded Mild Steel Cylinder] --> B1[ABC Powder Charge 4 kg]
    A1 --> C1[Propellant Gas N2 or CO2 15 bar]
    A1 --> D1[Internal Dip Tube]
    D1 --> E1[Valve Head with Pressure Gauge]
    E1 --> F1[Carrying Handle and Trigger]
    F1 --> G1[Reinforced Rubber Hose]
    G1 --> H1[Nozzle with Discharge Control]
    H1 --> I1[Powder Cloud Radius 3 to 5 m]
    I1 --> J1[Target Fire Class A B C]
    K1[Anti Corrosion Epoxy Coating] --> A1
    L1[Wall Mounting Bracket] --> A1
    M1[Annual Inspection Tag and IS Mark] --> A1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **propellant gas (C1)** sits above the powder and pressurises the cylinder. When the trigger is pulled, the gas drives the powder up the dip tube.
- ABC powder is **monoammonium phosphate based** (NH₄H₂PO₄), giving a **yellowish** tint. BC-only (sodium bicarbonate) powder is **white/blue**.

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Flowchart — Selection of Extinguisher in Lab

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Fire Detected in Lab] --> Q1{Is the Equipment Energised?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Voltage Class?}
    Q1 -- No de-energised --> A1[Class A extinguisher water or foam]
    Q2 -- Less than 1000 V --> A2[Use CO2 4.5 kg OR DCP 4 kg]
    Q2 -- 1 kV to 11 kV --> A3[Use CO2 9 kg with insulated gloves]
    Q2 -- Above 11 kV --> A4[Evacuate and call professional crew]
    A2 --> P1[Maintain 1 m safe distance]
    A3 --> P2[Maintain 2 m safe distance and lockout if possible]
    A2 --> Pass1[Pull the pin]
    A3 --> Pass1
    Pass1 --> Aim1[Aim at base of fire]
    Aim1 --> Squeeze1[Squeeze the handle]
    Squeeze1 --> Sweep1[Sweep side to side]
    Sweep1 --> Stop1[Watch for reignition for 10 minutes]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Operating a CO₂ Extinguisher (PASS Technique)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant Ex as Extinguisher
    participant Fire as Electrical Fire
    Op->>Ex: Step 1 Pull the safety pin
    Op->>Ex: Step 2 Aim horn at base of fire from 1.5 m
    Op->>Ex: Step 3 Squeeze the lever
    Ex->>Fire: Discharge CO2 snow 78 C
    Fire-->>Fire: Oxygen displaced flame starved
    Op->>Fire: Step 4 Sweep side to side until fire out

```
