## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a Class C fire as per IS 2190. Why is water not used to extinguish such fires?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Class C fire** (per **IS 2190:1992**) is a fire in **energised electrical equipment** such as switchgear, transformers, motors, and live wiring. Water is **not used** because (i) it is a **conductor of electricity** and can create a path for current back to the operator, causing **electric shock or electrocution**, and (ii) water contacting **molten metal** in electrical faults produces **hydrogen and explosive steam**, causing secondary explosions. Approved agents are **CO₂, DCP, and clean agents**, which are **electrically non-conductive** and break the fire chain by smothering or chemical inhibition. (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the two reasons.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three fire extinguishers suitable for Class C fires and state one limitation of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per extinguisher + limitation):**

| Extinguisher | Limitation |
|--------------|------------|
| **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide)** | Limited discharge time (10–15 s); **no cooling after discharge**; risk of reignition. |
| **DCP (Dry Chemical Powder)** | Leaves **heavy corrosive residue**; reduces visibility; not suitable for delicate electronics. |
| **Clean Agent (Novec 1230 / FM-200)** | **Expensive**; limited shelf life of propellant charge; not effective for outdoor windy fires. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [Option 1]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a CO₂ fire extinguisher**. Why is it the **preferred** extinguisher for electrical fires? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **4.5 kg CO₂ extinguisher** is to be used on a **Class C fire** in a 415 V LT panel. Calculate the **(i) effective discharge time** and **(ii) heat absorbed by the CO₂ snow**, given: discharge rate = 0.4 kg/s, latent heat of sublimation = 571 kJ/kg. Comment on the **dielectric safety** of the operator at 1 m distance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Construction (3 marks) — list and explain any 5 components:**
1. **Seamless steel cylinder** — stores liquid CO₂ at 58 bar at 21 °C.
2. **Siphon tube** — reaches the bottom; ensures liquid (not vapour) discharges first.
3. **Valve head with safety pin and pressure relief burst disc (85 bar)** — operator safety.
4. **High-pressure hose** — rated to 200 bar burst pressure.
5. **Anti-static discharge horn** — directs the jet and prevents static build-up on the operator.
6. **Pressure gauge** — indicates cylinder readiness.

**Working (2 marks):**
On pulling the pin and squeezing the lever, the valve opens, liquid CO₂ rises through the siphon tube, expands through the horn, and forms a **snow + vapour cloud at −78.5 °C**. This **displaces oxygen** (falls below the 15 % flame limit) and **absorbs heat** (latent heat of sublimation = 571 kJ/kg), achieving extinguishment by **smothering and cooling**.

**Why preferred for electrical fires (2 marks):**
1. The CO₂ vapour has a **dielectric strength of 30 kV/cm**, far exceeding the 415 V line-to-line voltage of LT panels — **no arcing path** for current.
2. **Zero residue** — no corrosion of contacts, PCBs, or insulation.
3. **Self-pressurising** — no separate propellant, no leakage when not in use.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Effective discharge time:**

$$
t_{eff} = \frac{m_{CO_2}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{4.5}{0.4}
$$

$$
t_{eff} = 11.25 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Heat absorbed:**

$$
Q_{abs} = m_{CO_2} \cdot L_{sub} = 4.5 \times 571
$$

$$
Q_{abs} = 2569.5 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Dielectric safety comment (3 marks):**
At **1 m** from the horn, the operator is well within the **safe dielectric zone** because the CO₂ vapour cloud has a **dielectric breakdown strength of ~30 kV/cm**, so the **effective withstand** at the operator's position is on the order of **hundreds of kV** — orders of magnitude greater than the **415 V** of the panel. However, the operator must:
- Wear **insulating gloves rated Class 0 (IEC 60903)** for added safety.
- **Not** touch the horn or any part of the extinguisher that may be wetted by condensation.
- **Stand upwind** to avoid the cold CO₂ vapour causing frostbite or asphyxiation in confined spaces.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Option 2]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of fire extinguishment by Dry Chemical Powder (ABC)** extinguishers. Why is DCP effective on Class C fires? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **CO₂ and DCP extinguishers** under the following heads: **(i)** extinguishing mechanism, **(ii)** residue, **(iii)** dielectric strength, **(iv)** typical application, **(v)** cost, **(vi)** recharge complexity, **(vii)** IS standard reference. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Mechanism (5 marks):**
1. ABC powder is **monoammonium phosphate (NH₄H₂PO₄)** with **40–60 % ammonium sulphate** and **anti-caking additives (tricalcium phosphate or silicone-coated)**, particle size **10–75 µm**.
2. On discharge, the powder forms a **dense cloud** in the flame zone.
3. The phosphate radical **PO₃⁻** and **HPO₄²⁻** released by the decomposition of NH₄H₂PO₄ **recombine with the free radicals (·OH, ·H, ·O)** that sustain combustion, **terminating the chain reaction** — this is called **negative catalysis** or **chain breaking**.
4. The powder also **radiation-shields** the unburnt fuel and **smothers** the flame by physical blanketing.

**Equation (1 mark):**

$$
NH_4H_2PO_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} H_3PO_4 + NH_3
$$

The **H₃PO₄** and its dehydration products act as **chain carriers** that cap the radicals.

**Why effective on Class C fires (1 mark):**
The powder cloud is **electrically non-conductive** at typical particle densities (resistivity > 10¹² Ω·cm) — hence the **jet does not short the live terminals**. However, the residue is **corrosive to copper and silver-plated contacts** — so it is **less preferred than CO₂** for sensitive electronics.

---

**Part (b) — Comparison Table (1 mark per row, 7 rows = 7 marks)**

| Parameter | CO₂ Extinguisher | DCP (ABC) Extinguisher |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------------|
| **(i) Mechanism** | Smothering + cooling (latent heat of sublimation) | Chemical chain inhibition by phosphate radicals |
| **(ii) Residue** | **None** (clean agent) | Heavy white/yellow powder, **corrosive** |
| **(iii) Dielectric strength** | **~30 kV/cm** (very high) | **~5 kV/cm** (good but lower) |
| **(iv) Typical application** | LT/HT panels, server rooms, CNC machines, labs | Vehicle fires, engine bays, outdoor electrical kiosks |
| **(v) Cost (per kg)** | Higher (~₹250–400/kg charged) | Lower (~₹120–180/kg) |
| **(vi) Recharge complexity** | Re-pressurise with liquid CO₂; needs licensed agency | Refill powder + replace N₂ cartridge; simpler |
| **(vii) IS Standard** | **IS 15683 (Part 4)** | **IS 15683 (Part 2)** |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-losing mistakes in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Class C (IS: India) with Class E (Euro/American BS EN3 / NFPA).** In NFPA 10, electrical fire is no longer a *separate* class — the classification is **Class C (US)** for **energised electrical**, while **BS EN3 (Europe)** uses the symbol marking. Indian IS 2190 calls it **Class C**. **Write both in your answer if you are unsure.**
>
> 2. **Writing "water mist" or "water spray" for live electrical fires.** You will lose **all 3 marks**. Only **fine water mist with a dielectrically tested nozzle** is occasionally permitted on **< 35 kV** by trained professionals — and even then, it is **not** a KTU-acceptable answer at undergraduate level.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the "watch for 10 minutes" step** in the operating procedure. This is a 1-mark question every year — students miss it.
>
> 4. **Confusing CO₂ with dry ice.** CO₂ **snow** is solid CO₂ at −78.5 °C and **sublimes** (solid → gas without becoming liquid) under atmospheric pressure.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning the "Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep" (PASS) acronym** in the operating sequence — easy 1 mark lost in viva.
>
> 6. **Confusing the dielectric strength values.** CO₂ = **30 kV/cm**, Air ≈ **30 kV/cm**, DCP cloud ≈ **5 kV/cm**. Memorising these three values verbatim fetches full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Class C fire = energised electrical equipment** (IS 2190); **never** use water or foam unless **de-energised and locked out**.
- [x] **CO₂ extinguisher** = preferred for Class C: 30 kV/cm dielectric strength, zero residue, 58 bar storage, −78.5 °C discharge.
- [x] **DCP (ABC) extinguisher** = chemical chain inhibition by phosphate radicals; leaves residue; dielectric ~5 kV/cm.
- [x] **Clean agents** (Novec 1230, FM-200) = used where **no residue** is critical (data centres, avionics).
- [x] **Fire tetrahedron** = fuel + O₂ + heat + **chain reaction** — DCP attacks the chain, CO₂ attacks O₂ + heat.
- [x] **Operating steps** = **PASS** — **P**ull pin → **A**im → **S**queeze → **S**weep; then **watch for 10 minutes**.
- [x] **Safe distances**: < 1 kV → 1 m, 1–11 kV → 2 m, 11–33 kV → 3 m, > 33 kV → **evacuate**.
- [x] **Critical oxygen concentration** for extinction ≈ **15 % (v/v)** — CO₂ must push ambient below this.
- [x] **IS Standards** = IS 2190 (classification), IS 15683 (extinguisher performance), IS 14612–14 (maintenance).
- [x] **Effective discharge time formula**: $t_{eff} = m / \dot{m}$ with **utilisation factor 0.50–0.60**.
- [x] **Latent heat of sublimation of CO₂ = 571 kJ/kg** — commit to memory for the 14-mark derivations.
- [x] **Dielectric strengths to memorise**: Air = 30 kV/cm, CO₂ = 30 kV/cm, DCP = 5 kV/cm, SF₆ = ~90 kV/cm (for context).
- [x] **Always state the "anti-static horn"** in the CO₂ diagram — it carries 1 mark by itself in board answers.
- [x] **Annual inspection** is mandatory under IS 14612 — **hydro-test every 5 years**, **discharge test every 12 months** for high-risk zones.
- [x] **Colour code** (IS 15683): CO₂ cylinder body = **red**, with a **black band**; DCP body = **red**, **blue band**; clean agent = **red**, **green band**.
